Notes: 15344542437 I'm pretty sure this the odd pupal husk and adult of a Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle (genus Chilocorus, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Great name! I give thanks to Bernhard Jacobi for putting me on the right track with this one, see this photo and Berhard's comment. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2014)

File.Name: ea13f_ea13-027.jpg
Notes:
15527701871 These are no doubt the smallest galls I've ever noticed, on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, shown top and bottom here. From the neat exit holes, I suspect they are the galls of Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). These galls are really tiny. I didn't get this photo at 1:1 magnification, but I did get another photo. The exit holes are only 110 pixels across, so I figure their size is 110x23.5/6000 = 0.43 mm. Really tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2014)

That formula is a matter of simple proportions, but it only works at 1:1 magnification:
critter size = (pixel_size) x (sensor_width) / (sensor_pixels)

File.Name: ea14a_ea14-081.jpg
Notes:
15515322306 This is a pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on autumn flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. These ladies have four or five eyespots under their hindwings, which distinguishes them from the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) with just two eyespots. See my [Previous] photo for a comparison. It gets confusing when they are both working the same plant! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

I never thought I would post all butterflies today! It was gloomy at home, so I went into the higher mountains to try to find some sun. The fog/cloud layer went up to about 3600 feet, but it was more-or-less clear above that, with some fog blowing over the mountains. There were even a few real raindrops down lower! I hope that's just a teaser for tonight. We have a 20% chance for just a bit of rain after 11 pm tonight. I'll be happy with just a taste...
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Douglas' Groundsel; American Lady
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae; Vanessa virginensis
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae; Vanessa virginensis

File.Name: ea14b_ea14-077.jpg
Notes:
15353245820 I found these two pretty ladies working the same plant in the mountains today, both working autumn flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family.

On the left is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly, with five eyespots on her hindwing. On the right is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly, with just two eyespots on her hindwing. You can see other differences if you compare their upper wings. And then there's the West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly - see this photo! I always get these confused and have to hit my books to figure them out. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)
CommonNames: Douglas' Groundsel; Painted Lady; American Lady; West Coast Lady

File.Name: ea14c_ea14-063.jpg
Notes: 15352796127 This is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly on autumn flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. These ladies almost always have a small white dot in the orange space on the forewing near the margin. They also have just two large eyespots in the submargin of the underside of their hindwngs. See my [Next] photo. These butterflies are quite camera-shy! Every time I got close, they took off. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginensis; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

This one is working an autumn flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. File.Name: ea14e_ea14-042.jpg
Notes: 15538700775 I believe this is a late-season Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting on an old stem. They are less blue than the spring butterflies, but you can still see some blue in there. A moment before, it was working nearby flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. These butterflies are still out there. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

File.Name: ea14f_ea14-045.jpg
Notes: 15352029189 Here is a pretty female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on an autumn flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

File.Name: ea15a_ea15-004.jpg
Notes:
15359732379 Life and death in the garden...

Here are two photos on a cultivar of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa, Asclepiadaceae) milkweed in the garden. Somehow the Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii?) in the order Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera always find the plants, and aphid predators appear. This one sure looks like a caterpillar (Lepidoptera) to me, but I don't really know. In the photo on the left, the predator is approaching a fat aphid. I got the photo on the right about an hour and a half later when I came home from my outing, and it's now munching what I assume is the same aphid. It's interesting that the aphid on the right seems to be squeezing out drops of "something" from those stalk-like cornicles or "siphunculi" on its back. Oh baby, it's a wild world out there! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2014)

Our "storm" last night brought cloudy skies and a bit of drizzle, but no rain. At least it didn't bring wind. Well, it's still early in the season. I'll think positive.
ScientificName: Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Aphis nerii; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Aphis nerii; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Lepidoptera

File.Name: ea15b_ea15-007.jpg
Notes: 15543565461 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on the first fully open flowers I've found this year of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. These are the first new flowers of any plant in this rain year! It's interesting that they flower before the rain comes, but only a few shrubs flower this early. Other shrubs in the same species will wait until winter rains really come. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2014)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; any
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Hymenoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; any

File.Name: ea15c_ea15-002.jpg
Notes: 15543523971 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs on a grass stem in the woods. I have no idea whose eggs they are. They are only about 240 pixels across on this photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, which means they are less than 1 mm across. Really tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2014)

File.Name: ea15d_ea14-086.jpg
Notes: 14925383284 Here are two shots of different Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterflies, with wings closed and open. The common name works for both views, but the open wings are remarkable. The butterfly on the left is on California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family, the one on the right on Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the same family. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

File.Name: ea15e_5718-074.jpg
Notes:
15546713512 This is a mating pair of Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterflies on new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) by the mailbox. It's like a Valentine. (18 July 2005)

This is an old photo from almost 10 years ago! I've been looking through the archives for this one to post to the Insect Hump Day group. It's great fun going through the archives looking for a good photo.

I got this photo with my Nikon D70 - my first DSLR, now comatose with a dead shutter after 100,000+ photos. I've gone through a few more Nikons since then. My D300 also failed with a dead shutter after 165,738 photos. My grandkids helped my D800 to fail last summer. I'm happy with my new D7100 which seems a decent replacement for the D300, though I'm still thinking of a D810. And it all started with my original Nikon FTn way back in the 1960s!
Locality:
18 July 2005)

File.Name: ea15f_ea14-053.jpg
Notes: 15522118536 This is a fasciated stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. The stem is flat like a ribbon and brown. You can see normal green round stems in the background. It looks like this stem did flower, but it seems to have died since then. Many plants get fasciated, some often and others (like this one) rarely. There's some info at Wikipedia. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

File.Name: ea16a_ea16-003.jpg
Notes:
14932899413 This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) with full pollen-baskets snuggling into a flower head of native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis, Asteraceae) on the dry wash of the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 October 2014)

Summer insects are getting scarce as we start to feel some autumn chill in the air. I'll keep trying to get insect photos, but their time is limited. It was cool today, but still warm enough to wear shorts! There were morning clouds at home, so I went to the river again today to escape the gloom. The clouds turned out to be real clouds, not the usual marine layer gloom. One important difference is that we don't get the "fog winds".

File.Name: ea16b_ea14-006.jpg
Notes: 15366369779 This is a pollen-dusted Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) snuggling into a flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plants are still producing beautiful flowers in this drought year! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

File.Name: ea16c_ea16-019.jpg
Notes: 15367270000 I believe this is a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). It's a Band-winged Grasshopper, subfamily Oedipodinae, in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera. It's wings had no particular color as it flew away. This one was perched on the dry river bed of the Santa Ynez River today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 October 2014)

File.Name: ea16d_ea15-008.jpg
Notes: 15367173790 Here is a female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) snuggling into a leaf of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Females don't sing, but the male crickets with broader wings sing for them - see this photo. This is the same shrub that has already started to flower, see this photo. These cool autumn days sure do bring out the colors. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2014)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: ea16e_ea14-047.jpg
Notes: 15366111529 This is a "butt shot" of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly - both its real butt and its faux butt designed to look like a hungry face! I got this photo on native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. HBBBT! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2014)

File.Name: ea16f_ea13-018.jpg
Notes:
15553521212 This is a "butt shot" of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) holding on to its Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly prey. The spider's butt looks like an onion! I found this on native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have another photo of the same encounter here and a better shot of the same spider here. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2014)

I hope this is acceptable for BBBT. Spiders aren't bugs, but they are good enough for BugGuide!

File.Name: ea17a_ea17-044.jpg
Notes:
15374167778 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly on autumn flowers of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae) on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. There are still flowers and butterflies in the woods if you look for them! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

It was cloudy and gloomy in the mountains today, so I went back to the river to find some sun. It looked like storm clouds over the mountains, but there's no rain in the forecast - for a long time.

File.Name: ea17b_ea17-016.jpg
Notes:
14940011283 This is a handsome brown "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. The family name is a reference to the 5-segmented antennae of these critters. There are a zillion different kinds, so I'll leave it at that. This one is sucking on a stem of non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

The mouthparts of these true bugs are interesting. If you count legs left and right, you will find that that leg-like proboscis going into the plant stem is something else!
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salsola tragus; Chenopodiaceae

File.Name: ea17c_ea17-004.jpg
Notes:
15536232136 These are mating Lygaeid Bugs in the family Lygaeidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. They are having their nuptials on flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers are just about done for the year, but there are still a few shrubs going. I'll miss them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

You can see a few more little true bugs in the flowers if you look close.
ScientificName: Lygaeidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Lygaeidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: ea17d_ea17-038.jpg
Notes: 15373750768 I believe this is a little black Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera perched on a flower head of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae) on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I haven't these for a while. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

File.Name: ea17e_ea17-040.jpg
Notes: 14939016314 I found this nearly dead grasshopper on Paradise Road today, no doubt a hit-and-run affair. I moved it off the road so it could die in peace and took a photo. I believe it's another Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis, Oedipodinae, Acrididae, Orthoptera), see this photo from yesterday. I thought twice about posting this photo, but you can see its anatomy so clearly. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

File.Name: ea17f_ea17-027.jpg
Notes:
15559648905 These are ghost trees of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family that were burned out by the 500 acre Rancho Oso Fire back in 2007. The trees look white - like sycamores - but you can see some dark furrowed bark still on the upper branches in the back which identify them as cottonwoods. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

You can learn a lot about the fire history of a place by looking close at the plants. Sometimes it's obvious like this, but other times it's much more subtle. If I ever go back to school - unlikely! - this would be my interest.

File.Name: ea18a_ea18-042.jpg
Notes:
15382531897 "Reach out and touch someone..."

This is the same fat yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) as my [Previous] photo, taken later in the day on the way back from my walk today. It has a comfy nest in the seed fluff, but here it is facing the open flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2014)

I wonder if this is the same spider I found last week on the same flowers - see this photo. It's in the same place as near as I can remember, and I believe these spiders are color-shifters.

It was a lovely day, not too breezy and on the cool side. It does feel like autumn, but too dry. Our clouds have evaporated, and we're heading into our fourth year of drought.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: ea18b_ea18-007.jpg
Notes: 15382324048 This fat yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has made a comfy nest in the seed fluff next to an open flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this photo early in the day. When I came back later, it was facing the flower ready for business! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2014)

File.Name: ea18c_ea18-026.jpg
Notes: 14948173053 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding its head in the base of a leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus, Rhamnaceae). Do you see the remarkable face on its abdomen? If not, try backing up a few feet from your screen. Now why would a spider have a human face on its butt? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2014)

File.Name: ea18d_ea18-009.jpg
Notes: 15382126748 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a new flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe the butterfly is a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides). The flower is interesting, as this is the lowest-elevation plant I know of, growing near the Knapp's Castle trail. They usually occur higher in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2014)

File.Name: ea18e_ea18-035.jpg
Notes: 14947954343 Here is a new flower and seed head of native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a weedy looking plant, but it is native, and the small flowers are remarkable up close. The seed heads remind me of corals in the ocean. The flowers look a lot like smaller versions of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea), see this photo. Both have stocking-like anthers below the pistils. The flowers aren't always as blue as this one, maybe because its newly opened? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2014)
CommonNames: Rod Wirelettuce; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea

File.Name: ea18f_ea18-027.jpg
Notes: 14947300844 This is the distinctive "peeling bark" of a shrub of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family, almost like manzanita or madrone. Some of these plants flower very early in the rain year, see this photo. Other shrubs like this one take their time, though you can see buds on this one if you look close. The early flowering shrubs always flower early, near the equinox, so it must be in their genes. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2014)

File.Name: ea19a_ea19-036.jpg
Notes:
15393083527 This is the nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. It's the same critter I showed in my [Previous] photo. It led me on a chase today, showing a remarkable instinct for avoiding having its photo taken. Those white spots on its body are striking. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

It was another pleasant day, but breezier than its been, especially this morning when I was out getting photos. I don't know how much longer I can keep finding interesting insects like this one. I always get my photos one day at a time, and so far I've been lucky!
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

Notes: 14958247234 This is the spider-like nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek today. This one led me on a chase today, showing a remarkable instinct for avoiding having its photo taken. The wind that was blowing the branch back and forth didn't help! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

File.Name: ea19c_ea19-005.jpg
Notes: 15392702188 Here is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) snuggling into a fallen leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in a plant of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). You can tell by the broad wings that this one is a male cricket, one of our nighttime songsters. I believe these brown ones are Western Tree Crickets (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill. I like the autumn colors of this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Coast Live Oak; Bush Monkey Flower; Western Tree Crickets

File.Name: ea19d_ea19-039.jpg
Notes:
15392520598 This is the nest of a Mud Dauber Wasp (Sceliphron caementarium or kin, Sphecidae, Hymenoptera) that fell from the eaves of our upstairs porch. These impressive wasps hunt for spiders to provision their nests. In this photo, you can see the remains of the larvae at the bottom of the cells, and the remains of the pupae above. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

I'm sorry to say I have no photos of the adult wasps. They are not interested in flowers, so they are hard to capture. But I see them often enough. Maybe that's the way wasps should be - seen, but not too close!

File.Name: ea19e_ea19-006.jpg
Notes:
15575765391 This is another of last spring's bisexual galls of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. You can see a neat exit hole where the critter chewed its way out. I found this one growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. These galls are "monothalamous" which means there's only one critter inside, unlike the summer unisexual galls which are "polythalamous" containing many larvae, see this photo. Yep, nature is complicated that way! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

This one looks like an inverted mushroom. It looks similar to galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii), but that one grows on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) and is a different beast. See this photo.
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Antron douglasii; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Antron douglasii; Quercus berberidifolia

File.Name: ea19f_ea19-018.jpg
Notes:
15578297845 These are galled flowers or berries of native California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The normal berries turned blue-black and were gobbled up by birds months ago. These galled berries are still on the shrubs long after their season. They all have that odd "crook in the neck". (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

There are similar galls on coffeeberry's relative, Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia), see this photo. I've also found this galled flower on our coffeeberry that appears different.

Ron Russo doesn't describe any of these galls in his "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006), but he does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. Attempts to rear these gall inducers for identification have been unsuccessful." This might be the one.
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus ilicifolia
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Frangula californica; Rhamnaceae; Rhamnus ilicifolia

File.Name: ea20a_ea20-005.jpg
Notes:
15402034388 This is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) catching some morning rays on an old stump by the edge of our garden. It's really enjoying the moment, with eyes closed in bliss. Lizard heaven! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2014)

I've only been seeing the small lizards since cooler autumn weather kicked in. I reckon it takes longer for the BIG critters to warm up than the small ones. It's simple geometry. A lizard that's twice as large in length has four times the surface area to collect sunlight, but eight times the bulk to warm up. (Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded lizards don't get too big.

It was another lovely morning, but the wind did kick up in the afternoon. Even calm winds are a problem for close photos. I had to take *many* photos of the tiny Green Lynx Spider a few photos back to get a keeper as the plant swayed back and forth in the tiniest breeze.
Locality:
Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded lizards don't get too big.

It was another lovely morning

File.Name: ea20b_ea20-037.jpg
Notes: 14967303174 Here is a worn but still pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", Lamiaceae) in the garden. I think it just noticed that I've been moving in closer and closer with my tripod - she's looking right at me! A moment later it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2014)

File.Name: ea20c_ea20-026.jpg
Notes:
15587892685 This is a tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae - scarcely more than a spiderling. It's in a seed head of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family, see my [Previous] photo. This is a tight crop from my original photo taken at 1:1 magnification. The spider is only 560 pixels across, so its size is only about 2.2 mm, really tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2014)

See this photo to see how I measured the spider. I don't know what prey it expects to find on a seed head, but good hunting little spider.

File.Name: ea20d_ea19-012.jpg
Notes: 15401677648 This is a seed head of humble Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plant grows along roads and disturbed places, but it is considered to be a native plant. See this photo in the upper-left. The seed heads are interesting because the seem to show two different kinds of seeds - furry seeds with pappus hairs for the disk flowers in the center, and glabrous seeds with no pappus hairs for the ray flowers around the edge. In a normal year they flower until the new year, but in this drought year they are already done. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

File.Name: ea20e_ea20-033.jpg
Notes:
14966858314 Here are aphids lined up like cattle at a feeding trough - in this case a cultivar of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa, Asclepiadaceae) milkweed in the garden. I believe the aphids are Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii?) in the order Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. The aphids do some damage, but they are pretty and attract interesting predators - see this recent photo. I wonder how the aphids always find the plants, year after year? (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2014)

I think there should be a group for photos of aphids and their friends and foe. I may have to start it myself.

File.Name: ea20f_ea20-029.jpg
Notes: 15401422598 I believe these are galls of the Willow Tube Gall Midge fly in the genus Iteomyia of the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The galls have little openings at the apex. They occur on the undersides of the leaves, but you can see the top of a gall in the upper-left of my photo. The willow is an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis in the Salicaceae plant family) growing by the dry creek in our canyon. They don't seem to do the leaves any good. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2014)

File.Name: ea21a_ea21-003.jpg
Notes:
15409888049 Here is the peeling bark of a large native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. I think I like the part in the shadows the best. What else can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

No interesting critters today, but I show what I get each day. It was windy this morning and this afternoon, but there was a nice period of calm in between.

File.Name: ea21b_ea21-027.jpg
Notes: 15596408675 This is Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a sandstone outcrop, showing some drought stress in this very dry year. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this. The drought seems to be taking its toll on this plant, but it still has some green. It looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places like this. I've heard they are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

File.Name: ea21c_ea21-018.jpg
Notes:
14976180503 I believe this is the gall of Ruptured Twig Gall Wasps (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on an interesting oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains. Apparently the wasp larvae form capsules that somehow rise to the surface and fall to the ground to mature, leaving open scars behind. It sure appear like some critter has wedged a seed into the opening! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

The oak is more difficult and more interesting. It mostly grows as a "scrub oak", but occasionally it becomes a copse of small trees. It's been called many names - Quercus pacifica or Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus parvula or Quercus parvula var. shrevei or Quercus shrevei. See the discussion with this photo. I think I prefer Quercus pacifica as a name, though that one usually grows on the Channel Islands as the Island Scrub Oak.

The oak looks like a Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath; without "hairy armpits"; the catkins are longer, more airy, and pinker; and the plants appear to be semi-deciduous - dropping their old leaves and getting new ones all at once. Whatever oak it is, it's a black oak with interesting galls that are also found on Coast Live Oak - see this photo.
CommonNames: Ruptured Twig Gall Wasps; oak; Island Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak; black oak; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ea21d_ea21-008.jpg
Notes: 15593474051 These are "leaf roller galls" (my name) on leaves of an interesting oak (Quercus, Fagaceae) in our mountains, maybe Quercus pacifica. (See my [Next] photo.) I also find these galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), see this photo and this photo. Both oaks are "black oaks" so I'm not surprised to find the same galls on them. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

File.Name: ea21e_ea21-010.jpg
Notes: 14975314784 I believe this is native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) - growing in the most un-sedge-like place you can imagine, a crack in a sheet of bone-dry sandstone rock. But life is tough, and this plant still has some green. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

File.Name: ea21f_ea21-011.jpg
Notes: 15409895478 This large Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family is losing its battle against the drought. It's only got two branches showing some life, and those are showing stress. This is something we'll see more and more of as we enter our fourth year of extreme drought. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

File.Name: ea22a_ea22-019.jpg
Notes:
14984800453 This is a mighty female Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working some of the last flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are our largest wasps - almost 2 inches long. This one has orange antennae curled to the outside, but see my [Previous] photo. The color of these huge wasps is amazing - "so blue it's black" with those contrasting orange wing, though this one has definite violet colors on its head. I haven't seen these for a while, so maybe this one is a different species. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 October 2014)

It was another lovely day with mostly benign winds. Our autumn weather has swung back to the warm side, up to 87∞ at the river today, where I was.

File.Name: ea22c_ea22-043.jpg
Notes: 15605477232 This Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera has a bad wing, but it can still fly. I can appreciate the situation because I have a bad leg and I still can walk! Its infirmity gives a nice inside look at its wings. Maybe it's another it's another Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis, Oedipodinae), but I doubt it with those dark colors and that blue on the wings. I guess it is another Trimerotropis. I found this one on a rock by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 October 2014)

File.Name: ea22d_ea22-004.jpg
Notes: 15601866721 This is a different Yellow Jacket-type wasp (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) on a narrow leaf of a Cattail (family Typhaceae) in our little water garden in an old bathtub in the garden. It's hairier and has different color patterns than our usual yellow jackets in this photo. These are the wasps that bug us on our upstairs porch, trying to get a bite of meat from our dinner. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2014)

File.Name: ea22e_c116-011.jpg
Notes:
15418387607 (Update 28oct14 - This comment is what I thought when I posted this photo, but read Alice's comment below. I guess it's time to rethink it.)

Ah, sex and death. These are mating Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) - or is the one feeding? Both spiders seem to have swollen pedipalps, a sign of mating. The larger female sure seems to be feeding on the shrunken male. I guess the myth that female spiders feed on the males after mating is true, at least in this case. The scene is a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree near the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2012)

I can't say I understand this photo. As I understand spider sex, the males deposit tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then pick it up with the specialized leg-like pedipalps on their face to deliver sperm to their lady to make babies. This photo doesn't seem to fit that pattern, but it sure looks intimate!

This is an old photo from before I started posting to Flickr. I've been looking through the archives for this one to post to the Insect Hump Day group. It's great fun going through the archives looking for a good photo.

File.Name: ea22f_ea22-005.jpg
Notes: 15417667449 This is a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family - loaded with berries! Madrone berries have been scarce in this drought year, so I was surprised to find this one growing right along the north-facing side of Highway 154. I got this photo from an easy pull-off on the other side of the highway, but the highway is curbed on the tree's side so there's no easy access for a closer shot. I'll figure something out. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2014)

File.Name: ea23a_ea23-032.jpg
Notes:
14991591164 We had a partial solar eclipse this afternoon. Here is my photo of the very cool "eclipse shadows" through the oak leaves on our upstairs porch. That's Julie's hand holding a piece of paper. The gaps between the leaves act like little pinhole cameras and make images of the sun. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2014)

Apart from the eclipse, it was an uneventful day. It was another warm day with mostly benign winds, a bit warmer than yesterday. Tomorrow might be another warm day, then it's expected to swing back to the cool side.

File.Name: ea23b_ea23-030.jpg
Notes:
14991537354 Here are small Wild Bees (Anthophila, Hymenoptera) on new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The bees are digging the new flowers! Maybe they are family Andrenidae but I really don't know. I was focused on the two bees on the left. The one in the air was a bonus! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2014)

These are the first new flowers of any plant in this rain year. It's interesting that they flower before the rain comes, but only a few shrubs flower this early. Other shrubs in the same species will wait until winter rains really come.

File.Name: ea23c_ea23-019.jpg
Notes:
15425498109 This Grasshopper (Acrididae, Orthoptera) looks like a voyeur - gazing right at a mating pair of small "true bugs" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera). One of those bugs seems to be looking right back! I found this encounter on a flower head of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae). I'm not sure, but the grasshopper may be a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes). (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2014)

I'm really confused by these Isocoma plants. I think of them as coastal plants, but they also grow in the backcountry along Paradise Road, and here they are in the mountains. I always find them along roads, and I wonder if they are all introduced by road equipment.

File.Name: ea23d_ea22-003.jpg
Notes: 14991832923 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis, Vespidae, Hymenoptera). It's resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) trying to warm up in the morning. It's showing its abdomen nicely. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2014)

14991157354 This is a little nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. It's saying goodbye to me, always the intruder in these quiet insect lives. It is a nice butt shot, HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2014)

I have more photos of the same critter here and here.

File.Name: ea23f_ea17-035.jpg
Notes:
15611838315 I believe this is a little black Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera perched on a flower head of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae) on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. This is not only a "Beautiful Bug Butt" photo, it's taking a dump! The frass is yellow, like the flowers. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2014)

I have another photo of the same critter here.

File.Name: ea24a_ea24-013.jpg
Notes:
15596054546 This is a handsome Lygaeid Bug in the family Lygaeidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - on the flower and then taking wing a moment later! It might be a Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei), compare photos at BugGuide. It's on a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2014)

It was another beautiful day in paradise - a bit cooler and breezier than its been, but it was still 88∞ at the river where I was today. Tomorrow is expected to be much cooler and maybe breezier. I can't complain about the winds this autumn, it's been quite calm.

File.Name: ea24b_ea24-003.jpg
Notes: 15620523592 This is a pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly showing why it has it's name - light gray on the underwings and dark gray - almost "blue dun" - on the upper wings. This one is perched on still-new flower buds of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the premier late-season flower for local insects. I'll be sorry to see it go. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2014)

File.Name: ea24c_ea24-017.jpg
Notes: 15434014210 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae with its prey, maybe a fly? I found it today on a still-flowering native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Spiders of all kinds have been scarce in this drought year so I was happy to find this one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2014)

File.Name: ea24d_ea21-021.jpg
Notes: 15595833876 This is a very bristly Fly (Diptera) perched on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I guess that it's a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), though I don't know for sure. I saw several of these on this day in the woods. Interesting flies have been scarce, but this one will do. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2014)

File.Name: ea24e_ea24-035.jpg
Notes:
15616729731 *Sigh* the death of a giant...

This grand old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree has finally given in to the drought. It's growing along Paradise Road, just beyond the second crossing in a place that has been especially hard hit by the drought. The dead Mistletoe (Phoradendron leucarpum ssp. tomentosum, Viscaceae) that grows as a parasite is often the first sign of trouble. I have a photo of the same tree still with some green leaves last year, compare this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2014)

I got a number of photos of dead or dying oaks by the river today. I'll post more tomorrow if the winds kick up.

File.Name: ea24f_ea24-059.jpg
Notes:
15616679221 The last crossing of Paradise...

This is all that's left of the swimming hole at the last river crossing of Paradise Road, just before the parking area and the walk to Red Rock. There is still a bit of water, Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii, Salicaceae) trees starting to show some fall color, and dark Camuesa Peak in the background. I took almost the same photo last year, see this photo. The water level looks about the same, though it feels drier. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2014)

File.Name: ea25a_ea25-037.jpg
Notes:
15625436751 This is a remarkable "parasitic wasp" on the surface of the large gall on the right in my [Previous] photo. (You can see it there!) It might be a Chalcidid Wasp in the family Chalcididae, but I'm not sure. You have to admire that long ovipositor designed to dig into the cynipid larvae hidden inside the gall! That's the gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The gall wasp are parasites of the oak tree, so I reckon this one is a hyperparasite - a parasite whose host is a parasite. I'm proud of this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

The wasp is quite tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and the wasp is only about 600 pixels across, not counting its ovipositor. That makes it about 2.4 mm long. (See this photo to see how I measured it.)

Looking at this photo again in the morning, I see something odd. It seems to have two separate ovipositors - the one at its rear end, and another one in the center at the base of its abdomen, which is actually entering the gall. You can count six legs, so that other thing must be something else. I admit that I don't understand this at all.

I was expecting wind today, but it was pleasant and calm. So much for our little storm trough.
Locality:
See this photo to see how I measured it.)

Looking at this photo again in the morning

File.Name: ea25b_ea25-040.jpg
Notes:
15628837822 This is a large mature gall of Live Oak Apple Gall Wasps (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), with a couple of smaller galls on the left. If you look close at the large gall on the right, just to the right of center, you can see the tiny wasp in my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

File.Name: ea25c_ea25-047.jpg
Notes:
15007644463 Here are ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending interesting galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls produce "oak honey" which attracts the ants, who in turn keep the predators away. You can see some honey dripping from the gall in the upper-right. This is a great example of different critters cooperating for a common good. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

The ants sure look like California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae. These are the ants that devil us in the spring, see this photo.

These are the first active galls of this species that I've found this year. They seem late, but better late than never. I had to use *gulp* flash for this photo to bring out some detail that was otherwise lost in the shadows in this contrasty light. I don't like using flash, but sometimes there's no choice.
Locality: Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae. These are the ants that devil us in the spring

File.Name: ea25d_ea25-002.jpg
Notes: 15441683847 This is a new flower of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family in the canyon. It does the soul some good to find these flowers at the very end of our season. They have such wonderful large pollen grains that explode from the stamens like popcorn. They that get everywhere, including the pistil. I don't know how these beautiful flowers avoid self-pollination. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

File.Name: ea25e_ea25-009.jpg
Notes: 15441453018 These are seed heads of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The seeds look like any other Epilobium, but this is a dry-land plant that grows in places without water. See this photo of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

File.Name: ea25f_ea25-011.jpg
Notes: 15440877479 This is a web-shrouded native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family growing in our canyon. It looks quite dead, but if you look closer you can see tiny green buds inside the dead leaves. There's still life in the plant! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

File.Name: ea26a_ea26-004.jpg
Notes:
15451593417 This is a small "true bug" (Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on an old flower head of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. A moment earlier, it was sucking the stem. It has wonderful liquid-looking eyes - all of them! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2014)

I was expecting wind today, but it was pleasant and calm again. We're under a Wind Advisory tonight, but it's not happening. No wind, no clouds, no rain, no weather. Bah!

File.Name: ea26b_ea26-026.jpg
Notes: 15637442575 I found this bristly Fly (Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today. I think I've seen the same kind of fly before, see this photo. I guess that it's a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), both for the bristles and the fact that I find them in the woods, away from humans. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2014)

File.Name: ea26c_ea25-024.jpg
Notes: 15016615414 I believe this is a tough-looking Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae, order Diptera perched for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's just a fly, but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are! This one is looking right at me. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2014)

File.Name: ea26d_ea26-007.jpg
Notes: 15450612039 Here are this year's seedpods of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plant in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. The plants die after flowering, but they usually leave a clutch of new plants at the base. They seem to have had a surprising good year in this time of drought. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2014)

File.Name: ea26e_ea26-039.jpg
Notes: 15451568780 This is a close shot of densely-packed ripe sporangia of native Narrowleaf Sword Fern (Polystichum imbricans, Dryopteridaceae) growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak at 4,000 feet, one of the highest points of our coastal mountains. I think I've found the same fern growing on exposed rock in Oregon. This photo may be a little too creative - you can't see the plant! I'll post another photo tomorrow. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2014)

File.Name: ea26f_ea26-022.jpg
Notes: 15451405420 Here are new flower buds of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Usually these are the second native to flower, after Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). The shrubs usually wait until we get a bit of winter rain, but these buds look almost ready to open. The plants on the pass don't look so good, and I'm not sure if they'll flower at all in this drought year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2014)

File.Name: ea27a_ea27-007.jpg
Notes:
15646613455 These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that I found dancing above the leaves of of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like little ocean creatures or dancing centipedes! I'm sure the "double-tracks" is the sun reflecting from each of the flies' wings on every beat. My exposure was 1/40 second, and I count (mostly) 6 beats per exposure. So they beat their wings about 240 times per second. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so. I assume this is a mating thing. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 October 2014)

In some years, we have massive mating flights of similar flies raising like clouds above the oak trees. See this photo from 2013, and this old photo (and the two previous photos) on Fotolog from Christmas Day, 2009.

I had to visit the Santa Ynez Valley today, and as always I made a stop on the way for a few photos. It was another lovely autumn day, temps about 70∞ and little wind. *Sigh* When will it end?
Locality: and the two previous photos) on Fotolog from Christmas Day

File.Name: ea27e_ea27-017.jpg
Notes:
15622480496 Here is Bradbury Dam at Lake Cachuma in this drought year. The reservoir is down to 29% of capacity, and the word is that there's only water for one more year. The hose you see is (I believe) to pump water to the holding Southern California Steelhead trout in Hilton Creek at the base of the dam. As you can guess, that's a matter of some controversy! We're on our own well in the mountains which is doing fine so far, though we've just about given up on our garden. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 October 2014)

I have an old photo of the flood gates when the lake was full back in 2005 here at Fotolog.

File.Name: ea27f_ea26-037.jpg

File.Name: ea28f_ea28-007.jpg
Notes: 15654871705 This is a late-flowering male (staminate) branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I think the female (pistillate) plants of this dioecious shrub are already finished. The male shrub is mostly "firing blanks" anyway. Many of the flowers are "cleistogamous" - flowering without opening in these dry scarious heads, something they do in a very drought year like this. The lady plants seem to get along fine without them. The rest of my photos today are on this same shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2014)

File.Name: ea29a_ea29-018.jpg
Notes:
15663038665 This is a different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Maybe it's the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus? If you look close, you can see a couple of little scavenger flies on the bug, maybe Freeloader Flies (family Milichiidae, Diptera)? If you look even closer at the tip of the bug's proboscis, you can see that it has a tiny aphid-sized prey. That's a tiny prey for sch a large predator! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2014)

It was downright warm today, up to 91∞ at the river today where I was. Tomorrow is expected to be cooler, and there's actually a 60% chance on rain on Friday night. Bring it on!

File.Name: ea29c_5717-062.jpg
Notes:
15662858535 Spider love! I believe these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae) caught in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transfering genetic material from the male's palps to the female's epigyne. It's a tender moment... and complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2005)

Mating is difficult for these preditors who are ready to attack anything that moves. I guess it's always difficult. The smaller male spider is on the left showing its fangs, and the larger female is behind. Both are hanging upside down. As I understand it, the male deposits tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then inflates his spermaphores on the end of his leg-like palps to pick it up. You can see the dark inflated spermaphores on the end of the leg-like palps in this photo. The female's epigyne opening is hidden behind them, but its ready to accept the sperm package. See this old follow-up photo at Fotolog that shows the conclusion of the encounter.

I've been looking through the archives for this one to post to the Insect Hump Day group. It's great fun going through the archives looking for a good photo. I think this one really shows spider sex. My photo last week was probably something else.
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; palps; epigyne; spermaphores; palps; epigyne; really

File.Name: ea29f_ea29-010.jpg
Notes: 15041838044 This is a web of Dodder - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Convulvulaceae or Cuscutaceae plant family. It's having its way with (I think) Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata), also in the Convulvulaceae. Dodder is a parasitic plant without chlorophyll that gets nutrients from another green plant like this. It doesn't seem choosy about partners. Usually by now the stems have lost their color, so I was surprised to find this bright orange plant today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2014)

File.Name: ea30a_ea30-007.jpg
Notes:
15050208194 These are emerging winged ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera), probably genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. This photo shows different ants from the same nest. These ants all have an interesting "spade shaped" articulated abdomen. There are the large alate queens, the smaller worker ants, and that odd one with the striped abdomen below the large ant on the right. I'm most interested in that odd winged ant in the lower-left that sure looks like something completely different, see my [Previous] photo. This emergence only lasted for ten minutes or so. I was in the right place at the right time to get a few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2014)

It was an interesting weather day, with clouds and wind moving in. We have an 80% chance of real rain tomorrow and tomorrow night! Hurray, this will be our first rain of the new rain year!

I didn't see it get made, but I found it on top of of the retaining wall of the garden today, a sunny place where lizards hang out in the morning. File.Name: ea31a_ba09-047.jpg
Notes:
15492423097 So that's why they call them Cat-Faced Spiders!

This is an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae with a Yellow Jacket-type Wasp (family Vespidae in the order Hymenoptera) prey. I'm pretty sure that the spider is a "Cat-Faced Spider" (Araneus gemmoides). You can see the cat face on the lower part of the spider's abdomen, between the front legs of the spider! I've always wondered about the name, and now I see it! It has a Yellow Jacket-type wasp (Vespula, Vespidae, Hymenoptera) for prey. Happy Halloween! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

This is my scary spider photo for Halloween. But what's really scary is that I haven't seen any of these large orb-weavers in the woods and had to dip into the archives. We've had them under the eaves of our porch for decades, but in this drought year there are none.

Our first rain storm of the new rain year has been moving in... and moving in... There's still no rain, but I have to believe it's coming. Please!

We're heading off to our cabin near Lone Pine in the Owens Valley tomorrow morning. We have a share in a cabin out there, and we need a work day or two. This won't be a photo trip, but I'll try to get a few. I'll be back in time for the election on Tuesday.

File.Name: ea31d_ea31-024.jpg
Notes:
15057871893 Here are two different "integral stem galls" on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). On the left is a the gall of a Gouty Stem Gall Wasp (Callirhtis quercussuttoni) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera riddled with the exit holes of the tiny gall wasps. On the right is a gall of a gall of Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the same family. Both are non-detachable, which means you can't just break them off like many galls. The difference is the exit-holes. The wasps on the left chew their way out and make neat holes. The galls are usually more knobby and very dense wood. With gall on the right, the gall capsules somehow rise to the surface and burst through - kind of like a boil. I got that photo yesterday, along West Camino Cielo road. The texture of the galls is subtly different. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2014)

It was a crummy day for new photos - gloomy, dark, and just breezy enough to keep everything in motion. I took my walk anyway, and got this photo.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Gouty Stem Gall Wasp; Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp

File.Name: eb04a_eb03-031.jpg
Notes:
15094069484 This is the newly restored Owens River flowing past cattails with trees and the southern end of the Inyo Mountains in the background. That must be a Cottonwood (Populus, Salicaceae) tree with brilliant fall colors in the center. Yep, the river is really flowing, thanks to the Lower Owens River Project (LORP) which has finally brought some water back to the lower Owens River. This has been the result of much litigation against the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power (LADWP). Think of the movie Chinatown, but thank you LA. (Owens River, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 3 November 2014)

I really want to kayak this river, but it would be a challenge. The cattails are thick, and I don't know that there's always a clear path, and there are reports of fences across the river. If you've done it, please leave a comment!

We used to come to our cabin near Lone Pine a lot, but grandkids in Portland have changed our course, and I haven't been here for about five years. I was expecting this to be a work trip, but the person we were to meet on Saturday had a health issue and we couldn't meet with the other person until late yesterday, so we had some time to explore.

Oh yes, we did have a good rain on Halloween night - 1.29 inches! It's not a bad start for our rain year, though we're still only at 73% of normal.

File.Name: eb04b_eb02-012.jpg
Notes:
15529108510 This is looking past the trees along Lone Pine Creek towards the snow-covered Inyo Mountains on Sunday morning. For some reason, the Inyos got a heavy snowfall on Saturday night, after our main storm passed on Friday night. I believe this is Mount Inyo on the left and Keynot Peak on the right. I got this photo from near our cabin on Lone Pine Creek. Damn, it was cold that night! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

The High Sierras are behind me, but I'm always drawn more towards these desert mountains across the valley.

Notes: 15713843755 This is looking past the fantastic rocks of the Alabama Hills towards the snow-covered Inyo Mountains. I got this photo from near our cabin on Lone Pine Creek. Damn, it was cold that night! Despite the cold, I found a few flowering plants around the base of these rocks. Photos tomorrow. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb04d_eb03-015.jpg
Notes: 15528461177 Here is Julie in a meadow of native Salt Grass (Distichlis spicata, Poacea) along the Owens River. You can see Willow trees (Salix,Salicaceae) in the background, and Common Rabbit Brush (Ericameria nauseosa or Chrysothamnus nauseosus, Asteraceae) that's gone to seed in the foreground. This grassland is part of the Lower Owens River Project (LORP) which has finally brought some water back to the lower Owens River. (Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 3 November 2014)

File.Name: eb04e_eb03-005.jpg
Notes: 15528327837 These are Willow trees (Salix,Salicaceae) growing in a meadow of native Salt Grass (Distichlis spicata, Poacea) with the High Sierras behind. You can see Mount Whitney on the right of the skyline. This grassland is part of the Lower Owens River Project (LORP) which has finally brought some water back to the lower Owens River. (Owens River, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 3 November 2014)

File.Name: eb04f_eb02-022.jpg
Notes: 15527535559 I can't go to our cabin in the Alabama Hills in Owens Valley without showing at least one photo of Mount Whitney, the highest summit in the contiguous United States with an elevation of 14,505 feet (4,421 m). Here it is, with a few clouds clearing out after our storm on Friday night. Many years ago, I camped on the summit of that mountain and watched the sun rise over the desert. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb05a_eb05-010.jpg
Notes:
15722910212 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a slender Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a flower head of pistillate (female) native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I haven't seen these critters for a while, my last photo was in early July. They look like skinny Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae), but they are plant feeders. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2014)

The rest of my photos today are flowers (!) in the Alabama Hills near Lone Pine in the high desert. It was a beautiful day today at home, with temps in the 80s. *Sigh* That will suck the moisture from the ground after our first rain on Friday night.
Locality: !) in the Alabama Hills near Lone Pine in the high desert.  It was a beautiful day today at home
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; Coyote Bush; Assassin Bugs
ScientificName: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Reduviidae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Reduviidae

File.Name: eb05b_eb02-061.jpg
Notes: 15101322684 Here are pretty little flowers of Desert Calico (Loeseliastrum matthewsii) in the Polemoniaceae plant family growing in the high desert. It's a "belly plant" for sure, at least at this time of year. The wonder is that it's flowering now, in November, at the end of our drought summer. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb05c_eb02-063.jpg
Notes: 15536360420 This is a small native plant of *some kind* of Pincushion Flower (Chaenactis, Asteraceae) growing on the coarse desert soil of the Alabama Hills. Maybe it's the Desert Pincushion (Chaenactis fremontii)? It's a "belly plant" for sure, at least at this time of year. The wonder is that it's flowering now, in November, at the end of our drought summer. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb05d_eb02-076.jpg
Notes: 15535906797 This is a small native plant of *some kind* of Buckwheat (Eriogonum, Polygonaceae) growing on the coarse desert soil of the Alabama Hills. It's a "belly plant" for sure! There are a zillion different kinds of buckwheat, and I'm happy to leave it at that. The wonder is that it's flowering now, in November, at the end of our drought summer. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb05e_eb03-050.jpg
Notes: 15697307136 This is an old plant of Saucer Plant (Oxytheca perfoliata, Polygonaceae) in the coarse desert soil of the Alabama Hills. Some plants don't need flowers to be beautiful. The leaves look a bit like Miner's Lettuce, but they are iterated! (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb05f_eb02-070.jpg
Notes: 15535020649 This is a still-flowering plant of Desert Tobacco (Nicotiana obtusifolia) in the Solanaceae plant family. The leaves have a real tobacco smell and contain nicotine. I found this one at the base of the rocks in this photo. This native plant was a ritual plant for Native Americans. I always feel the vibes from ancient times when I find this plant flowering in the desert. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb06a_eb06-009.jpg
Notes:
15109212633 This is the perfectly named "Dead Man's Foot" (Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" coming up in the garden next to the trunk of a large native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". You can see some bluish colors. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. Here are two photos, the whole fungus on the left, and a closer shot of the peridioles which break down to release the spores. The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" which is a good description. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2014)

I had an eye exam this morning - no problems, I just need new glasses as my old ones are falling apart. I got eye-drops to dilate my pupils and I had to wear shades. It didn't seem a good day to look for photos, but I found this one and the last one at home. My photos today are mushrooms, beautiful bug butts, and interesting galls - not bad!
ScientificName: Pisolithus tinctorius; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; tinctorus; peridioles
associatedTaxa: Pisolithus tinctorius; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; tinctorus; peridioles

File.Name: eb06b_eb06-005.jpg
Notes:
15704791426 This is the first mushroom that I've found in this rain season, which began last Friday night. It's not beautiful or elegant, but it's a real mushroom! I'm sure it's a Bolete (Boletus) mushroom with pores instead of gills. This one was growing in the garden about 20 feet from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I won't try to guess what kind of bolete it is. This one is not in the best condition, and we have a number of different small boletes in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2014)

Mushrooms have been scarce in the woods for the last three drought years. I have to re-learn them. I hope this is the first of many to come!

File.Name: eb06c_eb05-011.jpg
Notes: 15729921222 This is a walking-away "butt shot" of a Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a flower head of pistillate (female) native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. These little bugs are impossibly slender. See this photo on the same critter. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2014)

File.Name: eb06d_eb02-048.jpg
Notes:
15726257741 Here are lots of old galls - with exit holes - on a desert shrub of Great Basin Sagebrush aka Big Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) in the Asteraceae plant family. I thought it would be easy to find out whose galls these are, but sagebrush collects many different galls. Maybe these are old galls of the Sponge Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia pomum, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) which are said to be the most common midge galls in the eastern sierra. They don't look very spongy. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

I'm better at finding galls than identifying them!
CommonNames: Great Basin Sagebrush; Big Sagebrush; Sponge Gall Midge
ScientificName: Artemisia tridentata; Asteraceae; Rhopalomyia pomum; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Artemisia tridentata; Asteraceae; Rhopalomyia pomum; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: eb06e_eb03-035.jpg
Notes:
15108707883 Here are old and new galls of the Willow Rosette Gall Midge (Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) on a branch of some kind of Willow (genus Salix, Salicaceae) along Lone Pine Creek. I'm not sure what kind of willow it is, and I won't try to guess. There's also a yellow rust fungus on some of the leaves, but it was too windy to get in for a closer shot. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 3 November 2014)

All willows seem to get these galls, though they look quite different on different kinds of willows. Are they really all caused by the same gall midge?

File.Name: eb06f_eb02-039.jpg
Notes: 15543091090 This is a newly galled stem of (I believe) a male staminate plant of Fourwing Saltbush (Atriplex canescens) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. I'm not sure, but it might be the gall of a Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera willistoni, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) - at least it's probably the work of some kind of gall midge. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb07a_eb07-003.jpg
Notes:
15116404993 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I believe this is a fairly large Bordered Plant Bug (family Largidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - maybe species Largus californicus. It's working a still-flowering native shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. These flowers are getting scarce, but there still are a few. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 November 2014)

We've had a couple of beautiful days, with calm winds and temps near 80∞ at home and near 90∞ at the river, where I was today. Give it time and it will swing back to cold. That's how it is here in autumn.

File.Name: eb07b_eb05-019.jpg
Notes: 15115731164 Look close! Here are a pair of well-camouflaged Tree Crickets (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that are snagged in a shrub of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). That's a female with narrow wings on the left and a male with broader wings for singing on the right. I believe these brown ones are Western Tree Crickets (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill. I believe it's unusual to capture both genders in one shot. I like the autumn colors in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2014)
CommonNames: Tree Crickets; Coast Live Oak; Bush Monkey Flower; Western Tree Crickets

File.Name: eb07c_eb05-002.jpg
Notes: 15116169603 This is a newly emerged winged (alate) Termite in the order Isoptera. It's struggling with its wings stuck to a blue plastic chair in the garden. The chair was quite dry - I wonder if it's static cling? It's reassuring that they are not coming from the foundation of our house! I tried to find where they are coming from, but no luck. Maybe this is a Subterranean Termite (family Rhinotermitidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2014)

File.Name: eb07d_eb02-141.jpg
Notes:
15549950078 These are tall stalks of native Carrizo Grass aka Common Reed (Phragmites australis, Poacea) growing by a seep in the Alabama Hills, with the high sierras in the background. That's Mount Whitney on the left. I got this photo just before we lost the sun. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

Look for these plants to find damp places in the desert. I found a number of other interesting plants here, including Clematis, Yerba Mansa, and a Mojave Thistle. But I lost the light. We meant to go back to this place, but we didn't make it on this trip. We have them at home too, in the dry backcountry like Little Caliente Hot Springs.

File.Name: eb07e_eb03-052.jpg
Notes:
15736833832 These are plants of native Indian Ricegrass (Stipa hymenoides aka Achnatherum hymenoides or Oryzopsis hymenoides, Poacea) growing in the desert of the Alabama Hills. Wikipedia reports that it was a staple food of Native Americans, and it's the Nevada state grass. It's a perennial, and you can see a few green leaves if you look close. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 3 November 2014)

This is a plant with many names. With recent changes in botanical nomenclature, it was easier to find on the web by searching for its common name. That's not the way it's supposed to be.
ScientificName: Stipa hymenoides; Achnatherum hymenoides; Oryzopsis hymenoides; Poacea
associatedTaxa: Stipa hymenoides; Achnatherum hymenoides; Oryzopsis hymenoides; Poacea

File.Name: eb07f_eb02-096.jpg
Notes: 15115269294 I believe this is a large clump of perennial Desert Needle Grass (Stipa speciosa aka Achnatherum speciosum, Poacea) growing in an outcrop of the Alabama Hills. You can see some green in there, remarkable for a desert plant at the end of three drought years in a row. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb08a_eb08-035.jpg
Notes:
15720281856 Here are two shots of an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working the last flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are less blue than the spring butterflies, but you can still see some blue in there. These are different flowers on the same shrub, but I believe its the same butterfly because of that torn wing on the butterfly-right. I'm really just posting this to prove that there are still butterflies out there! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2014)

It was another unseasonably warm day, but the weather is expected to cool down next week. Our good rain a week ago is starting to evaporate away.

These fantastic late-season flowers are just about done, but there still are a few. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2014)

File.Name: eb08c_eb08-010.jpg
Notes: 15741908031 This is a pollen-dusted Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) working non-native flowers of Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae). I guess these large black bees can't be too choosy when there are few other native flowers in the mountains. There are still a few native flowers - see my [Next] photos. Notes: 15123758624 I believe this is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) working a flower of Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare, Apiaceae). Both insect and flower are non-native! I guess I can't complain too much, as there are few other flowers in the mountains right now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2014)

File.Name: eb08e_eb02-131.jpg
Notes:
15558152598 This is the Desert Trumpet (Eriogonum inflatum, Polygonaceae) growing in the Alabama Hills desert. If you squint at the photo, maybe you can see that it's still flowering even after three drought years in a row. It's a strange but very common plant near our cabin on Lone Pine Creek. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

Buckwheats are amazing! They run the gamut from small herbs to large shrubs. There are a zillion different kinds, and they are mostly hard to identify. But this one is easy!

File.Name: eb08f__eb02-118.jpg
Notes: 15557593919 At a first glance, this looks like the same California Buckwheat we have at home, but on a second glance the foliage looks quite different with gray-hairy leaves. I believe this one is the Eastern Mojave Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum var. polifolium, Polygonaceae), a desert variety of a familiar shrub. It's growing near the base of a granitic outcrop in the Alabama Hills desert. (Alabama Hills, Owens Valley, Inyo County, California, 2 November 2014)

File.Name: eb09a_eb09-030.jpg
Notes:
15568447380 This is a "Green Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the Heteroptera, Hemiptera, maybe genus Chinavia. It was perched on a seed head of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. This was my only insect photo today, unless you count the rest of my gall photos. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)

It was a bit cooler and breezier today, a sign that the weather is about to swing to the cool side. I have a few busy days coming up, and I'll save the rest of my Lone Pine photos.
