File.Name: eb09b_eb09-003.jpg
Notes:
15567929787 Here are a pair of very similar "oak apple" galls growing on very different oaks in the woods.

On the left is a gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) growing on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). These are our largest and most recognizable insect galls, the ones everyone knows. It's interesting that the large gall is accompanied by a smaller fresh gall on the same branch. I'm confused by the schedule of these galls. I got this photo today.

On the right is a gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I got this photo a few days ago, on November 5.

Scrub Oak is a "white oak" and Coast Live Oak is a "black oak". They have different galls, which is sometimes useful for telling our many different oaks apart. Both gall wasps are in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: eb09c_eb09-019.jpg
Notes: 15754409462 I found both of these odd galls on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family today, and they share a resemblance. Both have a "salt and pepper" appearance like a frittata, and both hug the margin of the leaf in the same way. Of course the one on the left is a hollow ball and the one on the right looks more like a billfold or an earmuff. They look similar, but probably that's just because all oak galls are based on the genetics of the oak they occur on. I don't know whose galls either of these is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)

File.Name: eb09d_eb09-009.jpg
Notes: 15133284853 This is almost a gall on a gall, but not quite. In the left of my photo is a gall of the Woolly Gall Wasp (Heteroecus dasydactyli, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), and on the right is a "Lumpy Gall" (my name) whose owner I don't know. Both are growing on a stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. The "lumpy galls" usually cause a bend in the stem, but for some reason this one is straight. The "woolly gall" looks partially dissolved after our rain a week ago, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)
CommonNames: Woolly Gall Wasp; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Heteroecus dasydactyli; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Heteroecus dasydactyli; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: eb09e_eb09-016.jpg
Notes: 15754143342 I don't know whose galls these are, but I call them "Lumpy Galls" because of their shape. They are "integral stem galls" on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. They usually cause a bend in the stem, and sometimes kill the leaves beyond. I have more photos of these galls here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)

File.Name: eb09f_eb09-005.jpg
Notes: 15753991282 This swelling is the galled stem of a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe these are integral stem galls of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae, Lepidoptera). This one is on a still-flowering male staminate plant of this "dioecious" shrub with separate male and female plants, compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; dioecious

File.Name: eb10a_eb10-017.jpg
Notes:
15576417788 Here is a small Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a flower of (I guess) Mexican Bush Sage (Salvia leucantha, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It was gloomy today, but I got this photo just when the sun finally burned through the clouds. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2014)

It was a crummy day for new photos today, mostly gloomy and breezy. I'll be busy for the next two days, and maybe not be able to post new photos. I'll try.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Mexican Bush Sage

File.Name: eb10b_eb10-011.jpg
Notes:
15759837431 There's new life in this dead-looking native succulent Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the (Crassulaceae) plant family. I got the photo on the left on October 30. Then we had a 1-1/4 inch of rain the next day on Halloween night. I got the photo on the right today, ten days later. You can see that the dead-looking plant is trying to come back. I hope it makes it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2014)

I take a lot of photos that I don't really expect to use, but sometimes they come in handy for before-and-after shots like this. The lesson is to take many photos!

File.Name: eb10c_eb10-007.jpg
Notes: 15759779381 There's new life in these dead-looking stems of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Our 1-1/4 inch of Halloween rain has mostly evaporated away, but it did some good for these native plants in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2014)

File.Name: eb10d_eb09-043.jpg
Notes: 15576187928 Here are nearly ripe berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The plants are also called Christmas Berry, and they make fine holiday decorations. The ripe berries are edible, but astringent. If you make an infusion in boiling water and add a bit of honey, they make a delicious drink. They are not having a great year with our drought, but some plants like this one look good. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)

File.Name: eb10e_eb08-028.jpg
Notes: 15576713670 This is looking down into a seed head of native Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family - aka Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus). The seeds have an interesting porous texture, like old bones. The summer flowers are wonderful, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 November 2014)
CommonNames: Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Weed's Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Calochortus weedii; vestus
associatedTaxa: Calochortus fimbriatus; Liliaceae; Calochortus weedii; vestus

File.Name: eb10f_eb07-006.jpg
Notes: 15141498644 This is a tiny moth caterpillar (Lepidoptera) munching pollen on a still-flowering shrub of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I don't know anything else about it. These flowers are getting scarce, but there still are a few. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 November 2014)

File.Name: eb12a_eb12-011.jpg
Notes:
15753091656 I found this adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) today. It's probably a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). This photo shows its impressive proboscis better than my [Previous] photo, which shows the top of the insect better. I dig the green colors of its proboscis! Like most members of the guild of assassins, it uses stealth rather than brute strength to get its prey - which are mostly small insects like leafhoppers, hence its name. These are one of my favorite autumn insects. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2014)

I apologize for not posting (or taking photos) yesterday. A dear friend went "under the knife" for vascular surgery yesterday, and I was preoccupied. They got home today, and all is well. Yesterday was a cold and gloomy day anyway!
Locality: or taking photos) yesterday.  A dear friend went "under the knife" for vascular surgery yesterday

File.Name: eb12b_eb12-013.jpg
Notes: 15753007036 I found this adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) today. It's probably a Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii). You can see its impressive spike-like proboscis folded under its head. Like most members of the guild of assassins, it uses stealth rather than brute strength to get its prey - which are mostly small insects like leafhoppers, hence its name. These are one of my favorite autumn insects. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2014)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry; Leafhopper Assassin Bug

File.Name: eb12c_eb12-004.jpg
Notes: 15157163773 This is a fairly large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a fallen oak leaf. This one has malformed wings, but maybe it just emerged from its pupal case and is still pumping them up? They have wonderful patterned eyes. I found this one on the ground about 10 feet from our compost bin, which may be its origin. It gets its first common name from the translucent areas on the first abdominal segment, barely visible here. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2014)

File.Name: eb12d_a427-080.jpg
Notes: 15156518124 Here are a pair of tiny mating Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. The flies are connected by the female's (on the bottom) ovipositor. This is a sweet moment that would go unseen without my camera. These little flies have speckled wings and wonderful eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2010)

File.Name: eb12e_a630-049.jpg
Notes:
15156986163 It's a choo-choo train of Carpenter Bees!

This all began when the first male Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera jumped on the female. Then another male came in to join the party to form a three-some! From right to left, the bees were doing what they could to shake off those behind. (Look at the antennae for sharpness!) That bee in the back looks like he means business - but what?? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2010)

I got this photo in the morning a few years ago on flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly", "Lamiaceae) in the garden. The female bees have black eyes and hairs; the males have gray-spotted eyes and a white mane.

This is an old photo from before I started posting to Flickr. It's great fun looking through the archives for photos to post to the Insect Hump Day group.
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Lamiaceae

File.Name: eb12f_eb12-015.jpg
Notes:
15156909443 You can't tell by looking - at least I can't - but this is new life on a native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's one of the plants with violet flowers, see this photo. This plant is a true biennial. It came up last year, and looked almost dead this summer. I hope it will flower next year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2014)

All of our native thistles are Cirsium occidentale, but there are plants in different varieties with white, violet, and red flowers. (See this photo.) The natives are wonderful, the invasive thistle are horrible. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you know the difference! If there's any doubt, leave them be.
Locality: See this photo.) The natives are wonderful

File.Name: eb13a_eb13-009.jpg
Notes:
15785876282 This is a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) trying to hide in the narrow leaves of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). It's a female with narrow wings. I believe these brown ones are Western Tree Crickets (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill. See my [Previous] photo for a "butt shot" of this critter! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2014)

It was chilly and gloomy all day today, but I did get some photos.

This is a female with narrow wings. I believe these brown ones are Western Tree Crickets (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill. It looks like something else, maybe a crawdad? HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2014)

It was perched on a seed head of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family. HBBBT! (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2014)

File.Name: eb13d_eb13-005.jpg
Notes:
15598549848 I believe this is a tiny Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) - mostly because of those spiny rear legs. I found it perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). You can see its sexual parts in the back. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2014)

I really have no business trying to get a photo of a critter this size, but I spotted it and had to try. This critter is about 1375 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot. That makes it about 5.5 mm long. (See this photo for how I figured.)

There are about a zillion different kinds of leafhoppers! This website reports that "There are more leafhopper species worldwide than all species of birds, mammals, reptiles, and amphibians combined." It was a gloomy morning with some drizzle and a slight breeze. I was looking for insects to photograph and I found this tiny one.
Locality:
See this photo for how I figured.)

File.Name: eb13e_eb13-006.jpg
Notes:
15599048870 I first thought this was a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera), but Alice Abelia set me straight. It's really a Dustywing (family Coniopterygidae, order Neuroptera). They are tiny predators that prey on even tinier critters. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2014)

In my favor, BugGuide says that "adults superficially similar to whiteflies (Aleyrodidae) but have longer legs (esp. the hindlegs), longer antennae, longer beak-like mouthparts, and wings held tent-like at a more steep angle over the abdomen at rest". I was chuckling trying to imagine one of these tiny Dustywings riding on the back of a mighty Dobson Fly - but these large critters have now been moved to the order Megaloptera. It's still a funny thought.

I found this one looking over the edge of a single spine of a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae). I really have no business trying to get a photo of a critter this size, but I saw it land and I had to try. This critter is about 680 pixels long in this 1:1 macro shot. That makes it about 2.7 mm long from its head to its wingtips. (See this photo for how I figured.)

It was a gloomy morning with some drizzle and a slight breeze. I was looking for insects to photograph and I found this tiny one.

File.Name: eb13f_eb13-025.jpg
Notes: 15760070806 We DO have fall colors here on the edge of Southern California, but you have to hunt for it. Here are fall-color leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family growing in the mountains. The maples are having a tough time in this drought year. Most leaves are just shriveling up and turning brown, but this tree has some proper colors. I like that normal green leaf in the midst of its colored brethren. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2014)

File.Name: eb14a_eb14-014.jpg
Notes:
15789198071 This is looking close at the head of a fairly large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a fallen oak leaf. It's maybe 3/4 inch long? This one has been grooming itself, and it's now rubbing it's hands together. They have interesting antennae and wonderful patterned eyes that look like TV pixels when you sit too close to the screen. I found this one on the ground about 10 feet from our compost bin, which may well be its origin. This is the same critter as my [Previous] photo. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2014)

Hah - there is a Black Soldier Fly Blog on the web! I believe they are good maggots to have in your compost, possibly predators.

It was another beautiful day today after the morning clouds cleared out. I went to the river today, and it got a bit breezy.

File.Name: eb14b_eb14-006.jpg
Notes:
15789125831 This is a fairly large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a fallen oak leaf. It's maybe 3/4 inch long? They have interesting antennae and wonderful patterned eyes. I found this one on the ground about 10 feet from our compost bin, which may well be its origin. I believe this one is newly emerged from its pupal case and is still pumping up its wings. (We seem to have a hatch going on.) It gets its species name and its first common name from the translucent areas on the first abdominal segment, clearly visible here. See my [Previous] and [Next] photos. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2014)

Hah - there is a Black Soldier Fly Blog on the web! I believe they are good maggots to have in your compost.

15170954844 This is a fairly large Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a fallen oak leaf. It's maybe 3/4 inch long? They have interesting antennae and wonderful patterned eyes. I found this one on the ground about 10 feet from our compost bin, which may well be its origin. This one is a fully functional adult, but I've also been finding a few that look like they are newly emerged. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2014)

File.Name: eb14d_eb14-033.jpg
Notes:
15605407978 This is a tattered but still beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly by the river today. It seems to be sucking dry seed heads, but it's actually working new flowers of some kind of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, Asteraceae). The stem is barely visible coming in from the upper-left. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 November 2014)

Butterflies are getting scarce, but there still are a few - as long as the flowers last. I'll keep trying to get photos until they are gone.

File.Name: eb14e_eb14-026.jpg
Notes:
15788886521 This is a worn but still pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working some of the last flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. These late-season butterflies are quite camera shy. I was on one side of the plant and the butterfly was low-down on the other side. I tricked it by shooting through the plant. I guess we're all paparazzi in one way or another! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 November 2014)

I'm really posting this just to prove we still have some butterflies in our autumn woods!

There are about a zillion different kinds of leafhoppers! File.Name: eb15a_eb15-019.jpg
Notes:
15775059046 This is a small net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Issidae. It's perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Very cool looking insect! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2014)

It's been a good time for tiny insects lately! It was a calm morning, but the wind has been picking up today. I had to take many photos to get this keeper, and it's still not the best. We're under a NWS Wind Advisory tonight. I hope it will let up tomorrow.

File.Name: eb15b_eb15-013.jpg
Notes:
15178591924 I believe this really is a Whitefly (Aleyrodidae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) (right) and pupae (left) on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. Maybe they are Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi). (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2014)

I was confused by the Dustywing (family Coniopterygidae, order Neuroptera) that looks very similar, see this photo. One important diagnostic different is that the Dustywings fold their wings up like a tent while the Whiteflies keep them flat. This one is also smaller, only 400 pixels across at 1:1 magnification, which makes it about 1.6 mm long. Like I said, it's really tiny.

File.Name: eb15c_eb15-004.jpg
Notes: 15613090488 This is a golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for just a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods. It has wasp-like colors. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2014)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: eb15d_eb15-009.jpg
Notes: 15612553669 This is a small Puffball (Lycoperdon) mushroom coming up in the woods with new seedlings. It's the first mushroom I've found outside the garden this year! It has an interesting texture. I won't try to guess what it is. Mushrooms have been so scarce for the last few drought years that I've almost forgotten what they are. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2014)

File.Name: eb15e_eb15-005.jpg
Notes:
15178783183 This is a gall of the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on the new bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, the first I've noticed this year. This one looks more yellow than green, but I'm pretty sure this is it. The galls only last for a short time, and then fall away. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2014)

I believe this is the unisexual gall of a critter that also has sexual galls in the spring, possibly this one. Nature is complicated like that!
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: eb15f_eb13-015.jpg
Notes:
15178187484 These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon - probably the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. These plants go into "the big sleep" when summer comes, and I was happy to see them come back! They are part of what the rains brings. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2014)

They almost look as though they're already forming gemmae, asexual buds which spread spores. See this photo.

File.Name: eb16a_eb16-047.jpg
Notes:
15807635275 This is a lovely Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working one of the last flowers of native composite Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. The four or five eye-spots on the hind wings are distinctive for this butterfly. They were quite camera shy, and it took a while to get this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 November 2014)

This is a True Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae subfamily, It is perched on just four legs, with her two front legs folded up in front in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air.

It was yet another lovely day. There wasn't much wind, though it was in the forecast last night. It does feel like autumn.

File.Name: eb16c_eb16-013.jpg
Notes: 15622564960 This is a little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for just a moment on a late-season flower of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. Yep, there still are a few flowers on last season's plants while the new season's plants appear. Both fly and flower are wonderful. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 November 2014)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: eb16d_eb16-023.jpg
Notes:
15805406621 I was checking on the flower buds of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today, and I happened to notice these tiny insect eggs. They are shaped like little closed baskets. I have no idea whose eggs they are. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 November 2014)

There are no flowers yet, though they are getting close. These will probably be the third native plant to flower in this drought year, after the plants in my [Previous] two photos. Last year, I noticed the first open flowers on December 13, but I think they'll be earlier this year. It would be nice if they open before next Sunday, when we leave on the train for our usual Oregon Thanksgiving - but maybe not.

File.Name: eb16e_eb16-001.jpg
Notes:
15621331899 Well I'll be darned! Despite the ongoing drought (we're going into our fourth year) here is a new flower of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. It has real flowers, not just bracts. The red color is from the leafy bracts. The real flowers are the yellow-green tubes, with exerted pistils. It's a subshrub with woolly white hairs. Life is tough! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2014)

I reckon this is our second native plant to flower in this drought year, after the Chaparral Currant in my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: eb16f_eb16-029.jpg
Notes: 15807049115 This is a small shrub of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family that is covered with flowers. It's kind of a gloomy photo that I got in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, but the new flowers are remarkable. I figure this plant has the first new flowers in this rain year. See this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 November 2014)

File.Name: eb17a_eb17-008.jpg
Notes:
15630921419 I believe this is an Edwards' Glassy-wing (Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii, Arctiinae, Noctuoidea, Lepidoptera) moth. It's a Tiger Moth, one of the moths that come from "woolly bear" caterpillars, see this photo. They seem to have two different modes - lay still like my [Last] photo, or run like hell! I got this shot when it paused for just a second. A moment later it disappeared over the porch railing. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2014)

I don't usually photograph nocturnal moths, but I found this one clinging to my shirt early this morning and I couldn't resist. I placed it on the upstairs porch railing in the morning shade and got the photo before it crawled away. These moths are unusual in having mostly clear unscaled wings.

I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, so I made my usual stop by Lake Cachuma. It was pleasant with calm winds, though high clouds have been moving in. There's no rain in our forecast.

File.Name: eb17b_eb17-007.jpg
Notes:
15630725429 I believe this is an Edwards' Glassy-wing (Pseudohemihyalea edwardsii, Arctiinae, Noctuoidea, Lepidoptera) moth. It's a Tiger Moth, one of the moths that come from "woolly bear" caterpillars, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2014)

I don't usually photograph nocturnal moths, but I found this one clinging to my shirt early this morning and I couldn't resist. I placed it on the upstairs porch railing in the morning shade and got the photo before it crawled away. These moths are unusual in having mostly clear unscaled wings.

File.Name: eb17c_eb17-010.jpg
Notes:
15631254047 I found both of these tiny mushrooms in native parts of our garden this morning. They are about the same size, but different. I'm pretty sure the one on the right is genus Mycena, but I'm not sure of the furry one on the left. I've been looking for them. Mushrooms have been scarce in these drought years, so I'm happy to find them! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2014)

It's interesting that the rain came on Halloween night two weeks ago, and the mushrooms are appearing now. I guess it takes them a while to get going.

File.Name: eb17d_eb17-030.jpg
Notes:
15196766003 I found both of these similar bottle-like galls on different leaves of the same shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. There are similarities and differences. Both are growing on leaves not stems. The one on the left is small, but the one on the right is tiny. I got both photos at 1:1 magnification with my 105 mm macro lens, and the gall on the left is about 790 pixels high while the one on the right is about half that, so I figure their size is 3.2 mm and 1.6 mm respectively. The one on the right has a regular circular exit hole where a critter got out, but the one on the left has a ragged hole that suggests that a predator/parasite went through. I've found this gall before, see this photo. I don't know whose gall it is. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 November 2014)

Now how did the crumbs from the critter's exit on the left get above the gall on the leaf?

Maybe they are the galls of the Red Cone Gall Wasp (Andricus kingi), which is a tiny Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera)?
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Red Cone Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; above; Andricus kingi; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; above; Andricus kingi; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: eb17e_eb17-026.jpg
Notes: 15630245509 Here are the same "oak apple galls" growing on different native plants. Sure they look different, but so you and I! They are all galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The galls on the left are on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) and the gall on the right is on Valley Oak (Quercus lobata), both in the Fagaceae plant family. Both oaks are considered "white oaks". The similar galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) - a "black oak" - are different and have a different life cycle. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 November 2014)
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Valley Oak; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: eb17f_eb17-024.jpg
Notes: 15817534902 This is a colony of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The individual galls grow together to form a mass. These striking galls have been scarce in this drought year. I found this group near Lake Cachuma today. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 November 2014)

File.Name: eb18a_eb18-020.jpg
Notes:
15638423408 This is a stunning Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly trying to get water from the rock. I really like the tip of its proboscis meeting its shadow! I found it this morning on a near-vertical sandstone rock outcrop in our canyon. It must be newly-emerged to have those brilliant colors. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

I went through my photo archives to find when I've photographed them before - 5 photos from April to July, and 13 photos from September to January. This seems to be their time in fall and winter, at least here in the mountains.

Fantastic insects like this keep appearing every day, but I don't know how much longer it will happen. I'm taking these photos one day at a time. It was quite breezy this morning from the south-west, which I think of as a rain wind. There's a chance of rain up in San Luis Obispo, but probably not here. Maybe later this week?

File.Name: eb18b_eb18-005.jpg
Notes:
15637875789 I noticed this ball of fluff this morning because it was moving over a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae)! I believe it is the debris-carrying larva of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) insect in the insect order Neuroptera. It's not the best photo, but what a great critter! See BugGuide for more photos of these interesting creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

I got this photo this morning before the sun even came close to the garden. It's a decent photo, considering.

File.Name: eb18c_eb18-010.jpg
Notes:
15825137202 I'll bet this is the pupa of a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera on a fallen oak leaf. It's maybe 3/4 inch long, about the same size as the large fly. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

I was looking for photos this morning, so I went grubbing in the compost that Julie recently spread. I was looking for this critter and I found it! I've been finding the newly-emerged flies nearby, see this photo and [Previous] to that. I believe they are good maggots to have in your compost, possibly predators. Maybe I'm breaking the rules for the Fly Day Friday, but I'm going to post this to group anyway even though I first posted it on Wednesday. It goes so well with the adult fly photos I posted last week. HFDF!

File.Name: eb18d_eb18-036.jpg
Notes:
15823443715 Here are two more shots of the different gall I found on the apex of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It doesn't seem to match anything in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) or Joyce Gross's fine California Oak Galls website. Maybe it's a new one? It looks quite distinctive with that crown of points around the top. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

I'm pretty sure this is the gall of a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). You can see a neat exit hole on the right. It's really tiny, only about 100 pixels in this 1:1 macro photo - only about 0.4 mm across! Well with foreshortening it's probably a bit larger, but it's still really tiny.

This time I played it safe so I wouldn't drop it! I picked the leaf and posed it on my white truck. I don't understand the different background colors, but the sun was in and out of the clouds and the white-balance must have changed.

File.Name: eb18e_eb18-032.jpg
Notes:
15821426471 Here are two shots of a different gall on the apex of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It doesn't seem to match anything in Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006) or Joyce Gross's fine California Oak Galls website. Maybe it's a new one? It looks quite distinctive with that crown of points around the top. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

I'm pretty sure this is the gall of a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). There was a tiny exit hole on the bottom, so I figured the gall was empty. I removed the leaf and tried to pose it for a photo, but it fell away and got lost. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: eb18f_eb18-030.jpg
Notes:
15637845368 Here are ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending interesting galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls produce "oak honey" which attracts the ants, who in turn keep the predators away. This is a great example of different critters cooperating for a common good. These are the only active galls of this kind that I found this year. See this photo of the same galls a month ago. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

The ants sure look like California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae. These are the ants that devil us at home in the spring, see this photo.
Locality: Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae. These are the ants that devil us at home in the spring

File.Name: eb19a_eb19-001.jpg
Notes:
15644987709 Here is a tiny spider perched on a cactus spine in the garden early this morning. Spiders of any kind have been scarce this autumn, which isn't surprising since insects have also been scarce in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2014)

It was a crummy day for photos - dark and windy, even a bit of drizzle. *Sigh* There was no rain to speak of, but I did my duty and did go out for photos. I'm starting to get distracted because we're heading up to Oregon on the train next Sunday, and there's lots to do before we leave...

File.Name: eb19b_eb19-012.jpg
Notes: 15830710315 I found this perfect egg-like case on an old catkin of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) today. I reckon it's a pupal case, woven out of silk-like hairs. I have no idea who it belongs to. It was a crummy day for photos - dark and windy, even a bit of drizzle - but it was perfect for this tiny case. Direct sunlight would have obscured the texture. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2014)

It was a crummy day for photos - dark and windy, even a bit of drizzle. Still, here it is! I reckon this is the third native plant to flower in this drought year - after Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) and Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa,Orobanchaceae). Notes:
15831970092 These handsome Long-horned Beetles (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera are "doing it" on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) leaf in the woods. They didn't like me watching and soon broke up. I can't blame them! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2010)

I don't think I've seen these interesting beetles since I got this photo back in 2010. This is an old photo from before I started posting to Flickr. It's great fun looking through the archives for photos to post to the Insect Hump Day group.

File.Name: eb19f_9611-144.jpg
Notes:
15828395081 This is a rave of coin-sized Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera - maybe Largus californicus) - going at it at the base of an interesting plant in the woods. For some reason known only to them, more than a dozen of these bugs were congregating at the base of this particular plant. Most of them quickly scattered as I placed my tripod, but I managed to capture a few that were entangled with mating. I came back again later, and they were still going at it. (Maybe they didn't know which way to run? ;=) The pairs seem to have a kind of universal joint between them. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2009)

I believe the interesting plant is Clustered Broomrape (Orobanche fasiculata) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. It's a plant with no chlorophyll that grows as an obligate root parasite and has a fungal connection with another flowering plant nearby - probably native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Boraginaceae or Hydrophyllaceae plant family.

This is an old photo from before I started posting to Flickr. It's great fun looking through the archives for photos to post to the Insect Hump Day group.

Notes:
15837526875 This is a "Little Brown Mushroom" (LBM) that appeared today in what's left of the mint patch of the garden after Julie trimmed away the old stems. It's a small mushroom, only about an inch across. It might be genus Tricholoma with those notched gills, but I'm just guessing. Mushrooms have been so scarce in these drought years that I'm happy to find any! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2014)

It so looked like rain today, but I'm still waiting. There has been a bit of rain in the western parts of Santa Barbara county out towards Santa Maria and Lompoc, but nothing here. The rain appears to be moving in from the west which may leave us out of range.

I had errands in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but I did get a few photos before I left. I'm starting to get distracted because we're heading up to Oregon on the train next Sunday, and there's lots to do before we leave...

File.Name: eb20e_eb20-011.jpg
Notes:
15217739463 These are a "rolled leaves" on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure if it counts as a gall or just a mechanically rolled leaf? The leaf does look discolored like a gall and maybe the cell structure is altered. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2014)

"Leaf rollers" and "gall insects" really have a similar strategy - to have the plant provide food and shelter. The more I think about it, the more similar they seem. Both kinds of insect get shelter and food from the host plant. And how can one folded leaf provide all the sustenance that an insect larvae needs without some help? Now that I think about it, how does the insect fold the leaf UP rather than DOWN in its natural direction? And I don't see any webbing or glue to hold the edge down.

File.Name: eb20f_eb20-010.jpg
Notes: 15835151681 This is a neat fungus pattern on a green leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can almost see the fungus growing outwards like a fairy ring from a central spot. It may be "Oak anthracnose" - though it's my impression that's a fairly generic descriptive term for a condition which can have many causes. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2014)

I especially like this photo because this butterfly's pose is so much like the pose in my [Previous] photo - of a different kind of butterfly. Try going back and forth between them and see if you can spot the differences. I'll post a side-by-side photo tomorrow.

Yes, I'm a bit distracted because we're heading up to Oregon on the train next Sunday, and there's lots to do before we leave... But I had to revisit the first flowers of this manzanita shrub! New flowers means new insects, and I found a few today.

These are flowers and seed heads of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. This is a late flower from last season rather than a new flower from this season. It's that time of year when old and new overlap. I was bummed when the Jepson Manual first changed the genus name, but the plants do have typical Epilobium seed pods. Compare this photo of a very different plant in the same genus. This shrubby plant looks mostly dead, but there is that one stem with wonderful flowers, and a few more up the hill. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2014)

Notes:
15669210859 Me and my shadow...

This is a stunning Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on a sandstone rock outcrop in our canyon. It must be newly-emerged to have those brilliant colors. See this photo for another shot of the same butterfly. By the way, this Admiral is a Lady! It's in the same genus as the West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) and Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterflies in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2014)

Just two photos today, as I'm totally distracted because we're heading up to Oregon on the train tomorrow for our Thanksgiving celebration with family. I enjoy being on the road, but I always get frazzled packing for it. We'll be back in less than two weeks, hopefully with many mushroom photos to share.

File.Name: eb22b_eb21-024.jpg
Notes:
15854445912 Here are side by side photos of a pair of handsome ladies - a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly on the left and a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on the right. Both are working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. See my [Previous] two photos. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 November 2014)

The differences are subtle. The West Coast Lady is smaller and has an orange bar on her forewings, whereas the Painted Lady has white markings. There are other differences if you look close. Both of these have four or five eyespots under the hindwing, whereas the American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) has just two. They are pretty obviously different when they are on the same plant like these were, but it gets trickier when they are alone.
Locality: Vanessa virginensis) has just two. They are pretty obviously different when they are on the same plant like these were

File.Name: ec04a_eb28-037.jpg
Notes:
15762920797 This is our harvest of Black Chanterelle aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms along with a few Golden Chanterelles (Cantharellus formosus) and Hedgehog Mushrooms (Hydnum repandum) for our Thanksgiving dinner at our cabin in the Oregon woods. The Hedgehog Mushrooms are at the top of the photo. For some reason, we only found a few this year. We cooked up this whole bunch and added it to our Turkey gravy and it was absolutely delicious. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

We've been off to our cabin in Oregon for the past 10 days. It rained every day but one, but I did get lots of mushroom photos. We got home last night to more rain! We missed the big storm on Tuesday, but we had the pleasure of more rain today. We now officially have 4.73 inches of rain on San Marcos Pass over the last few days! That actually bring us slightly above normal for this time of year. It's not the end of our drought, but it helps. I hope to get some mushroom photos from home soon!

It's curious - I think we got as much rain in that one day at home as we got during the whole week in Oregon.
CommonNames: Black Chanterelle; Horn of Plenty; Golden Chanterelles; Hedgehog Mushrooms; Hedgehog Mushrooms
ScientificName: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus formosus; Hydnum repandum
associatedTaxa: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus formosus; Hydnum repandum

File.Name: ec04b_eb28-042.jpg
Notes:
15761131888 Here is a pan of fresh-picked Thanksgiving Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms sizzling on the stove. (We always celebrate Thanksgiving on Friday to give the kids a driving day.) These remarkable mushrooms deepen their color as they cook. There are also a few Golden Chanterelles (Cantharellus formosus) and Hedgehog Mushrooms (Hydnum repandum) in the pan - see my [Next] photo. We added them all to the Turkey gravy and it was absolutely delicious. This photo has some white balance issues, but it's clear enough. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

We let them "stew in their own juices". It's really remarkable how much juice these mushrooms make in a hot pan! (The thin-fleshed black ones make less juice, so it was wise to add the goldens.) Clean your mushrooms by trimming them and avoid water if possible - they are like sponges. Then slice them into a pan on high heat with no oil, and they will make their own sauce as the water cooks out of them. Let the juice cook down until it's almost gone, and add a bit of butter or olive oil, salt, and garlic and let them simmer... delicious!
Locality: The thin-fleshed black ones make less juice
CommonNames: Black Chanterelles; Horn of Plenty; Golden Chanterelles; Hedgehog Mushrooms
ScientificName: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus formosus; Hydnum repandum
associatedTaxa: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus formosus; Hydnum repandum

File.Name: ec04c_eb28-017.jpg
Notes: 15762428109 These are Black Chanterelle aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods. These mushrooms are growing in the same place we've found them for the last few years, and it helps to know where they are ahead of time because they look more like dead leaves or a hole in the ground. They are absolutely delicious. This year we added them to our turkey gravy. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

File.Name: ec04d_eb28-038.jpg
Notes: 15326151804 This is most of our harvest of Golden Chanterelle (Cantharellus formosus) mushrooms for our Thanksgiving dinner at our cabin in the Oregon woods. We didn't find a huge number of them this year, but we did OK! They are beautiful and tasty, a prize for our Thanksgiving table as well as the official state mushroom of Oregon. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

File.Name: ec04e_eb26-022.jpg
Notes:
15326008534 I believe these are Golden Chanterelle (Cantharellus formosus) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods. They are beautiful and tasty, a prize for our Thanksgiving table as well as the official state mushroom of Oregon. We didn't find a huge number of them this year, but we did OK - see my [Next] photo! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

*Sigh* It used to be easier to identify these mushrooms when all the orange chanterelles were considered to be Cantharellus cibarius. Now that species has been split up into C. formosus and C. californicus and more. The Oregon mushrooms are smaller and maybe a bit mushier than our chanterelles at home in California. Both are distinctive and tasty.

File.Name: ec04f_eb28-030.jpg
Notes: 15762210129 I believe these are the Funnel Chanterelle aka Winter Chanterelle (Cantharellus infundibuiformis) in the Oregon woods, growing on decomposing wood. They are said to be edible but they are small and rather thin-fleshed, so it would take a lot of mushrooms to make a meal. We didn't collect them for the table. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

File.Name: ec05a_ec05-010.jpg
Notes:
15955189222 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a lovely hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus), formerly included in A. columbianus as in my [Previous] photo. It's the first one I've seen in this new rain year. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. I only found this one slug today, crawling through the new seedlings (mostly weeds) along our road. The longer tentacles in front on the right are eyestalks with sensitive dark "pupils" and the lower pair are feelers and tasters. I was happy to find this large slug. It's a sign that our woods are coming back to life after a long dry drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2014)

How in the heck do these slugs survive from one year to the next, especially in this drought year? Do they dehydrate into almost nothing like pipe cleaners, or do they find a burrow and go into a big sleep? I suspect the latter, though I have yet to find one in the dry time.

The woods at home are showing signs of new life - I also found a streaming slime mold and several new mushrooms today. Everything now depends on what the weather brings next. We have an even chance of more rain tonight, then a few dry days. There's a rumor of more good rain at the end of next week. We sure need it!

File.Name: ec05b_eb28-002.jpg
Notes: 15769687069 This is a large green Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods under a mushroom. I guess the mushroom is a large white Russula - at least the stem "snaps" like one. These slugs look different from our banana slugs at home, compare my [Next] photo. Sometimes these large slugs have black spots or a bit of orange color. They have a strong ridge along the back that our California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) seem to lack. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; California Banana Slugs

File.Name: ec05c_eb26-013.jpg
Notes: 15335975213 This is another Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) that may be different from my [Previous] photo. It's growing from a bed of moss in the Oregon woods. Both mushrooms are tangerine size, but the branching color and patterns patterns look different. These fantastic mushrooms are easy to recognize and hard to identify. I won't try to guess. It looks like it belongs under the ocean, with waving strands of seaweed! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

File.Name: ec05d_eb30-028.jpg
Notes: 15333205604 This is a different Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) than my [Previous] photo. That mushroom is cantaloupe size, but this one is only the size of a tangerine. These fantastic mushrooms are easy to recognize and hard to identify. I won't try to guess. It looks like it belongs under the ocean! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2014)

File.Name: ec05e_eb26-027.jpg
Notes:
15955406735 Coral Mushrooms (genus Ramaria) are easy to recognize and hard to identify. I won't try to guess. This pair seem to be different from both of the [Next] ones - but I might be wrong. It looks like they belong under the ocean! These are each about the size of a small cantaloupe. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

I didn't notice that little brown mushroom on the left of the coral mushroom on the left. I believe it's in the genus Leptonia in the Entolomataceae family. I'll post another photo of another one in a day or two.

File.Name: ec05f_eb28-024.jpg
Notes: 15955354355 These are strange Truncate Club Coral (Clavariadelphus truncatus) mushrooms growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. They are said to be delicious and sweet, but I haven't tried them. They look a bit like Chanterelles (see my [Previous] photos), but they are related to the Coral mushrooms in my [Next] photos. These were the only ones I found this year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

File.Name: ec06a_ec05-008.jpg
Notes:
15942673576 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a beautiful cluster of still-streaming Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) plasmodia becoming sporangia on fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, the first I've found this year. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. I've found them before, and I believe this photo shows what they become. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2014)

I went back to get a follow up photo the next day, and I couldn't find them. I guess they quickly turned into sporangia and were just too inconspicuous to notice. These may be my favorite critters in the woods. They've been scarce in these drought years, so I was happy to find them.

I tried and tried to post this photo last night, but I kept getting "time out" errors. I assumed it was a Flickr problem, but the problem recurred this morning. Finally I reset our router and everything was fine. "The fault, dear Brutus, lies not in our stars, but in ourselves..." Live and learn, at least next time this happens I'll know what to check first.

(I reset the "date posted" to December 6 to keep my records straight.)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia

File.Name: ec06b_eb28-020.jpg
Notes: 15775991130 I guess these are the Scarlet Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe punicea) mushrooms with yellow stems and viscid caps, but cp. Hygrocybe coccinea. It's one of those. These specimens were rather small, an inch or less across. They are growing in moss in a wet place in the Oregon woods - but aren't they all! Maybe it's edible, but I reckon it's better appreciated than digested. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)

File.Name: ec06c_eb26-047.jpg
Notes: 15962510142 I believe these are Golden Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe flavescens aka Hygrophorus flavescens) mushrooms growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. It's said to be edible but watery and tasteless. Even with lousy light, what a brilliant mushroom! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

File.Name: ec06d_eb26-036.jpg
Notes: 15961111251 These are top and bottom photos of a wonderful Violet Cortinarius (Cortinarius violaceus) mushroom in the Oregon woods. They are such a deep violet color that they look almost black. See this photo for another look with more color in less gloomy light. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

File.Name: ec06e_eb28-014.jpg
Notes:
15775584908 This is a fresh "Lobster Mushroom" (Hypomyces lacifluorum) in the Oregon woods. Actually the "lobster" is a parasitic crust over another host mushroom, typically a Lactarius or Russula, but you really can't tell because the lobster takes over. Woodland critters seem to love these mushrooms. We find many of them pushed over like this one or broken up with pieces scattered. Usually they are orange, but sometimes red. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

I'm leery about eating them, because you never know what mushroom they are growing on. Surprisingly, they are tasty - at least fresh off the grill, when the fungus is fresh. True to their name, they have a slightly fishy taste, like crab cakes. We didn't collect them for the table this year, but we have in the past. There's a market for them in Portland.

File.Name: ec06f_eb26-039.jpg
Notes: 15775644500 This is an odd-looking "Delicious Lactarius" (Lactarius deliciosus) mushroom in the Oregon woods. It looks anything but delicious with those green colors on the cap! It is said to be "edible, but not necessarily delicious". Green is such an odd color for mushrooms which don't do photosynthesis. This one is growing under Douglas Fir - like most of my Oregon mushrooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

File.Name: ec07a_ec07-008.jpg
Notes:
15970204381 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is what the streaming orange Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) in this photo turned into in the past two days. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. No wonder I couldn't find them yesterday! They are tiny, but quite beautiful up close, with what fly fishermen call a "blue dun" color. (I had problem posting last night, so the other photo didn't go up until this morning.)

This scene is on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The sporangia look like fungi, but the spores will become tiny amoeba-like critters, not fungus material. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2014)

There's a nice NASA video that shows why we got so much rain last week - 4.67 inches on the pass! It was a real "pineapple express" when a low-pressure system from the north meets up with a large stream of moisture of tropical origin. We got home from the Oregon the day after the storm. I'm sorry we missed it.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Physarella; Badhamia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Physarella; Badhamia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ec07b_eb30-003.jpg
Notes: 15352470803 This is a lovely Mycena mushroom growing from a mossy tree trunk in the Oregon woods. It has pretty violet colors. There are hundreds of different species so I won't try to guess. This one sure is pretty. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2014)

File.Name: ec07c_eb28-012.jpg
Notes: 15352407403 I guess these mushrooms from Oregon are (from left to right) - the Fluted White Elfin Saddle (Helvella crispa), The Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle (Helvella maculata or maybe H. sulcata?), and the Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa). All have carved out stems. I personally wouldn't collect any of them for my dinner table. Compare my photo from last year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2014)
CommonNames: Fluted White Elfin Saddle; Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle; Fluted Black Elfin Saddle
ScientificName: Helvella crispa; Helvella maculata; H. sulcata; Helvella lacunosa
associatedTaxa: Helvella crispa; Helvella maculata; H. sulcata; Helvella lacunosa

File.Name: ec07d_eb26-042.jpg
Notes: 15784518240 Here are two photos of the top and bottom of an interesting Polypore fungus in the family Polyporaceae. It's interesting because it grows with a stalk rather than a shelf, but it has the same tough texture of other polypores. Polyporus badius and P. elegans come to mind because of the black stem, but I'm not sure what it is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

File.Name: ec07e_eb26-005.jpg
Notes: 15784259428 Thanks to Jacob Kalichman, I now believe this mushroom is the Gold Flecked Waxy Cap (Hygrophorus chrysodon) in the Oregon woods. It reminds me of a Bolbitius, but more robust. The powdery stems are attractive, and the Springtail (order Collembola) insects dig them. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2014)

File.Name: ec07f_eb30-001.jpg
Notes: 15784188048 Here are two photos of what I believe is a genus Leptonia mushroom in the Entolomataceae family growing in the Oregon woods. You can see a hint of the pink-colored spores under the cap of the photo on the right. The twisted stem and mycelium at the base seem distinctive. The stems are hard and brittle, like a glass tube. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2014)

File.Name: ec08a_ec07-023.jpg
Notes:
15792755858 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

These are new "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus eastwoodiae) mushrooms that I found in the woods yesterday. (Or maybe Boletus regius? See Matt's comment and my response.) They were formerly considered Boletus satanas, e.g. in David Aroras' Mushrooms Demystified (1986), which is still my main mushroom book. They are the first really large mushroom that I've found this year. These Bolete mushroom have a spongy spore-bearing surface instead of gills - yellow in these, but turning red. The mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle them, and I find it irresistible to leave messages for anyone who might find them. But don't believe everything you read on mushrooms! These mushrooms are quite poisonous. Don't eat them - but please enjoy them! (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2014)

I never found one of these hefty mushrooms last year with the drought, and I figure it's a good sign to find them this year. My big question is whether the mushrooms will come back after our three year drought. I've found a few after our recent rains. I think it's still an open question.

Sure - I have more photos of Oregon mushrooms to post, but I needed at catch-up day for these new mushrooms at home. I'll get back to Oregon photos tomorrow. We have another good chance of rain on Thursday or Friday. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Boletus eastwoodiae; Boletus regius; Boletus satanas
associatedTaxa: Boletus eastwoodiae; Boletus regius; Boletus satanas

File.Name: ec08b_ec06-022.jpg
Notes: 15979346932 This is a hefty "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus eastwoodiae) mushroom that I found in the woods a couple of days ago. It was formerly considered Boletus satanas, e.g. in David Aroras' Mushrooms Demystified (1986), which is still my main mushroom book. It's the first really large mushroom that I've found this year, growing along the road in our canyon. The voluptuous shape reminds me of the paleolithic Venus figurines found in Europe. These Bolete mushroom have a spongy red spore-bearing surface instead of gills. The mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle them - see my [Next] photo. They don't look very appetizing - in fact they are quite poisonous. Don't eat them - but please enjoy them! (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2014)

File.Name: ec08c_ec08-001.jpg
Notes: 15793914179 I believe these are Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees in the woods. They are said to be edible, but I haven't tried them. There are a fair number of them right now after our recent good rain. One field trait for this mushroom is the copious mycelium at the base of the stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2014)

File.Name: ec08d_ec07-003.jpg
Notes: 15357559814 This is the electric white growth of a fungus growing on an old acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It appears to be reaching out to the acorn below it. I once thought this was just the mycelium of a fungus, but I'm pretty sure it is the whole fungus. It's common when you look for it. I have no idea what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2014)

File.Name: ec08e_ec08-007.jpg
Notes: 15357450704 These are different little Mycena mushrooms in the woods. They are darker colored and more clumped than our usual species. They are quite pretty underneath with those orange rimmed gills. I don't seem to have a decent photo of our ordinary Mycenas, but see this photo, on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2014)

File.Name: ec08f_ec05-018.jpg
Notes: 15977615361 These are neat little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family - some with tiny "eggs". I found them growing in an old one-gallon nursery pot, so they might be introduced from the nursery. They might be the Dung-Loving Bird's Nest Fungus (Cyathus stercoreus). The eggs are really tiny spore sacks called peridioles. Rain drops scatter them from the cups, and you can see a few of them lying around in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2014)
CommonNames: Bird's Nest Fungi; Dung-Loving Bird's Nest Fungus

File.Name: ec13a_ec13-002.jpg
Notes:
15396233243 This is a large cluster of beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. They are still dripping with rain water from our 5 1/4 inches of rain two days ago. These are growing in a place where the sun never shines, so the white balance is what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2014)

I apologize for disappearing so abruptly. Our DSL internet connection went down suddenly, and none of my tricks worked to restore it. Finally a Verizon service person came up to our house this afternoon and did some magic to restore it. (Thanks, Mike!) I don't know what he did, but he had to go down to the substation to "change a few jumpers" and it finally came back to life. I had to chuckle because Mike called a Verizon ITT tech support number and had to wait 20 minutes listening to crappy music on his cell phone speaker until they answered!

It was tough going through the big storm without an internet connection. TV news is absolutely no substitute. We didn't get service restored until late today, and I still haven't checked email. These two photos are it. I've been taking photos all through the "internet drought", so more photos tomorrow.

File.Name: ec13b_ec12-013.jpg
Notes: 16013749451 Real water in the Santa Ynez River! It's been a while. This is looking down at the bend in the river near Manzanita Lane, where the road washed out a few years ago. We got 5 1/4 inches of rain on San Marcos Pass two days ago and it had to go somewhere! I'm most impressed with how little runoff there is in the river and the creeks. The rain mostly soaked into the ground, where the drought-parched woods can get it. We're now at 164% of normal rainfall, with a chance of more rain coming next week. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2014)

File.Name: ec14a_ec14-004.jpg
Notes:
15998694146 This is an interesting Fly (Diptera) that I found this morning perched on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I won't try to guess what kind of fly it is, but note those odd flattened lower-leg segments. (I can't resist - maybe Drosophilidae?) (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)

It was cold this morning, still in the 30s when I got up, and not much warmer when I got this photo. One nice thing about cold mornings is that the insects slow down. I got this photo with the timer-delay feature of my camera that opens the mirror then waits a couple of seconds to open the shutter. It's a great feature to minimize camera shake and use a small aperture for DoF.

There were high clouds today, and some lower clouds as the day went on. I guess these are the first signs of our next storm approaching. More rain is expected tomorrow night and Tuesday. Wahoo!

This is a pretty good "butt shot" too. HBBBT!

File.Name: ec14b_ec14-011.jpg
Notes: 16022455901 This is some kind of Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera growing on a sandstone rock in the woods. Maybe it's the Many-fruited Pelt Lichen (Peltigera polydactylon)? I expect to find these lichens in Oregon, but this one is growing near home. They are not common. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)

File.Name: ec14c_ec14-010.jpg
Notes:
16022322951 Secret life in the woods...

These are the same Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) as my [Previous] photo a day later, now become not-quite-ripe sporangia on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Try flipping back and forth between this photo and my [Previous] one. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. They look like tiny fungi, but something different comes from their spores. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)

File.Name: ec14d_ec13-034.jpg
Notes:
15836777408 Secret life in the woods...

Here are more still-streaming Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) plasmodia becoming sporangia on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. See my [Next] photo to see what they'll become just a day later. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2014)

File.Name: ec14e_ec13-040.jpg
Notes:
15836694640 This mushroom had me confused for a while. I dug it out and I think it's "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom. I don't think I've ever seen such a white one, if that's what it is. These mushrooms develop pink colors on cap and stem with age and handling. I dug it out for the photo on the left, and replanted it on the right - with darker stains from the handling. It doesn't seem to have a ring or any visible volva, though it has an unmistakable "amanita aura". This one is an early mushroom - but they also fruit again later in the spring if there is rain. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2014)

I haven't seen one of these since April 2013, see this photo. I've had the question whether our mycorrhizal mushrooms will appear this year after three years of drought. Finding this mushroom is a good sign.

File.Name: ec14f_ec13-032.jpg
Notes: 15836611410 Here is a micro-world of tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves along our driveway. You have to look close to spot these tiny 'shrooms, but it's worth it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2014)

File.Name: ec15a_ec15-021.jpg
Notes:
15846514067 This is another lovely hand-sized California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, formerly included in A. columbianus). You can see it's "pneumostome" - that hole in its side - which leads to its single lung. They only have this hole on the slug-right side. These large slugs are 6-8 inches long. I've seen as many as three of them at a time along our road. The upper tentacles in front on the right are eyestalks with sensitive dark "pupils" and the lower pair are feelers and tasters. I've been happy to find these large slugs, a sign that our woods are coming back to life after a long drought. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2014)

It started raining while I was posting my photos tonight. This first storm is expected to bring less than an inch of rain, but there's another storm tomorrow night that might bring more. I'm so happy that we have real rain this year! The rain brings banana slugs, mushrooms, slime molds, ferns, liverworts, and more - all the things I've been showing in my photos lately. Bring it on!

File.Name: ec15b_ec15-001.jpg
Notes:
16031497132 This is looking closer into a cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree, see this photo. You can see a natural white spore print on the overlapping caps. If you look close, you can see the whitish maggots of Drosophilid Flies on the lower caps. The name "honey mushroom" describes the color and not the taste of these mushrooms. They are edible and delicious if can get to them before the flies do! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2014)

The mushroom caps aren't always this colorful. We have another cluster growing from a stump in the garden that is downright dull. Mushroom are individuals, like you and I.
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Drosophilid Flies

File.Name: ec15c_ec11-004.jpg
Notes:
15846026849 Going, going, gone!

Look close! I noticed this little insect trapped inside a fold of the resupinate Crust Fungus (Steraceae and allies) that's growing from the base of an old wine barrel planter in our garden. In a few days it was gone from view, completely engulfed by the fungus. The white balance changes from sunny to cloudy across these photos. I have no idea what kind of insect it is. Be careful what bed you lay in! (San Marcos Pass, 9-10-11 December 2014)

File.Name: ec15d_ec15-029.jpg
Notes: 16031320882 This is some kind of resupinate Crust Fungus (Steraceae and allies) that's growing in an old wine barrel planter in our garden. It's engulfing the bits of Madrone bark that has fallen in. See my [Next] photo to see what else it's engulfed! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2014)

File.Name: ec15e_ec15-013.jpg
Notes: 16006164766 Here are new fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) ferns in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing on a sandstone outcrop with moss and lichen. My mom used to call these Resurrection Ferns because they seem to appear from nowhere when the rains arrive. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)

File.Name: ec15f_ec15-017.jpg
Notes:
16005982716 Secret life in the woods...

Here's one more photo of the same Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) sporangia that I show in this photo and this one. It's a day later, and they are almost invisible now. I wouldn't have found them if I hadn't marked their place. They are on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Try flipping back and forth between all three photos. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. They look like tiny fungi, but something different comes from their spores. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2014)

It's interesting that these are gray and not blue like my previous photo. Maybe it's a white balance thing, but I don't think so.
