File.Name: ec16a_ec16-003.jpg
Notes:
16037997411 This is a small slug on the cap of the same red-capped Bolete mushroom in the woods as my [Previous] photo. It has a slime-tail, like an anchor line. It's only about 1/2 inch long, maybe 3/4 inch when extended. I don't know what kind of slug it is, but it sure looks like a miniature Banana Slug except for the color, complete with a ridged back. It was raining a bit when I got this photo. I came back after the rain ended, and the slug was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2014)

RJ, I hope you write that book on western slugs and snails!

We're in a lull between two storms as I type. We had a bit over 1/2 inch on rain last night, and we're expecting about the same tonight. These little rains are great for the mushrooms and other critters that the rain brings. We stand at 165% of normal rainfall, but everything now depends on what happens in January and February which are typically our wettest months. We've had wet autumns before that completely crapped out in the new year.

File.Name: ec16b_ec13-004.jpg
Notes:
15853732259 This is a small red-capped Bolete mushroom in the woods, maybe Boletus dryophilus or an uncracked Boletus chrysenteron? It has rather large blue-staining pores. This one appears to be resting on a new plant of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Lamiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2014)

It's been so long since we've had mushrooms in the woods with the drought, that I'm forgetting the names I used to know!
ScientificName: Boletus dryophilus; Boletus chrysenteron; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus; Boletus chrysenteron; Stachys bullata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: ec16c_ec16-019.jpg
Notes: 15419990633 I'm not sure what this rather large Cortinarius mushroom is, but it's definitely in the subgenus Bulbopodium of the genus Cortinarius with a non-viscid stem and a rimmed basal bulb. (This one appears to have an atypical non-viscid cap.) It has a light-colored cap with what appear to be white fragments of a universal veil on top. Maybe it's Cortinarius regalis? It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Other species are yellow or violet. Like all Cortinarius species, this one has reddish brown spore-stained fibrils on the stalk. In time, the cap and gills will be stained the same color. All old corts look the same, but they are quite pretty when young. Edibility is unknown. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2014)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Cortinarius regalis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Cortinarius regalis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ec16d_ec16-016.jpg
Notes:
16038800902 These mushrooms have "pink" or at least flesh-colored spores which have colored the gills, which places them with the Entolomas. They are growing on decaying oak wood. I first thought they are the Deer Mushroom or Fawn Mushroom (Pluteus cervinus), but now I think they are Pluteus petasatus. Both are in the Pluteaceae family of mushrooms. They are said to be edible and quite good when fresh and firm. See this photo and this photo from December 2012 in the same place. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2014)

(My own photos are becoming my best field guide!)
ScientificName: Entolomas; Pluteus cervinus; Pluteus petasatus; Pluteaceae
associatedTaxa: Entolomas; Pluteus cervinus; Pluteus petasatus; Pluteaceae

File.Name: ec16e_ec16-024.jpg
Notes: 15851951468 Woodland critters have been nibbling this hefty "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus eastwoodiae) mushroom that I found in the woods a couple of days ago. It was formerly considered Boletus satanas, e.g. in David Aroras' Mushrooms Demystified (1986), which is still my main mushroom book. These large mushrooms are blue-staining, and you can see a blue flush on the nibbled parts. See this photo and this one. This one is considered quite poisonous to you and me, but the critters seem to like it. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2014)

File.Name: ec16f_ec16-029.jpg
Notes: 16037377931 This is a "gelatinous blob" of Black Witch's Butter (Exidia glandulosa) in the Tremellales group of fungi. It's easy to miss this dark "mushroom" growing with lichens on a fallen oak branch. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2014)

File.Name: ec17a_ec17-004.jpg
Notes:
15860485559 This is looking under the cap of a tiny white-spored Mycena mushroom. I'm not a contortionist! This one was growing above our 4 foot high retaining wall which makes a perfect window into the woods. The orange colors on the gill and cap margins are exquisite. See my [Previous] two photos for more Mycenas. There are 1000s of these in the woods right now. You can hardly take a step without crushing them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2014)

We got another 1-1/4 inch of rain last night, more than expected. Now it sounds like we'll have dry weather until Christmas at least, which will give the mushrooms a chance to come up. Mushrooms are creatures of wet weather, but I don't think they like actual rain. They need dry weather to spread their spores, so a few days after a good rain is the time to find them.

File.Name: ec17b_ec17-008.jpg
Notes: 15860390079 This California Banana Slug (Ariolimax californicus, formerly included in A. columbianus) is tasting a little Mycena mushroom, see my [Previous] and [Next] photos. Slugs don't really have teeth, but they do have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help them munch leaves and mushrooms. This one didn't care for the taste and soon moved on. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2014)

File.Name: ec17c_ec17-017.jpg
Notes: 15860337869 This is a group of tiny white-spored Mycena mushrooms - the classic "LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom". They are small and fragile - quite ephemeral. These are growing from fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). There are 1000s of these in the woods right now. You can hardly take a step without crushing them. They are ubiquitous, but you do have to hunt for a nice group like this. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2014)

File.Name: ec17d_ec16-006.jpg
Notes: 15426652643 This is a neat cluster of Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) growing on sandstone in the woods. They are a fruticose lichen with a stalk, but they come from a foliose base. If you look close, you can see lots of new stalks coming up from the leafy base. Look for their orange tips. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2014)

File.Name: ec17e_ec15-028.jpg
Notes:
15858803858 Here is a troop of "Little Brown Mushrooms" - LBMs - fruiting on oak wood chips. They have rusty-brown spores and adnate gills. I believe they are Tubaria furfuracea or kin. My mushroom book describes them as the "quintessential LBM". The little mushrooms look robust here, but they dry out to almost nothing. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2014)

No LBMs with rusty spores should be eaten. They are hard to tell apart, and at least one of them - Galerina autumnalis - contains deadly amanita-toxins!

File.Name: ec17f_ec14-007.jpg
Notes: 15858770798 This is another large cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from an old stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in a tangle of Snowberry and Wild Blackberry. The name "honey mushroom" describes the color and not the taste of these mushrooms. They are edible and delicious if can get to them before the flies do! But it's too late for these - I can already see the whitish maggots of Drosophilid Flies on the lower caps. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Drosophilid Flies

File.Name: ec18a_ec18-003.jpg
Notes:
16053000232 This is a mass of fly maggots on all that's left of a stem of Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. I believe the maggots are the young of Drosophilid Flies (Drosophilidae, Diptera), possibly the fly in this photo. It's hard to get a sharper photo in the low light because the maggots are in constant motion. Try leaning back from your screen. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2014)

The NWS forecast was for sun today, but instead there was drizzle and light rain most of the day. I went to town in the morning to do some errands, and it was still drizzly when I got home. I got the maggot photos in the drizzle. There's a chance of light rain tomorrow night, mostly north of us. The way it's going this year, we might get some more rain!

File.Name: ec18b_ec18-004.jpg
Notes:
15866311800 Here are fly maggots on all that's left of once-beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. I believe the maggots are the young of Drosophilid Flies (Drosophilidae, Diptera), possibly the fly in this photo. The maggots are basically aquatic, and turn the mushrooms into liquid mush. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2014)

It's hard to get a sharper photo in the low light because the maggots are in constant motion. Try leaning back from your screen.

File.Name: ec18c_ec18-006.jpg
Notes:
16027709756 This is all that's left of the once-beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) that I showed in this photo and this photo last week. The mushrooms have become a slime mass of fly maggots. I believe the maggots are the young of Drosophilid Flies (Drosophilidae, Diptera), possibly the one in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2014)

Well, I guess it happens to all of us!

File.Name: ec18d_ec14-001.jpg
Notes:
16027625736 This another top-side photo of the same interesting Fly (Diptera) that I found last week perched on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). See this photo for a side view. I'm not sure, but I think it might be a Drosophilid Fly (Drosophilidae). If it is a drosophilid, it might be partially responsible for the maggots in the next few photos, about 20 feet away. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)

This photo has a clear view of the upper wings, so if you can ID it, please let me know. I used to understand fly wing venation 30 years ago, but those days are long past!
CommonNames: Fly; Coast Live Oak; Drosophilid Fly

File.Name: ec18e_ec14-005.jpg
Notes: 16027525256 This is a tiny Midge fly, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera with those plumose antennae. I guess it's a male. This one is perched for a moment on a twig in the woods. This tiny fly moved around constantly, and this was a lucky shot in our winter-dark woods without flash. It's interesting how it holds its wings forward. I wonder if it's one of the tiny midges the "dance in the sunbeams" - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2014)

File.Name: ec18f_ec07-017.jpg
Notes:
15430922914 These are tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on new urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I guess the thrips are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae - see Bug Guide. I find these critters every year on these flowers, and nowhere else. I watched them for a while - they are like little roosters in the chicken yard! They seem to spend most of their time chasing each other away, though you can also see a few collecting at the still-closed throats of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2014)

This is a catch-up photo from last week. It seemed to fit in today with insects (counting maggots!) in all my photos. Mushrooms have been on my mind!
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: ec19a_ec19-017.jpg
Notes:
16035027536 This is the first new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family that I've found this year, growing in our canyon. Leaves, petioles, and sepals are glandular, which separates it from the Canyon Gooseberry (R. californicum). I visited this plant two days ago and didn't notice the flowers, yet here they are today. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. The shrubs had a tough time in the third year of our drought, and some plants are dead or without flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2014)

It was mostly cloudy today, and it sure felt like more rain - but the radar map is clear tonight. No problem. We up to 12.8 inches of rain this rain year, 164% of normal. That's almost as much as we had in the last rain year from September to August. Of course it will have to continue to rain to keep that up. January and February are usually our wettest months. We'll see what happens.
ScientificName: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; R. californicum; en pointe; continue
associatedTaxa: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; R. californicum; en pointe; continue

File.Name: ec19b_ec19-041.jpg
Notes:
16034789936 This is another Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on fallen leaves of mostly Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. You have to be in the right place at the right time to notice these shy creatures! They look like little fungi, but the spores will become tiny microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists that live in decaying wood and humus. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2014)

They only last for a day or two. I think they might be my favorite creatures to find and photograph.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia

File.Name: ec19c_ec19-005.jpg
Notes: 15874384039 This is a "gelatinous blob" of Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica), a familiar Jelly Fungus in the Tremelliales. It's common from Oregon to Southern California (at least!), but it's nice to see after months of drought. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. These odd "mushrooms" appear after rain and then dry out into a hard yellow crust as they loses moisture. This one is growing with Bracket Fungi (Polyporaceae or Stereaceae) like they usually do, on a fallen branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It's easy to over-expose these fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2014)
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Jelly Fungus; Bracket Fungi; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Tremelliales; Polyporaceae; Stereaceae; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Tremelliales; Polyporaceae; Stereaceae; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: ec19d_ec19-010.jpg
Notes: 15872893258 I believe these are Sordid Waxy Cap (Hygrophorus sordidus) mushrooms. They are large but rather boring, with no interesting veils or smells or colors. They are said to be edible but not choice. Mushroom author David Arora describes them as "slugulose". The gills are soft, but they don't feel particularly waxy to me. These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I haven't seen them for a few years. The recent rains have been a blessing! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2014)
CommonNames: Sordid Waxy Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ec19e_ec19-013.jpg
Notes: 15872971000 It's always fun to try to find the smallest mushroom. This isn't it, but they are getting close. I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm ball. Some of the fungi are bigger, some smaller. They are growing on a pencil-thick twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I guess they are tiny Ascomycete Cup fungi in the Pezizaceae or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2014)

File.Name: ec19f_ec19-011.jpg
Notes: 16059513242 These are tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. You can see little explosions of mycelium from the base of the fruiting bodies, and maybe a couple of root-like rhizomophs. There are tiny buttons below the stalks on the right. I've been seeing a lot these lately. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2014)

File.Name: ec20a_ec20-035.jpg
Notes:
16065738351 Here are two photos of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Orobanchaceae plant family. The plants come up like mushrooms, fully formed, with bright red bracts. These aren't flowering yet, but it wont be long - see this photo and read the interesting comments. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. I'm surprised to find them so early in the rain year. I'm sure they will be the first non-shrub natives to flower this year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)

It was mostly cloudy today, and it sure felt like rain but it never happened. The wind has been kicking up a bit this evening, which reminds me of how calm the weather has been after the last few storms. I think that's unusual.
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: ec20b_ec20-028.jpg
Notes:
15881840717 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. The "ssp. mollis" refers to the long white hairs on the petioles and stems. It looks like some critter couldn't wait for the flowers to open, and drilled a hole into the flower on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)

Compare this photo with the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this photo. The stems and petioles of this one are very hairy and the leaves are not blue-green. This shrub has berries with several separate nutlets and grows from a burl, the other one has berries with one large nut and no burl. The shrubs are similar but different, and it's still easy to get confused!
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) in this photo. The stems and petioles of this one are very hairy and the leaves are not blue-green. This shrub has berries with several separate nutlets and grows from a burl
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: ec20c_ec20-001.jpg
Notes: 16041748756 I believe this is a tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushroom growing from fallen oak leaves. I included my pen as a ruler. My pen has a 0.7 mm ball, so the caps are only a few millimeters across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom I know that I actually have a name for. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)

File.Name: ec20d_ec20-005.jpg
Notes:
15881637747 Here are crustose lichens growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what they are, but I recognize the different apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Lichens are classified according to their fungal parts, so I reckon this is an important difference. I can see green, tan, red, and black (at the very bottom) apothecia , not to mention the yellow lichens. It's remarkable diversity. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)

Did you notice? In this and my [Previous] photos, I show crustose, foliose, and fruticose lichens.

File.Name: ec20e_ec20-016.jpg
Notes: 16041505756 This is a different Cladonia lichen growing in the woods. They are a fruticose lichen with a stalk, but they come from a foliose base. These are growing with mosses and manzanita leaves. If you look close, you can see that the mosses are covered with tiny raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)

File.Name: ec20f_ec20-019.jpg
Notes: 15879765188 I believe these are small Rock Tripe Lichens in the genus Umbilicaria growing on a sandstone rock face. They are considered a foliose lichen. They might be the Emery Rocktripe Lichen (Umbilicaria phaea), see this page. They are not common in our mountains, and I don't know anything else about them. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)
CommonNames: Rock Tripe Lichens; foliose lichen; Emery Rocktripe Lichen

File.Name: ec21a_ec21-013.jpg
Notes:
16050222806 I was surprised to find this new caterpillar of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly today, munching foliage of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The caterpillar is small, less than 1/2 inch long. I seems too early for them, but maybe it's a good sign for the butterflies next summer? Or maybe it's just confused by the relatively warm weather we've been having lately? (We still haven't had frost.) (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2014)

I usually find these caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. They are said to prefer "scrophs" in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. The family has been split up, but I wonder if the butterflies know something that the botanists have missed?

The winds held off today, but they are cranking up tonight. We're under a Wind Advisory tonight.

I figure that the Winter Solstice is the real start of the new year, despite what the calendar says. The moment of the solstice happened this afternoon at 3:03 PM PST. This is the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a hair longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. Happy Solstice everyone!
Locality: Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. They are said to prefer "scrophs" in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. The family has been split up
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Climbing Penstemon
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: ec21b_ec21-025.jpg
Notes:
15456243743 These are little galls on a leaf of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I don't expect to find galls on this shrub, but here they are. I believe they are galls of the Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. Usually the tiny tubes form under the leaves, but that one gall to the left of center is on top. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2014)

I haven't forgotten about galls, though I've been distracted by the mushrooms lately!
CommonNames: Black Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia mellifera; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: ec21c_ec21-008.jpg
Notes: 15889692699 This is a handsome little Agaricus mushroom in the woods. Like all agaricus mushrooms, it has pink (soon to turn brown) gills, a ring on the stem, and no "death cup" volva" at the base. I don't know which this one is, though I've found quite a few of them in the woods lately. This one doesn't stain yellow in any part. It has a sweet taste like almond extract or anise that is vaguely reminiscent of the phenolic taste of some of the "bad agaricus" mushrooms in our woods. It may well be edible, but I don't trust any of these agaricus species in the woods. The local woodland Agaricus species are a pretty risky group in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2014)

File.Name: ec21d_ec21-001.jpg
Notes: 15888255888 I guess these are little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing in the mint patch where I got so many photos late last summer. They are larger than our common Mycena mushrooms in this photo, but they are still "little brown mushrooms". The one I picked has a large clump of mycelium at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2014)

File.Name: ec21e_ec21-004.jpg
Notes: 15888289660 I believe the are Crepidotus mollis mushrooms growing from a cut log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in a woodpile. They start out white and turn kind of dingy brown as the mature. You can see some of the bluish mycelium in this photo. I turned the log up to get this photo and save my back. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2014)

File.Name: ec21f_ec20-032.jpg
Notes: 16074810522 This is a little forest of Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from the woodland mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. They are pretty common in the dark parts of the woods where the sun never shines, but you have to get down on your hands and knees to find them. A troop like this is not so common. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2014)

File.Name: ec22a_ec22-016.jpg
Notes:
16082803621 This is a tiny Midge fly, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera with those plumose antennae. I guess it's a male. This one appears to be floating, but I reckon it's caught up in a spider web. I found this on a spiny leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It was still alive, moving between my photos. There were several of them caught on the same leaf. I wonder if it's one of the tiny midges the "dance in the sunbeams" - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2014)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. This critter is about 500 pixels long. That makes it about 2.2 mm long. (See this photo for how I figured.)

It was noticeably warmer today, and still windy. The small ephemeral mushrooms are folding, and I reckon this is the end of our mushroom season for at least a week. There's still lots of moisture in the ground, but the surface is drying out. I hope this isn't a repeat of the 2011-2012 rain season when the rains suddenly stopped and our drought began.
Locality:
See this photo for how I figured.)

It was noticeably warmer today
CommonNames: Midge; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: ec22b_ec22-013.jpg
Notes: 15898526979 Here are new flower catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family just starting to flower. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This one is male and staminate. They never did open last year in the drought. The flowers are lovely when they get going, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2014)

File.Name: ec22c_ec22-009.jpg
Notes: 16058746616 Here is a patchwork quilt of crustose lichens on a sandstone rock along my trail today, growing in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. I reckon you could spend some time studying this one rock! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2014)

File.Name: ec22d_ec22-007.jpg
Notes: 15898370169 This is a handsome Agaricus mushroom in the woods shedding her partial veil which covers and protects the new gills. Some mushrooms like Amanitas also have a universal veil which covers the whole mushroom like an egg shell. I'm pretty sure this is the same kind of mushroom I showed yesterday in this photo. This one does seem to have yellow stains around the cap margin, so maybe it's something different? I don't trust any of these agaricus species in the woods. The local woodland Agaricus species are a pretty risky group in my experience. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2014)
ScientificName: Agaricus; partial veil; Amanitas; universal veil
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; partial veil; Amanitas; universal veil

File.Name: ec22e_ec22-011.jpg
Notes: 16084339495 Here is a troop of dark-spored Psathyrella mushrooms - maybe the Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophila or kin). This whole group is probably growing from the dead oak wood in the foreground. I picked a couple of the mushrooms for the photo, and they sure look like they have pallid gills, but see my [Previous] photo which slows dark-spore-stained gills. The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. You can see a few fresh-looking dark caps closer to the front. As mushrooms go, they are kind of weedy, and these are growing with mostly weeds. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2014)

File.Name: ec22f_ec22-030.jpg
Notes: 15897003490 I believe these are dark-spored Psathyrella mushrooms - maybe the Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophila or kin), though this was a small cluster. See my [Next] photo for a more typical group. You can see the dark spores on the gills of the upper mushroom with the split cap. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2014)

File.Name: ec23a_ec23-027.jpg
Notes:
15906173569 These look like the real Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin), but I'm not sure about the ones in my [Previous] photo. The lichens look like something you'd see in the ocean. They are growing on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. I don't know why this evergreen shrub attracts lichens, but it sure does. I guess the orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between the lichenized fungus and algae. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 December 2014)

It was unseasonably warm today, mostly without the winds we had yesterday. I didn't expect to find mushrooms today, but I did. I wish I knew what they all are!
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia

File.Name: ec23b_ec23-026.jpg
Notes: 15904642228 These are lovely little "fruticose" lichens growing on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. I don't know why this evergreen shrub attracts lichens, but it sure does. This might be an older version of the same Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) as my [Next] photo, but I'm really not sure. I guess the orange cups are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. I'm not sure if there is any connection with the gray foliose lichens, except for proximity. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 December 2014)
ScientificName: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; apothecia

File.Name: ec23c_ec23-019.jpg
Notes:
15904621370 These are another "mystery mushroom" to me, growing on well-rotted wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I can see a hint of pinkish spore prints on the lower caps, but the gills look brown. Maybe a Pluteus? I don't know what these mushrooms are, but they are attractive. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 December 2014)

Darn, I have the vague feeling that I've seen these mushrooms before, but I can't find the photo. We haven't had many mushrooms for the past three years because of the drought, and I've forgotten the names!

File.Name: ec23d_ec23-012.jpg
Notes: 16091750715 These are brown Agaricus mushrooms with dark blackish-brown spores staining the gills. I guess they are the "California Agaricus" (Agaricus californicus) mushroom, which is mildly poisonous to most people. I found these today growing with the weeds near the Lake Cachuma rest stop. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 December 2014)

File.Name: ec23e_ec23-004a.jpg
Notes: 15905543499 This is a "mystery mushroom" to me. If you look close at the upper stem on the right you make out rusty-brown fibrils on the stalk , which pegs it as a Cortinarius. My second thought is a pink-spored Entoloma, though I don't recognize the colors. Whatever it is, it's a fairly large and attractive mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2014)

File.Name: ec23f_ec23-001.jpg
Notes:
16065713956 This mushroom has a "fungus infection". The "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus has taken over a Bolete (genus Boletus) mushroom, like mold on a piece of bread. Maybe the host mushroom is Boletus dryophilus, but it's beyond knowing. The infection starts as a moldy-looking growth, then turns yellow and powdery. It smells like a diaper pail. Needless to say, it's not edible even if the host mushroom is. It's associated with decay. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2014)

Curiously, the "Lobster Mushroom" (Hypomyces lacifluorum) that we find in the Oregon woods is a related fungus that grows over Lactarius or Russula mushrooms, and is highly rated as an edible. See this photo. Edible yes, but not my favorite.
Locality: Hypomyces lacifluorum) that we find in the Oregon woods is a related fungus that grows over Lactarius or Russula mushrooms
ScientificName: Hypomyces chrysospermum; Boletus; Boletus dryophilus; Hypomyces lacifluorum; Lactarius; Russula
associatedTaxa: Hypomyces chrysospermum; Boletus; Boletus dryophilus; Hypomyces lacifluorum; Lactarius; Russula

File.Name: ec24a_ac24-025.jpg
Notes:
15477194584 My Christmas photo features Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata) on top of a beautiful Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family in the woods, with ripe red berries. They are enjoying the winter bounty of nature. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2010)

This is from the archives, but it's one of my very favorite Christmas photos. I got this photo exactly four years ago, on Christmas Eve, 2010. These are wary native woodland pigeons, not common city pigeons. A flock of these native birds still winters in our canyon in the mountains, though I haven't seen this many berries on the Madrones for many drought years. This photo reminds me of my Mom's old Christmas cards. Start here (at Fotolog) and keep clicking [Next].

I got all my [Previous] photos today on a hike up to the old Kinevan apple orchard. I'm kind of proud that there's a native plant, an insect, a slime mold, a mushroom, and a bunch of lichens! This diversity of life on the day before Christmas is my present this year. Thank you!

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: ec24b_ec24-021.jpg
Notes: 15911997968 These are beautiful multiple trunks of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. It's my favorite native tree for sure, and it has Christmas colors! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

File.Name: ec24c_ec24-048.jpg
Notes: 15479644683 I'm pretty sure this beautiful creature is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. It's our usual winter dragonfly. This one is perched on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) near the Kinevan frog pond. It kept coming back to the same perch, so it was just a matter of waiting... The pond is full, and I saw some recent egg masses, but I didn't see any frogs. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ec24d_ec24-009.jpg
Notes:
16098414312 This is a clump of tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a dead log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I believe they are Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa in the family Ceratiomyxales. They look like corals under the ocean. There's a tiny Cup Fungus (Pezizaceae) on the left side. I found them in a hollow of the log, which may have protected them from the dry weather we've had for the last few days. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

These creatures really are tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. The fingers of this slime mold are about 120 pixels long. That makes it about 0.48 mm long. See this photo for how I figured, and see this photo for more info about slime molds. They might be my favorite creatures!
CommonNames: Slime Molds; Coast Live Oak; Cup Fungus

File.Name: ec24e_ec24-053.jpg
Notes: 16097144721 Here is a cluster of dark-spored Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood in the woods. Sometimes the mushrooms are brighter yellow, see this photo from 2013. The mushrooms are said to be poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I wish all poisonous mushrooms were so obliging! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

File.Name: ec24f_ec24-045.jpg
Notes: 16098240162 Here is a mass of lichens on bare stems of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae) today. I have no idea how many different kinds of lichen are actually in the photo! It sure looks like there is great diversity. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

File.Name: ec25a_ec24-006.jpg
Notes:
16081594866 I think this is something remarkable. It's a tiny Midge fly, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera, I guess a female. But what's remarkable is those long spikes on its abdomen which made me think at first it was a mayfly. Even more remarkable is that snaky white "thing" coming out of its butt. I'm pretty sure this fly has succumbed to a Cordyceps fungus which has filled its body with fungal mycelium. I found this fly on a leaf at the top of a small Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus, Rosaceae) plant, and I've read that these Cordyceps fungi often cause their infected insect host to climb or fly to the highest point it can reach, all the better to spread its spores. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

I didn't notice the fungus until last night when I got the photo on my computer screen. I tried to get a better photo of the fungus today, but it was quite windy, and I didn't like my photos. Maybe tomorrow, if it's still there.

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. This midge is about 450 pixels long, though it's curled up a bit. That makes it about 1.8 mm long. The fungus on its butt is only 150 pixels long, which make it 0.7 mm long. (See this photo for how I figured.)

This is also a pretty interesting "butt shot". HBBBT!
ScientificName: Chironomidae; Diptera; Cordyceps; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Chironomidae; Diptera; Cordyceps; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: ec25b_ec25-001.jpg
Notes:
15484909004 This is a small Bolete mushroom in the garden, maybe Boletus dryophilus or B. truncatus etc. It has a brown velvety cap and fairly small pores and a stem that is red on top rather than the bottom. As far as I know, these small boletes are all "edible" but not very good. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2014)

Darn, it's been so long since we've had mushrooms in the woods with the drought, that I'm forgetting the names I used to know!

File.Name: ec25c_ec25-027.jpg
Notes: 15919801380 This is a large "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus eastwoodiae aka Boletus satanas) mushroom. I put my 3-5/8 inch pocket knife (and digging tool) in the photo for scale. I didn't want to dig this one up, but they have a voluptuous stem, red pores instead of gills, and a red-staining cap. See this photo. They are quite poisonous. Don't eat them - but you have to admire them! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

File.Name: ec25d_ec24-037.jpg
Notes:
15484661924 I believe these are the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms growing close to a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the Kinevan orchard. These mushrooms have yellowish caps with some white volval remnants on top, a partial-veil ring on the stem, and a saclike volva at the base - but both the ring and the volva are a bit hard to see in this photo. That long stem was deeply rooted! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

This mushroom is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. It's one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous! There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods, but I suppose tiny mushroom spores can travel around the planet.

File.Name: ec25e_ec24-035.jpg
Notes:
16081058436 I believe these are the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms growing close to a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the Kinevan orchard. The upper cap does have a slightly striate cap which is not typical of this species, but this may be the result of our recent warm weather. These mushrooms have yellowish caps with some white volval remnants on top, a partial-veil ring on the stem, and a saclike volva at the base. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

This mushroom is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. It's one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous! There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods, but I suppose tiny mushroom spores can travel around the planet.

File.Name: ec25f_ec24-001.jpg
Notes: 15920711579 I believe these are Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms in sandy soil on the edge of our garden, not far from Coast Live Oak. They don't seen to have any latex when you slit the gills. (My book says the latex is "white or watery white, unchanging, often scanty or even absent.") They don't have the maple-syrup smell of Lactarius fragilis, though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2014)

File.Name: ec26a_ec26-017.jpg
Notes:
15929899609 This is the little Cricket (Gryllidea, Orthoptera) that appeared on the sandy cap of the massive Cortinarius mushroom in my [Previous] two photos. I've been through the photos at BugGuide, and I still don't know what kind of cricket it is. It's antennae go off to the left for some ways, and it sure has muscular hind legs! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014)

Thanks to Alice Abelia, I believe this is a Camel Cricket (genus Pristoceuthophilus, family Rhaphidophoridae) - maybe Pristoceuthophilus celatus.

It feels dry, but there are still mushrooms in the ground, mostly hiding under "mushrumps" - those mounds of leaves and woodland detritus that the mushroom pushes up. Gophers make these too, so not every mound is a mushroom! he air feels dry, but there is still plenty of moisture in the ground. There's a chance for more rain next week.

It also feels cold tonight! There's a Freeze Warning for the Santa Ynez Valley that may get to us too. This might be our first frost of the year. Usually we have frost in November, but our storms this year have mostly been warm.
ScientificName: Gryllidea; Orthoptera; Cortinarius; Pristoceuthophilus; Rhaphidophoridae; Pristoceuthophilus celatus
associatedTaxa: Gryllidea; Orthoptera; Cortinarius; Pristoceuthophilus; Rhaphidophoridae; Pristoceuthophilus celatus

File.Name: ec26b_ec26-019.jpg
Notes:
16090036526 This is another photo of the cap of the massive Cortinarius mushroom in my [Previous] photo, with the Cricket (Gryllidea, Orthoptera) that appeared in my [Next] photo. I lifted the mushroom up and put it back. I don't know where the cricket came from! You can see that the mushroom has white "universal veil" material on its cap. I've been through the photos at BugGuide, and I still don't know what kind of cricket it is. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014)

Always "replace divots" - put every mushroom you pick back in the same hole you got it from, and pat it down and cover it up like you found it. It might well re-attach to the mycelium. It's a matter of etiquette, and it's a courtesy to other mushroom collectors who might follow you!

File.Name: ec26c_ec26-015.jpg
Notes:
15495939623 This is a massive Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today, with my 3-5/8 inch pocket knife (and digging tool) in the photo for scale. I don't know what kind it is. There are many different kinds of these mushrooms. This one does not have a bulbous base, but it seems to have a viscid cap covered with sand. I guess it's subgenus Phlegmacium. Maybe it's the same as this one? See my [Next] photo for a look at the top. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014)

The weather has been dry since our last rains, but there are still mushrooms in the woods. They are mostly hiding under the oak leaves where there is still some moisture. Look for the "mushrumps" - those mounds of leaves and woodland detritus that the mushroom pushes up. Gophers make these too, so not every mound is a mushroom!

File.Name: ec26d_ec26-023.jpg
Notes:
15928042258 I believe this is a Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) mushroom growing from a fallen oak branch in the woods. It has a toothy grin! It appears to be a gilled mushroom, but it's actually a polypore in the fungal order Polyporales. if you look close, you can spot a tiny Midge fly (Chironomidae, Diptera) on the cap and a fat Springtail (Collembola) on the "gills" on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014)

Maybe this is an example of convergent evolution - a polypore fungus that developed its gills independently of the rest of the Agaricales mushrooms?
ScientificName: Lenzites betulina; Polyporales; Chironomidae; Diptera; Collembola; Agaricales
associatedTaxa: Lenzites betulina; Polyporales; Chironomidae; Diptera; Collembola; Agaricales

File.Name: ec26e_ec26-035.jpg
Notes: 15929624067 This is a new caterpillar of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in a little boat-like seedpod of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). The caterpillar is small, less than 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Phrymaceae

File.Name: ec26f_ec25-005.jpg
Notes:
15927782258 This is another photo of the remarkable (I think) Cordyceps fungus that has brought down this tiny Midge fly, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera. See my [Previous] photo for more info. That snaky white "thing" coming out of its butt is the fruiting body of the fungus, and I don't have a clue white those white spikes are. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2014)

I got this photo yesterday and hoped for a better photo today - but the wind last night blew the fly away, so this is all there is. The fly is only about 1.8 mm long and the fungus is only 0.7 mm long. Again, see my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: ec27a_ec27-016.jpg
Notes:
15938722847 These are wonderful new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. This looks like a studio shot, but it's just another photo in the woods. The shrubs don't all open their flowers at the same time like some other natives, but spread them out from autumn until spring, September until March. That's good for the pollinators and photographers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2014)

It was cold this morning, but it didn't quite freeze. I wanted to get out of our ice-box canyon on the north-facing side of the mountains, so I drove up Camino Cielo looking for sunlight and flowers...

File.Name: ec27b_ec27-004.jpg
Notes: 15938344389 Here are new flower buds of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It won't be too long until they open! This particular species of Ceanothus is unique because it has clusters of white flowers and alternate leaves, which you can clearly see in my photo if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2014)

File.Name: ec27c_ec27-005.jpg
Notes:
15937000840 On the left is a new sprout of native Fremont's Star Lily (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus), and on the right is a new sprout of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum). Both used to be placed in the Liliaceae plant family, but now the plant on the left is moved to the Melanthiaceae plant family, and the plant on the right is considered a Agavaceae. Those darned botanists! They look quite distinct side by side like this, but it's easy to confuse them in the woods when they grow together. The one difference I count on is that the Amole has wavy leaves and the Star Lily's are quite straight. (San Marcos Pass, 27 and 22 December 2014)

I got the photo on the left today along West Camino Cielo, and the photo of the right a few days ago near the Painted Cave community.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Soap Plant; Amole
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Melanthiaceae; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Melanthiaceae; Agavaceae

File.Name: ec27d_ec27-022.jpg
Notes: 16098336426 I found these different Agaricus mushrooms in the woods today. They have almost white gills (that will turn brown), a skirt-like ring with patches on the underside, yellow stains, and a taste that starts sweet and anisy but soon turns to unpleasant phenol. (I spit for some time after nibbling a piece.) I'm pretty sure they are the Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus), but the bottom of the stem did NOT stain bright yellow. Well, they were growing together and I broke the mushroom on the right away, so maybe I missed the very base of the stem? Handsome, but poisonous and yucky. I don't trust any of our woodland Agaricus species. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2014)

File.Name: ec27e_ec27-002.jpg
Notes:
16098250426 This is another small Bolete mushroom in the woods, maybe Boletus dryophilus or B. truncatus etc. It has yellow pores that stain blue and a stem that is yellow on top and red lower down. As far as I know, these small boletes are all "edible" but not very good. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2014)

Darn, it's been so long since we've had mushrooms in the woods with the drought, that I'm forgetting the names I used to know!

File.Name: ec27f_ec26-011.jpg
Notes: 15936543128 It's been dry lately, but there is still moisture under the oak leaves in places where the sun seldom shines at this time of year. The mushrooms are hiding under the oak leaves where there is moisture to ripen their spores. The trick to finding them is to look for "mushrumps" like this - that tell-tale mound of leaves and woodland detritus that the mushrooms push up. Sometimes they are just gopher mounds, but other times they hold surprising new mushrooms. This mushrump held the massive Cortinarius mushroom in this photo yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2014)

File.Name: ec28a_ec28-022.jpg
Notes:
16131789251 This is the first flowering plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Orobanchaceae plant family that I've seen this year. It's growing in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year, so please excuse the funky white balance. This is the first real wildflower that's not a shrub that I've found in this rain year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, surely Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. Apparently, the plants form haustoria - like mistletoe - to connect to their host species. See this photo and read the interesting comments.

It's been cold, but not quite cold enough for ice. There has been frost in the Santa Ynez Valley just down the hill, but not here in the mountains. The woods feel dry. There is a small chance of rain next Tuesday and Wednesday.
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; haustoria

File.Name: ec28b_ec28-016.jpg
Notes: 15513922303 Here are small plants of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Orobanchaceae plant family, with my 3-5/8 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. The plants come up like mushrooms, fully formed, with bright red bracts. These aren't flowering yet, but see my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: ec28c_ec28-010.jpg
Notes: 16131590711 This is a golden Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae, Diptera) perched for just a moment on a fallen oak leaf on this cold morning. I've been seeing a few of these flies among the leaves of the oak trees from our upstairs porch. I assume they are out for a reason, and I I wonder what prey they're after in this cold time of year? (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: ec28d_ec28-006.jpg
Notes: 15945961758 I believe this is a Flesh Fly (Sarcophagidae, Diptera) catching some sun on a fallen oak leaf this cold morning. It has reddish eyes and a red-tipped butt. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: ec28e_ec28-026.jpg
Notes: 15510988944 I believe this is a Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushroom growing in leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) today. You can see the start of the yellowish spore mass pushing out of the apical pore or tear. This puffball has a well-developed sterile base, giving it a shape like a light bulb. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: ec28f_ec28-025.jpg
Notes: 16132373842 Here are mosses and Cladonia lichens growing in the woods, under Manzania in a shady place. I believe the lichens are mostly Cladonia furcata though there are also a few Pyxie Cups in the mix. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: ec29a_ec29-003.jpg
Notes:
16117664336 I believe this is a tiny humpbacked Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae of the order Diptera. You can see spines on the tibiae of its remarkably long legs. It's perched oh-so-delicately on the rib of a fallen apple leaf. But I might (always) be wrong, especially with these tiny flies! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2014)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. The fly is about 580 pixels long. That makes it about 2.3 mm long, less than 1/10 inch. See this photo for how I figured.

The NWS weather forecast is moving from a slight chance of rain tomorrow to colder and windy. Darn, we really need some rain right now to keep the mushrooms going.

File.Name: ec29b_ec29-030.jpg
Notes:
15957219449 Here are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The breeze picked up as I was trying to get this photo, so you'll have to wait for a closer shot. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 December 2014)

We have lots of Ribes species in our mountains. This is the third one I've seen in flower, after Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) and Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum), see this photo and this one.
CommonNames: Canyon Gooseberry; Hillside Gooseberry; Chaparral Currant; Bitter Gooseberry

associatedTaxa: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae; Ribes; Ribes malvaceum; Ribes amarum

File.Name: ec29c_ec29-010.jpg
Notes: 16142453682 These are new flower heads of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family. I love the soft, woolly foliage. There aren't any flowers yet, just the red flower-like bracts. It's interesting that plants nearby started flowering as early as November 16 this year, see this photo. After that initial burst, the plants stalled a bit. They're just starting to show signs of flowering again. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2014)

File.Name: ec29d_ec29-022.jpg
Notes: 16143148575 Here are more beautiful flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Most flower buds are white, but these are really rosy. This is the stuff that cultivars are made of. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 December 2014)

File.Name: ec29e_ec29-031.jpg
Notes: 15956902039 Here are a look-alike pair of native grasses in the Poacea plant family. I believe the one on the left is Purple Needlegrass (Stipa pulchra aka Nasella pulchra) from Painted Cave in the mountains today, and the one one the right is Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) from Lake Cachuma a week ago. They look very similar when they're not in flower. Both look half dead - but also half alive! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2014)
ScientificName: Poacea; Stipa pulchra; Nasella pulchra; Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua
associatedTaxa: Poacea; Stipa pulchra; Nasella pulchra; Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua

File.Name: ec29f_ec28-009.jpg
Notes: 16142161822 This is just a fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a fallen oak leaf yesterday morning. It's probably one of those It's probably just another Muscidae flies, though this one seems more substantial than most. I dig the glint on its wings and the seemingly random pattern of black spots on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2014)

File.Name: ec30a_ec30-017.jpg
Notes:
16126401816 These are handsome yellow Cortinarius mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees. They have a bulb at the base which places them in subgenus Bulbopodium. They also show the rusty-red remnants of the "cortina" hairs which originally form a veil that protects the gills. I call all these yellow-orange cortinarius mushroom "Cortinarius fulmineus", but I believe that name is just a placeholder for whatever the mushrooms really are. Edibility is unknown. The problem is that all these cortinarius mushrooms end up looking alike. If you care to experiment, please let me know! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2014)

Did you notice that little Beetle (Coleoptera) under the cap on the right?

Did you notice that little Beetle (Coleoptera) under the cap on the right? I was feeling under the weather today, with a cold or flu coming on. I wasn't feeling very sharp with my camera, but I did take an easy walk through our canyon. I really didn't expect to find anything, but darned if I didn't find new mushrooms hiding under the oak leaves!

We actually had a bit of slushy snow tonight! It's been cold all afternoon, and it's expected to drop into the 20s tonight, first frost for sure.
Locality: Coleoptera) under the cap on the right? I was feeling under the weather today
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius fulmineus; Coleoptera; Coleoptera
