File.Name: ec30b_ec30-005.jpg
Notes:
16151268612 This is the same massive Cortinarius mushroom that I show in this photo with my knife for scale. It's catching a bit of morning sunlight, showing the rusty-red gills and cortina hairs on the stem that are stained by the spores. I think it might well be the same Cortinarius regalis mushroom as my [Previous] photo, subgenus Bulbopodium rather than Phlegmacium. But compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2014)

I "replanted" this mushroom after getting my earlier photo a week ago, and it seems to have taken. Always replant mushrooms that you pick but don't collect for the table. Put them back in the ground and pat them down. That gives them a chance to reconnect with their mycelium and keep on maturing spores like this one, and it's a matter of etiquette for other mushroom collectors who might follow.

File.Name: ec30c_ec30-028.jpg
Notes: 16149917361 I found this large Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today, hiding so deep in a mushrump that it was hard to dig out. It has a fat stalk and what might be a deformed bulbous base. Maybe it's Cortinarius regalis? This one is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). This might be the same mushroom as this photo, differences in size and white-balance notwithstanding. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2014)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Cortinarius regalis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Cortinarius regalis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ec30d_ec30-021.jpg
Notes:
15965565549 I found this handsome Russula mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's the first one I've seen this year. It has rosy colors and a mild non-acrid taste when nibbled. We have lots of species in our woods, so I won't try to guess which one this is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2014)

Well, we might have many species if the rain continues, judging from the past. That's still an open question.

File.Name: ec30e_ec30-004.jpg
Notes:
15964044998 Here are a conjoined pair of handsome Agaricus mushroom in the woods. These have almost white gills at first (right), but they turn brown after a while (left). Like all agaricus mushrooms, they have at least fragments of a partial-veil ring on the stem, and no "death cup" volva" at the base. They have thick root-like rhizomophs at the base. I don't know which this one is, though I've found quite a few of them in the woods lately. This one has yellow stains, but it doesn't particularly stain yellow when handled. See this photo and this one. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2014)

This mushroom has a sweet taste like almond extract or anise that is vaguely reminiscent of the phenolic taste of some of the "bad agaricus" mushrooms in our woods. It may well be edible, but I don't trust any of these agaricus species in the woods. The local woodland Agaricus species are a pretty risky group in my experience.

File.Name: ec30f_ec30-025.jpg
Notes: 16125616176 I guess this is a cluster of different little Mycena mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) in the woods today. They are literally folding from the dry weather we've been having lately. These are different from our usual Mycenas, growing in a definite cluster. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2014)

File.Name: ec31a_ec31-021.jpg
Notes:
15538557414 This is another photo of the male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) in my [Previous] photo, taken a moment later. It's taking wing from a flowering shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's remarkable how their iridescent throat feathers turn from black to red in a moment depending on the angle of the light. I was hoping to get a photo of the hummers working the flowers, but they are fast and camera shy. The hummers seem to claim these flowering Manzanita shrubs. They seem to spend most of their time chasing other invading hummers away! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2014)

It was cold today and I've come down with a cold or flu, so I went to a sunny place with flowers and just waited for a while. It worked. We did get a bit of slushy snow/ graupel last night, but we only ended up with 0.05 inches in the rain gauge.

I believe the real new year started a week ago on the solstice, and I've always been curious why the new year begins on January 1 in our calendar. Happy New Year!

File.Name: ec31b_ec31-015.jpg
Notes:
15975012387 Look close - the hummer's eyes are shut!

This is a small male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) catching a quick nap in a flowering shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae). It's remarkable how their iridescent throat feathers turn from black to red in a moment, see my [Next] photo, see my [Next] photo of the same bird a moment later. I was hoping to get a photo of the hummers working the flowers, but they are fast and camera shy. The hummers seem to claim these flowering Manzanita shrubs. They seem to spend most of their time chasing other invading hummers away! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2014)

File.Name: ec31c_ec31-034.jpg
Notes: 15974799887 This is looking closer at staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. You can clearly see the densely hairy lower surface of the leaves. See my [Previous] photo. The "string of pearls" flower catkins are strange and beautiful! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2014)

File.Name: ec31d_ec31-030.jpg
Notes: 16134722136 This is a native shrub of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family in full flower. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This one is male and staminate. The "string of pearls" flower catkins are strange and beautiful! See my [Next] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2014)

File.Name: ec31e_ec31-048.jpg
Notes:
15974679097 These are the first new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Last year (last "rain year" anyway) I found them on January 7, so these are early but not crazy. It's (almost) a new year! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2014)

We have several different species of Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains. This one is distinctive for its white flowers and mostly alternate leaves. In a good year, the shrub that covers the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow. The individual flowers are intricate, like little origami boxes. It was windy when I got this photo. I'll get better photos next year, which starts tomorrow!

File.Name: ec31f_ec31-028.jpg
Notes: 15972854788 This is a small Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae - growing in the "snow" near the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro, near the FAA tower. The snow may be "graupel" but who cares! We had a bit of slushy snow/graupel at home too. It's cold! It hasn't been out of the 30s all day on our winter-shaded porch. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 December 2014)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: d111a_d110-066.jpg
Notes:
8372458686 This glorious sunset greeted us home on our train trip yesterday. Yes, it really was this orange. You can see some "God light" - aka "crepuscular rays". I have an old stumper about these wonderful light beams. That's a off-shore oil rig on the horizon. I got this photo just south of Point Arguello through the window of our the moving train on our way home from Portland, Oregon yesterday. (Point Arguello, 10 January 2013)

We got home last night to serious cold - it was 24 degrees on the porch when I finally got up this morning. I took a short walk today,and didn't find much. I hope this new year won't be a repeat of last year with good early rain, then nothing in January and February which should be our wettest months. Our rainfall has dropped a bit below 100% of average .

File.Name: d111b_d101-013.jpg
Notes:
8372398618 These are untrod waves on what used to be the Bixby Ranch, just south of Point Conception. That's Cojo Point up close and Government Point behind it. It's private. These are legendary surfing spots. The only way to access these waves is to trespass by foot or come by boat from Jalama to the north or Gaviota from the south. It's hazardous - there's a beached sail boat on the closer point. At least you can look at the waves from the Coast Starlight train! I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland, Oregon last week. (Point Conception, 1 January 2013)

Apparently the 25,500 acre Bixby Ranch was sold to an investment company in Boston for $136 million. I don't know what's happening with the property now. This should be a National Seashore - but public access would change it for the worse.

File.Name: d111c_d101-022.jpg
Notes: 8371302767 This is looking back at mighty waves breaking across the bay at remote Jalama Beach, with Point Conception in the background. If you look close, you can see some development at Jalama on the left and the lighthouse at Point Conception on the right. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland, Oregon last week. (Jalama Beach, 1 January 2013)

File.Name: d111d_d101-044.jpg
Notes: 8372262690 Here are a couple of the unusual and surprising "Dune Lakes" that lie behind the sand dunes between Oceano and the northern edge of Nipomo Mesa. There are interesting native plants out there! Oso Flaco Lake to the south is public, but I guess these lakes are private. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland, Oregon last week. (Oceano, San Luis Obispo County, California, 1 January 2013)

File.Name: d111e_d110-019.jpg
Notes: 8372237432 The Coast Starlight train goes right through the middle of the Elkhorn Slough in Monterey Bay. Here is a small part of it, with gulls and the Moss Landing Power Plant in the background. Julie and I want to come kayaking here. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way home from Portland yesterday. (Elkhorn Slough, California, 10 January 2013)

File.Name: d111f_d110-004.jpg
Notes:
8371127387 I don't like graffiti in the woods, but it seems to belong along railroad tracks. We saw this amazing wall on a building in Oakland, California on our ride home on the Coast Starlight train yesterday. It's at least 20 feet long. I think I can make out letters in there, but I have no idea what it spells. Look at the fingers holding up the letters! This is art. (Oakland, California, 10 January 2013)

I reckon Oakland has the best graffiti I've seen. Fortunately, our train was stalled for a moment in front of this one.

File.Name: d111g_d102-007.jpg
Notes:
8372102464 This is looking across frozen Klamath Lake towards Mount McLoughlin in the distance. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland last week, which is why nothing is sharp in the foreground. (Klamath Lake, Oregon, 2 January 2013)

Mount McLoughlin is the southernmost of the great volcanic peaks in the Oregon Cascades. The Coast Starlight train route lies west of the mountains in California, but crosses to the east side north of Mount Shasta. It crosses the cascades between Chemult and Eugene. The nearby city of Klamath Falls is surely the coldest stop on the train route. The train pulled in at about 8:00 am and the tempurature was only about 6∞F!

File.Name: d111h_d102-021.jpg
Notes:
8372031234 This is looking over the lip of Willamette Falls on the Willamette River near Portland, Oregon. It only falls about 40 feet, but according to Wikipedia it's the largest waterfall in the Pacific Northwest by water volume, second largest in the United States behind Niagara Falls, and the eighteenth largest in the world. I would love to explore that old mill with my camera, but I reckon it's off-limits. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland last week. (Portland, Oregon, 2 January 2013)

Wikipedia reports that the original power plant, built in 1888 on the near side of the river, sent power 14 miles to Portland, becoming the United States' first long distance transmission of electrical energy.

File.Name: d112a_d112-004.jpg
Notes:
8374544173 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking at me over the edge of a leaf of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. What wonderful eyes! This is the first spider of any kind I've found in this new year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The spider is about 245 pixels across on my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the gall is 23.4x245/4800 = 1.9 mm, less than 1/10 inch. It's really tiny!

It's another very cold night - 26∞ already, and dropping.

File.Name: d112b_d101-012.jpg
Notes: 8375566432 This is another small point break on "The Ranch" - Hollister or Bixby ranches, I lost count - near Point Conception. This is all private land, so the only access is by boat or a long illegal walk, from Gaviota or Jalama. These folks at the bottom of my photo came by a rubber boat. It's hazardous, but maybe worth it for the waves. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland, Oregon. This should be a National Seashore - but public access would change it for the worse. (Point Conception, 1 January 2013)

File.Name: d112c_d101-038.jpg
Notes: 8375515134 These are eroded gullies in the Solomon Hills, not too far from the city of Guadalupe. The remote Point Sal beach is just over the mountains. The hills are named for Salomon Maria Simeon Pico, who may well be the inspiration for the Zorro stories. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way to Portland, Oregon. (Solomon Hills, 1 January 2013)

File.Name: d112d_d110-039.jpg
Notes: 8375472308 This is the Salinas River where the train crosses it from east to west. This is upstream from the Nacimiento and San Antonio Rivers, which give it a boost. It doesn't look like much here, but this is the largest river of the central coast of California, running 170 miles to the ocean near Monterey. I would like to explore here. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way home from Portland, Oregon. (Near San Miguel, San Luis Obispo County, California, 10 January 2013)

File.Name: d112e_d110-023.jpg
Notes: 8374368699 These are new row crops - artichokes? - near Salinas, California. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way home from Portland, Oregon. (Salinas, California, 10 January 2013)

File.Name: d112f_d110-009.jpg
Notes: 8374347413 Did you ever wonder what happens to the paper you (hopefully) dump into the recycle bin? Maybe it comes to this plant along the train tracks in San Jose, California. I got this photo through the window of our the moving train on our way home from Portland, Oregon. It's not pretty, but here it is. That's a lot of paper! (San Jose, 10 January 2013)

File.Name: d112g_d108-008.jpg
Notes: 8375348912 I've always coveted this signed poster of The Most Immaculately Hip Aristocrat Lord Buckley. It's safe on the wall at Auntie Laurie's house in Portland, Oregon. If you don't know about Crown Prince Richard, try a Google or YouTube search. "Dig Infinity - and they dug? it!" (Portland, Oregon, 8 January 2013)

File.Name: d112h_d110-056.jpg
Notes: 8374200799 This is another photo of the glorious sunset that greeted us on our train trip home. Yes, it really was this orange. You can see some "God light" - aka "crepuscular rays". This is looking over the estuary of the Santa Ynez River, where it meets the ocean. The black specks are birds, not specks on my camera sensor. I got this photo on the bridge at Surf Beach through the window of our the moving train on our way home from Portland, Oregon. (Surf Beach, 10 January 2013)

File.Name: d113a_d113-018.jpg
Notes:
8379228566 These are new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's not flowering on the pass yet. I found these flowers lower in the mountains on the way to Santa Barbara, along Highway 154. If you look close, you can see an early March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) working a flower in the lower-right. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 13 January 2013)

The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! In a good year, they cover the south-facing mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. The delicate purple 3-parted styles are beautiful, and they have a shiny "floral disk" at the base of each flower that serves as a nectar gland. In these flowers the disk is rich blue, but in other plants of the same species they are yellow.

File.Name: d113b_d113-006.jpg
Notes: 8379208884 These are lovely new glandular flowers on a native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2013)

File.Name: d113c_d112-013.jpg
Notes:
8379147768 These Cortinarius mushrooms look superficially like the mushrooms in my [Previous] photo, but they are different. These mushrooms really are orange-capped with yellow gills. I guess they are part of the Cortinarius fulmineus group - at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with yellow colors. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

There are dozens or hundreds or maybe gazillions of Cortinarius species of mushrooms in our woods. The old ones all look the same, but I love the fresh ones for their color and character. Edibility unknown, mainly because they are so difficult to distinguish.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Cortinarius fulmineus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Cortinarius fulmineus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Cortinarius

File.Name: d113d_d113-011.jpg
Notes:
8379075806 These might be the same blue colored Cortinarius mushrooms as my [Previous] photo, though they look very different. But they were growing under the same Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees as my previous mushrooms, and they do have blotches of blue colors on the caps. The rusty-brown spores of these mushrooms collect on the gills and caps and give them an orange color. They are definitely subgenus Bulbopodium, maybe Cortinarius glaucopus? Notice the cute little Millipede (class Diplopoda, Myriapoda) hanging on to the mushroom on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2013)

This photo and my [Previous] photo show why you shouldn't collect any Cortinarius mushrooms for the table. There are so many species and they are all so variable that they are impossible to distinguish. Do you feel lucky?
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius glaucopus; Diplopoda; Myriapoda; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius glaucopus; Diplopoda; Myriapoda; Cortinarius

File.Name: d113e_d113-013.jpg
Notes:
8378959296 This is the genus Cortinarius mushroom that superficially resembles the edible Blewitt mushroom in my [Previous] photo. But only superficially. It has blewitt colors, but it also has a viscid cap, a bulbous base, and the rusty-orange fibers on its stem that are the remnant of the "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills. The cortina hairs fall away from the cap and catch the falling rusty-brown spores. As the mushrooms mature, you can still recognize the spore-stained hairs on the stem. I believe this one is subgenus Bulbopodium. Maybe it's Cortinarius glaucopus or Cortinarius sodagnitus? (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2013)

There are dozens or hundreds or maybe gazillions of Cortinarius species of mushrooms in our woods. The old ones all look the same, but I love the fresh ones for their color and character. Edibility unknown, mainly because they are so difficult to distinguish.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; cortina; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius glaucopus; Cortinarius sodagnitus; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; cortina; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius glaucopus; Cortinarius sodagnitus; Cortinarius

File.Name: d113f_d113-015.jpg
Notes: 8377851377 These are edible and tasty Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods! Be careful though - the mushroom caps start blue, but they soon fade to tan like the one on the left is doing. Yet they always have a "blewitt aura" that is recognizable with experience. We also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar - see my [Next] photo. Caveat emptor! These blewitts are growing under native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri). (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2013)
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Caveat emptor; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Caveat emptor; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: d113g_d110-006.jpg
Notes:
8377790985 Sequoia cellfonium?

Hah - this is a cell phone antenna "disguised" as a tree. I've also seen them disguised as windmills and boulders. I don't own a cellphone, so I reckon I can say this bugs me. I'd rather see a plain antenna. (San Jose, 10 January 2013)

I got this photo through the window of our moving train on our way home from Portland, Oregon. I've got many more photos from this trip, but they can wait for a rainy day when I can't get outside. There's new life in the woods at home!

File.Name: d113h_d112-002.jpg
Notes: 8378786590 This is another photo of the tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in my [Previous] photo on a leaf of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I know, the spider is not quite sharp - but look in the gap between it's two body parts. This spider is carrying an orange hitchhiker! I can only make out 6 legs, which seems to indicate an insect rather than a mite, but I'm not sure. I don't think lice favor spiders. I'm not sure what this critter is. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

File.Name: d114a_d114-005.jpg
Notes:
8382732912 There are new insects in the woods, but they are mostly tiny flies like this and the critters in my [Previous] photo. I believe this is a humpbacked Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae of the order Diptera. You can see spines on its tibiae. It's perched oh-so-delicately on a seed leaf of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2013)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The fly is about 975 pixels long on my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.4x975/4800 = 4.75 mm, less than 2/10 inch. It's small!

I also got this photo in a place where the sun never shines at this time of year. Please forgive the white balance.
ScientificName: Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae; Diptera; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae; Diptera; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae

File.Name: d114b_d114-018.jpg
Notes:
8382691008 These are the light-trails of the tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams. I got the photo from our upstairs porch, with Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees behind. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so. I assume this is a mating thing. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2013)

The trick to getting photos like this is to slow your camera down. I got this photo at 1/3 sec @ f18 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. In some years, we have massive mating flights of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees. See this photo (and the two [Previous] photos) from Christmas Day, 2009.
Locality: and the two [Previous] photos) from Christmas Day

File.Name: d114c_d112-023.jpg
Notes:
8382604524 These are Amanita mushrooms in the woods, but what kind? I believe these are the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) crossed with the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom. The two species seem to intergrade. I usually find A. gemmata under Madrone or Manzanita where it has a bright yellow cap. (See this photo.) I usually find A. pantherina under Live Oak, and it looks quite brown. (See this photo.) This mushroom looks in between. It's definitely not edible, and may be dangerously poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

Yipes - mushrooms are hard enough to identify without crosses!
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita gemmata; Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; A. pantherina
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita gemmata; Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; A. pantherina

File.Name: d114d_d112-035.jpg
Notes: 8381475797 This is a close shot of the unfolding partial veil of a Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) or kin mushroom growing in deep oak leaf-litter in the woods. It has a "double-ring" with patches on the underside in a cogwheel pattern. You can see the chocolate-brown gills below the veil. I don't know what gives with those beautiful cortina-like hairs on the veil! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

File.Name: d114e_d112-037.jpg
Notes: 8382513002 I guess these are the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) or kin mushrooms growing in deep oak leaf-litter in the woods. They have long stems, brown gills, no volva, a pleasant almond-like smell and taste, and a "double-ring" with patches on the underside. They may well be edible, but I'm wary of all woodland agaricus species. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

File.Name: d114f_d114-007.jpg
Notes:
8381358969 The Wood Rats have been nibbling on these new Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods growing from the stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. These young mushrooms are still quite gray, without the honey colors that are their namesake. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2013)

It's odd - these are usually among our earliest mushrooms, by Thanksgiving or soon after. I found one early cluster of the mushrooms this year in a place that was awkward to photograph, but nothing else until today. Usually we have huge clusters of them by now. There are other usually-early mushrooms that I haven't seen this year, like Amanita rubescens. I keep looking for them, like little lost sheep.
CommonNames: Wood Rats; Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita rubescens
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita rubescens

File.Name: d114g_d114-015.jpg
Notes: 8382373218 Maybe this beautiful lichen is the "fruticose" Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on a living branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I assume the "gold eyes" are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. They can't reproduce the lichen - only the fungus. There's a different "foliose" lichen in the photo with little dark hair-like structures that may well be the apothecia of their fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2013)
CommonNames: Gold Eye Lichen; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d114h_d114-002.jpg
Notes: 8382336862 How cold has it been lately? These are ice crystals on an old Lactarius mushroom in the garden. It's been frozen for days, and I think it's becoming freeze dried! It's been in the low 20's at night. I know, that's warm by Montana or Klamath Lake standards - but it's cold for sunny Santa Barbara! (We live on top of the mountains behind Santa Barbara.) (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2013)

File.Name: d115a_d115-006.jpg
Notes:
8385909100 This is a remarkable native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family in the woods today - the first I've seen this year. (It used to be considered Scrophulariaceae.) The plants come up mushroom-like and ready to flower, though this one doesn't have any flowers yet, only red bracts. I have a photo of the flowers here. This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place, though there are acorn cups of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) next to it. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)

It still cold in the mountains - freezing as I write this. But the temperature got up to 40∞ today, so I think were on a warming track. It's the first time we've been out of the 30's since we got home from Oregon!
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Bigberry Manzanita; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Quercus berberidifolia

File.Name: d115b_d115-017.jpg
Notes:
8384755727 These are the first new flowers I've seen of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family in our woods. This is our other manzanita that flowers after the Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). This one is ssp. mollis because it has long white non-glandular hairs on the twigs. We have other species in the mountains west of us, but only these two on San Marcos Pass near home. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)

The urn-like flowers are similar, but Eastwood Manzanita is different from Bigberry Manzanita in several ways. It has a burl that can come back after a wildfire and the berries contain separate nutlets, and the stems and petioles are hairy but not glandular. The leaves are darker and duller. The inflorescence has rather long bracts. Compare my [Previous] photo of Bigberry Manzanita flowers.
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: d115c_d115-019.jpg
Notes: 8385800618 The are pretty urn-shaped flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Compare my [Next] photo of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa). The flowers are similar, but the shrubs are different. For one thing, these flowers come first. See my [Next] photo. If you look close, you can see where wild bees have made holes to get into the flowers before they open. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)

File.Name: d115d_d115-003.jpg
Notes: 8385765490 I guess these are Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms showing the effect of the dry weather lately - but the orange stains and fibrillose caps suggest Gymnopilus. On the other hand, the decurrent gills seem to indicate Omphalatus. They are growing from the base of a still-living Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)
ScientificName: Omphalotus olivascens; Gymnopilus; Omphalatus; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Omphalotus olivascens; Gymnopilus; Omphalatus; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: d115e_d115-013.jpg
Notes: 8384605777 I guess these are Vulgar Milk Cap (Lactarius argillaceifolius var. megacarpus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I first thought they might be Russula mushrooms, so I gave the one one the right the "russula test" - see if the stem breaks like a piece of chalk. It does! I then put the pieces back together for a photo. I didn't notice the drop of white latex "milk" on the gills which pegs it as a Lactarius until I got the photo up on my computer. It has lavender colors and a mild taste at first, slowly becoming a bit hot and acrid. My book says it's possibly poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)
CommonNames: Vulgar Milk Cap; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Lactarius argillaceifolius; megacarpus; Quercus agrifolia; Russula; Lactarius

Notes: 8385636932 This is another blue-colored Cortinarius mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. It maybe the same mushroom as
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Cortinarius glaucopus; Cortinarius

File.Name: d115g_d115-008.jpg
Notes:
8385587796 This is a different Cortinarius mushroom growing under native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri). I guess it is subgenus Phlegmacium, with a viscid cap and an equal stalk without a rimmed base. I tried keying them out in my book, but I get lost every time - David Arora's key is 9 pages long with 138 couplets and over 1,000 species! It's best not to eat any Cortinarius mushrooms anyway. The genus contains several poisonous species, and the species are very hard to tell apart. But I love photographing the pretty young ones! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)

Identifying mushrooms is a bitch - like trying to identify clouds by their shape...

File.Name: d115h_d115-029.jpg
Notes:
8385541556 This is a perfectly named "Dead Man's Foot" (Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" coming up in it's favorite habitat - along a road, though this one is atypically coming out of a sandstone outcrop along a road. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". You can see some bluish colors. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)

Usually this is our earliest mushroom, coming up in fall before the rains, but this is the first one that I've noticed this year. The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - it gets its name from the pea-shaped peridioles which are barely visible in my photo. (Look at the chunky texture of the outer surface.) They break down to release the spores.

File.Name: d116a_d116-004.jpg
Notes:
8387613943 These are new shiny red flowers of a native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. This shrub is just starting to flower. The shiny leaves are as pretty as the flowers. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. It's interesting how the hummingbird flowers are spread out over the winter: California Fuschia -> Big-berry Manzanita -> Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry. It's like a design to keep the hummingbirds going! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2013)

It's definitely warmer today - into the 50s rather than the 30s. I'm still finding interesting mushrooms in deep leaf litter where there is moisture, but the woods are feeling very dry. Darn, it's looking like a repeat of last year with a wet autumn followed by a dry January (and February?) - which should be our wettest months. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: d116b_d116-009.jpg
Notes: 8387590623 Here are the female seed-bearing pistillate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family, with pistils sticking out of the flowers expectantly. These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. See my [Previous] photos for the male pollen-bearing catkins. This is the first "lady shrub" I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2013)

File.Name: d116c_d116-007.jpg
Notes: 8388661146 This is looking close at the pollen-bearing staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. They hang from the stems like "strings of pearls" in impressive displays. (See my [Previous] photo.) These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. See my [Next] photo for the female catkins. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2013)

File.Name: d116d_d116-003.jpg
Notes: 8388622466 Here are pretty new flowers on pollen-bearing staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family - hanging like Christmas tree ornaments. The male flowers appear as "strings of pearls". These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This shrub is male and staminate. They've been flowering for over a month (see this photo from Dec.11), but they are just starting to become showy. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2013)

File.Name: d116e_d115-027.jpg
Notes: 8388560454 I guess these are Tricholoma mushrooms, but mostly by the process of elimination - they are not brittle like Russula, no milk like Lactarius, not waxy like Hygrophorus, no veil like Armillaria, the gills are notched and not decurrent like Clitocybe, etc. Tricholma is what's left among the white-spored mushrooms. I seem to end up here pretty often! I won't try to guess what species these are, but they are large handsome mushrooms. They were growing in deep leaf litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Russula; Lactarius; Hygrophorus; Armillaria; Clitocybe; Tricholma; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Russula; Lactarius; Hygrophorus; Armillaria; Clitocybe; Tricholma; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d116f_d115-023.jpg
Notes: 8388518978 I'm not sure what kind of mushrooms these are, but I suspect genus Hygrophorus. They look a lot like Hygroohorus eburneus without the slime. Maybe they are the Snowy Waxy Cap (Camarophyllus borealis) or kin? They are growing under a copse of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family, where few mushrooms grow. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2013)
ScientificName: Hygrophorus; Hygroohorus eburneus; Camarophyllus borealis; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Hygrophorus; Hygroohorus eburneus; Camarophyllus borealis; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: d116g_d112-014.jpg
Notes: 8387384755 I guess these pretty hand-sized mushrooms are Entoloma rhodopolium or one of its many kin in the Entolomataceae family. They have a dry light brown cap and "pink" or flesh-colored spores when mature. The mushrooms have a "farinaceous" odor like linseed oil, but no chlorine smell like the Entoloma in my [Previous] photo. They are not edible. These are growing in deep leaf litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

File.Name: d116h_d112-039.jpg
Notes: 8387356555 I believe these are the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin. (Or maybe they are Entoloma ferruginans which are said to be common in Southern California, though they seem to have a darker cap from photos I've seen.) They have a surprising chlorine smell like bleach or a swimming pool, and they are definately NOT EDIBLE. You wouldn't want to try one. They have unusual "pink" flesh-colored spores that are just barely visible in my photo. These were growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)
ScientificName: Entoloma nidorosum; Entoloma ferruginans; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Entoloma nidorosum; Entoloma ferruginans; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d117a_d117-008.jpg
Notes:
8390303133 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched oh so delicately on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's cute! You can see the real fly underneath its thick hairs. I suspect they have mating in mind - they are like roosters in the hen yard - ready to jump on anything that comes along! They are hard to approach with camera and tripod. It took a while to get this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

It was warmer today - almost 60 degrees, and a few insects are out in the woods. I'll bet there will be more tomorrow.

File.Name: d117b_d117-004.jpg
Notes:
8390267187 *Sigh* I only had one chance for this photo before the wasp flew away, and the focus isn't quite right. It's a good sharp photo of its ovipositor though!

This is a small Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae) in the order Hymenoptera prowling on dead leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's the first one I've seen in this new year. The seasons are changing. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

File.Name: d117c_d117-007.jpg
Notes: 8391331650 This is just a fly... but it's out in the woods in the middle of winter! I don't know what kind of Fly (order Diptera) it is, though it's rather small. This one is perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's looking right at me! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

File.Name: d117d_d117-011.jpg
Notes:
8391315216 These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon - maybe the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. They are producing sporangia like little surfing palm trees. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

Note how dry the mosses look. The woods really are drying out, though there is still moisture (and mushrooms) in the shady woods under the deep leaf-litter.

File.Name: d117e_d117-015.jpg
Notes:
8390201655 This is the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing in deep oak leaf-litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. Note the brown cap with white "stars" that are the remnants of the universal veil that also leaves a cup or volva at the base of the stem. You can also see the ring or partial veil on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: d117f_d117-024.jpg
Notes: 8391226820 These are beautiful Agaricus mushrooms growing in deep oak leaf-litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. They have dark fibrillose caps and smooth stems below the partial veil. That seems to rule out the Wine-Colored Agaricus (Agaricus subrutilescens) which has cottony white scales below the ring. Maybe they are unusually dark Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) mushrooms? If so, this shows how they get that typical "double-ring" with patches on the underside in a cogwheel pattern. (See a href="http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/8382513002/">this photo.) They have a mild smell and may well be good for the table. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Wine-Colored Agaricus; Woodland Agaricus
ScientificName: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus subrutilescens; Agaricus silvicola
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus subrutilescens; Agaricus silvicola

File.Name: d117g_d117-020.jpg
Notes: 8390093961 Here is a cluster of different Cortinarius mushrooms growing in deep oak leaf-litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. They are definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with viscid caps, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb - see my [Previous] photo. They have reddish colors. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

File.Name: d117h_d117-022.jpg
Notes: 8391146592 This is a different Cortinarius mushroom growing deep oak leaf-litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. This one has reddish colors. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2013)

File.Name: d118a_d118-032.jpg
Notes:
8393036511 Here is a single beautiful Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) climbing up the grass-like stems along the creek below the Coronado Butterfly Preserve today. The spots on its wings and body suddenly make sense as camouflage! (Ellwood Mesa, 18 January 2013)

I usually try to visit this butterfly grove around Christmas Day, but I never made it this year. But my trip today was worth it to capture photos of butterflies on the ground. It was warm and beautiful today. I had to drive to town anyway, so I decided to visit the butterflies. See my [Previous] photos. The butterflies were feeling frisky, and I don't think they'll be around much longer.

File.Name: d118b_d118-003.jpg
Notes:
8394106520 Here are more beautiful Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) "puddling" in the creek at the Coronado Butterfly Preserve today. Nah, I didn't use a "starburst filter" - this is what came out of my camera and lens. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 January 2013)

It may seem an odd time of year for butterflies, but the monarch butterflies from all the western states congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. Some of them settle in Ellwood. This is one of the world's great migrations.

Notes:

File.Name: d118f_d118-013.jpg
Notes:
8393971114 You have to look close to see the large cluster of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus) hanging from the non-native Eucalyptus trees at the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. They really look like dry leaves - you could walk right past without noticing. I only saw this one great cluster. It was a warm day, and the butterflies where feeling frisky - out and about. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 January 2013)

The monarch butterflies from all the western states congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. This is one of the world's great migrations. I usually try to visit this butterfly grove around Christmas Day, but I never made it this year. But this trip was worth it to capture photos of butterflies on the ground. See my [Next] photos.

File.Name: d118g_d118-038.jpg
Notes: 8393947054 Here are new flowers of native Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) in the (Anacardiaceae) plant family. The leaves are thick and plane. They look a lot like Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) with folded leaves like taco-shells which grows in the mountains, but these are strictly coastal. Both species form their buds early, then wait to flower. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 January 2013)

File.Name: d118h_d118-042.jpg
Notes:
8392823713 These are new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! In a good year, they cover the south-facing mountains like snow that you can see from the beach. The delicate purple (mostly) 3-parted styles are beautiful, as are the the 5 yellow stamens, and they have a shiny "floral disk" at the base of each flower that serves as a nectar gland. In these flowers the disk is rich blue, but in other plants of the same species they are yellow. They are still not flowering on top of San Marcos Pass yet. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2013)

If you look close, you see see a "rolled leaf" in the top-left where some critter has made a home.

File.Name: d119a_d119-013.jpg
Notes:
8396321615 I found this beautiful Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly down by the creek today. I finally got the photo of it perched on a sandstone outcrop, with crustose lichens. It looks black and white when it flies, but it is really rich chocolate brown with creamy yellow wing margins. The blue spots are wonderful. I don't see them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)

I have found these early butterflies before - see this photo from February 4, 2006. (Yes, I've been doing this for a while!)

File.Name: d119b_d119-016.jpg
Notes: 8397362962 With that petioled abdomen, I guess this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are waking up after a winter nap. Or is it? My photos of what I think is Mischocyttarus flavitarsis here and here don't show those yellow lines on the thorax. Maybe it's something else? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Mischocyttarus flavitarsis

File.Name: d119c_d119-009.jpg
Notes: 8396234117 These are different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon. They are smaller and more tightly packed than the ones I've been showing, e.g. here and here. They are surprisingly difficult to get in focus! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)

File.Name: d119d_d119-020.jpg
Notes: 8396182365 This is last summer's seed head on what I think is the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae) in the Cyperaceae plant family. There are a zillion kinds of carex in California, so I really don't know. If I'm right, the fibers were an important basket fiber plant for Native Americans. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)

File.Name: d119e_d119-024.jpg
Notes:
8396108801 This might be our most uncommon habitat - a woodland of native dry-land sedges under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I think these might be the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae) in the Cyperaceae plant family - but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. They grow 2 or 3 feet tall. Beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)

There are some dry-land Juncus (Juncaceae) rushes in the background.
CommonNames: sedges; Coast Live Oak; Santa Barbara Sedge
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Carex barbarae; Cyperaceae; Juncus; Juncaceae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Carex barbarae; Cyperaceae; Juncus; Juncaceae

File.Name: d119f_d119-029.jpg
Notes: 8397142600 I guess this is a very light-colored Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. It's coming up through a clump of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the Poaceae plant family. I didn't pick it. The mushrooms are having enough trouble in this suddenly dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)
CommonNames: Panther Amanita; Coast Live Oak; Thin Grass
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae

File.Name: d119g_d119-001.jpg
Notes: 8396044245 Here is another new cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. They have yellow gills, but a deep brown spore color. They are beautiful, but poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d119h_d119-004.jpg
Notes: 8397106980 The Wood Rats have totally decapitated these new Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods growing from the stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. See this photo. Maybe they've read David Arora's Mushrooms Demystified which says "Use only firm caps and discard the tough stems." (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)
CommonNames: Wood Rats; Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Mushrooms Demystified

File.Name: d120a_d120-030.jpg
Notes:
8399910917 These are tiny woodland flies (Diptera) mating on a whorled-leaf native plant of Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Maybe they are Gall Midges or Wood Midges in the family Cecidomyiidae? It's hard to see, but the (male?) fly at the bottom has its abdomen curled up in mating position. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

The warm weather is bringing out more insects and fewer mushrooms. I saw Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterflies and Pyrausta dapalis (Crambidae) moths in the Lepidoptera today, but neither wanted to pose. There's no rain in the forecast until next Saturday at the earliest.
CommonNames: flies; Climbing Bedstraw; Gall Midges; Wood Midges; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Diptera; Galium porrigens; Rubiaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Celastrina ladon; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: d120b_d120-016.jpg
Notes: 8399848489 This is a different furry Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a living leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Maybe it's the same genus Eristalis as the fly in my [Previous] photo, but I really don't know. This fly is bee-sized, and furry like a bee. It's not really bow-legged - it's grooming its abdomen. The long bee-like hairs on its hind legs seem distinctive. "So many flies, so little time!" (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d120c_d120-008.jpg
Notes:
8400893724 This is a furry little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I guess it is genus Eristalis. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

Compare my photo from November 15 - they sure look like the same fly. It's interesting that these flies are one of the last of the old year as well as one of the first of the new year. I guess they over-winter as adults, so they are here on both ends of winter. It makes sense. They lay their eggs near the larvae of other insects, and fall and spring is when they are about.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d120d_d120-005.jpg
Notes:
8399766779 Me and my shadow...

This is another furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's cute! You can see the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

File.Name: d120e_d120-017.jpg
Notes:
8399706401 This is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on the tip of an old leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's not as sharp as I would like, but this is my first for the new year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

It's interesting that these flies are one of the last of the old year (see this photo) as well as one of the first of the new year. I guess they over-winter as adults, so they are here on both ends of winter. It makes sense. They lay their eggs near the larvae of other insects, and fall and spring is when they are about.
Locality: see this photo) as well as one of the first of the new year. I guess they over-winter as adults

File.Name: d120f_d120-032.jpg
Notes: 8399645451 This is a hefty "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom that I found in the woods today, with my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife for scale. The voluptuous shape remind me of the paleolithic Venus figurines found in Europe. These Bolete mushroom have a brick-red spongy spore-bearing surface instead of gills. The mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle then. They don't look very appetizing - in fact they are quite poisonous. Don't eat them - but you must enjoy them! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

File.Name: d120g_d120-024.jpg
Notes:
8400686114 This is another photo of a remarkable native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family in the woods today - the first I've seen this year. (It used to be considered Scrophulariaceae.) The plants come up mushroom-like and almost ready to flower, with leafy red bracts. This one doesn't have any flowers yet, though it does have buds in the upper-leaf axils. Another week should do it. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

The plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant.

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold on the Internet "legal high" market. The effects are described on one web site as "tranquilizers, muscle relaxants, powerful aphrodesiacs, and sedatives". They sell for quite a bit, but I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants bothers me - especially these interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated.
Locality:
Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant.

File.Name: d120h_d120-025.jpg
Notes: 8399545947 This is Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a sandstone outcrop. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this! This plant looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places, like the lichens in the photo. I've heard they are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

File.Name: d121a_d121-018.jpg
Notes:
8404436910 This is a close shot of a Cortinarius mushroom showing the "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills when young. They tear away from the cap as the mushroom matures, and hang from the stem - usually decorated with the rusty-red spores. This is the same kind of Cortinarius mushroom as my [Previous] photo. I love to photograph these mushrooms when they are young. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)

Today's photos are all Cortinarius mushrooms - maybe all the same kind, except for my photo of the Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus). That's the problem with these mushrooms - they are so variable as they age that you don't know what you have. There are poisonous species, so don't eat any of them - unless you are feeling adventurous!

It's very dry, but there are still mushrooms in the woods hiding under the oak leaves where the sun never shines. There's a chance of rain next weekend.
Locality: Cortinarius collinitus). That's the problem with these mushrooms - they are so variable as they age that you don't know what you have. There are poisonous species

File.Name: d121b_d121-014.jpg
Notes:
8404373720 These are the Cortinarius mushrooms that look superficially like the edible Blewitt mushroom. (See this photo.) They are quite blue when young, but they usually have rusty hairs on the stem and viscid caps. I guess they are "Cortinarius glaucopus" - but that's just the name I generically give all these blue mushrooms. I'm not sure what they are. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)

These are large satisfying mushrooms, but give them a few days, and they all look the same. None of them should be eaten because of that. But they are great to photograph!

File.Name: d121c_d121-012.jpg
Notes: 8403216281 These are more Cortinarius mushrooms in the subgenus Bulbopodium in the woods today. Look at that thick deposit of rusty-red spores in a natural spore print below! They might be the same "Cortinarius glaucopus" I've been showing today, but I don't know. These mushrooms were growing under a deep mulch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. The sun rarely shines here at this time of year, but I caught this on just right. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius glaucopus; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius glaucopus; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d121d_d121-008.jpg
Notes: 8403172219 These are distinctive Cortinarius mushrooms - the Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus), growing in a mixed forest of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees. It's a slimy mushroom with the remains of a universal slime veil on the cap and stem. There should be globs of slime along the stem, but the dry weather says otherwise. You can see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stems. It's quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)
CommonNames: Banded Cortinarius; Coast Live Oak; Madrone

File.Name: d121e_d121-025.jpg
Notes: 8404219398 These are more Cortinarius mushrooms in the subgenus Bulbopodium in the woods today. They might be the same "Cortinarius glaucopus" - but the caps of these look more gray than blue. That's the problem with these large satisfying mushrooms - give them a few days, and they all look the same. None of them should be eaten because of that. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)

File.Name: d121f_d120-028.jpg
Notes: 8403085045 These are more blue-colored Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods. Like most Cortinarius mushrooms, they have distinctive rusty-red fibers on the stem. They are definitely subgenus Bulbopodium with viscid caps, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. Maybe the are Cortinarius glaucopus - but that's the name I generically give all these blue mushrooms. You probably shouldn't collect any Corinarius mushrooms for the table, because they are so variable and hard to tell apart. It would be a great project, but be sure to leave notes for the rest of us! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

File.Name: d121g_d119-025.jpg
Notes: 8404130820 This is a long-stemmed Cortinarius mushroom growing in deep oak leaf-litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It is definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a small rimmed basal bulb. You can see pretty violet colors on the stem but not the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2013)

File.Name: d121h_d121-024.jpg
Notes:
8404078612 "There be mushrooms here!" It's been dry this month, but there is still moisture under the oak leaves in places where the sun seldom shines at this time of year. The mushrooms are hiding under the oak leaves where there is moisture to ripen their spores. The trick to finding them is to look for the "mushrumps" like this - that tell-tale mound of leaves and woodland detritus. Sometimes they are just gopher mounds, but other times they hold surprising new mushrooms pushing up the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)

There were blue Cortinarius mushrooms under this mushrump. See my [Next] few photos.

File.Name: d122a_d120-015.jpg
Notes:
8407565290 Yoga for flies?
"I could do that but I don't want to..."

Here is another photo of the furry Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) grooming its wings on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I guess it's a same genus Eristalis. This fly is bee-sized, and furry like a bee. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

*Sigh* I never got outside today. I've been struggling with M$ Word making the new newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. Maybe I'll get out tomorrow. There's change in the weather today... I hope rain is coming. Four photos are just fine tonight.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d122b_d120-022.jpg
Notes:
8406431727 Here are new native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Orobanchaceae plant family in the woods. (They used to be considered Scrophulariaceae, and I still think of them that way.) The new plants come up mushroom-like with leafy red bracts, almost ready to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. You can see the spent flowers on the ground. They must have a fungal connection with their host plant.
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: d122c_d121-021.jpg
Notes: 8406407695 Here are more "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin mushrooms. They have a surprising chlorine smell like bleach or a swimming pool, and they are definately NOT EDIBLE. You wouldn't want to try one. They have what passes for "pink" spores (really flesh-colored, or salmon) that are visible on the gills in my photo. These were growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2013)

File.Name: d122d_d112-033.jpg
Notes:
8406357893 Maybe this is the Tiger Tricholma (Tricholoma pardinum) in the woods? It's growing in deep oak mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The almost monochrome streaks on the caps are striking. They have notched gills and a mealy "farinaceous" smell. They are large, attractive mushrooms. It's said to be poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2013)

This is an older photo from the archives, but I didn't want it to disappear. I've found a few more of them since then, but this is the better photo.

File.Name: d123a_d114-013.jpg
Notes:
8409183597 This is another little pink-spored mushroom in the Entolomataceae family - Nolanea, Leptonia, or Entoloma. They do have "twisted-striate" stems which suggest Nolanea verna, but I really don't know - or much care. They have no particular smell. The stems are quite brittle. I just brushed against the picked one, and it broke. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

I've been at my computer all day finishing the new newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I've sent it out for review. I'll post a link when it's final. Almost done, but I have to take it to the printer tomorrow. Four photos are just fine tonight.

We've had a few drops of rain today, but not enough to measure. It feels like we're on the bottom edge of "virga" - rain that doesn't quite make it to the ground. I hope we get some real rain.

File.Name: d123b_d120-018.jpg
Notes: 8409110923 Along with Cortinarius mushrooms, this is the time for Milky Cap (Lactarius) mushrooms in the woods. I cut the gills of this pure white one and didn't see any latex, but it's been very dry. It has a mild taste. I can see a couple of drops of white latex on the stem. It's definitely a Lactarius mushroom, but I'm not sure what kind. They are handsome enough, but not particularly interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

File.Name: d123c_d120-003.jpg
Notes: 8410182350 This is a small plant of succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's growing on a bank of sandstone by the creek in our canyon. I'm not sure if it's a seedling or a yearling, but I guess the latter. I put my pen in the photo for scale. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)

File.Name: d123d_d120-001.jpg
Notes: 8409045887 By chance, I got this photo of the same kind of little fly (order Diptera) that I found mating in this photo taken on the same day. I guess it is their time! I have a hard enough time with BIG flies, but maybe this is a Gall Midge or Wood Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae? It's perched on an old leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2013)
CommonNames: fly; Gall Midge; Wood Midge; Mugwort

File.Name: d124a_bc21-049.jpg
Notes:
8412311833 This might be a King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom in the woods - or maybe the White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii)? The latter makes more sense because it's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) instead of pines, but it does have a dark cap. It's also known as porcini, cËpe, steinpilz, and others. I like the Italian name porcini the best - "little pig" because that's what it feels like, so solid and dense! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

Like all Boletes, this one has pores instead of gills. The fresh pores are white and "stuffed" - not yet open to the air. This mushroom is prime, except for that yellow streak that may be the start of an infestation of the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that turns them into yellow mush. We trimmed that part before we ate it.

Notes:
8412263491 These are brilliant Boletus flaviporus mushrooms in the woods. They have viscid reddish-brown caps and intensely yellow gills that do not stain blue. They are striking mushrooms that are endemic to the west coast. They are said to be edible, but mediocre. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 20 December 2011)

File.Name: d124c_bc15-008.jpg
Notes:
8412216989 Here is a colander full of freshly washed Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms! This photo is from last year because I haven't found a single one yet this year. Que l·stima. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2011)

Chanterelles are easy to recognize once you've seen them - but you have to learn for yourself. They are yellow-orange, and they have blunt folds underneath rather than sharp gills. Books and photos help, but you really have to learn the mushrooms from another person who knows, and then you will know.

File.Name: d124d_bc12-008.jpg
Notes:
8413291670 Here are beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms in our woods. You can clearly see the yellow-green colors on the smooth and polished caps, but they're not always this obvious. The mushroom on the left has a perfect partial-veil ring on the stem, but the "death cup" volva at the base is harder to see. This mushroom is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous! There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods, but mushroom spores can travel around the planet.

Notes:
8413249768 I believe this is a "Woodland Amanita (Amanita silvicola) mushroom in the woods - NOT "The Blusher" Amanita rubescens, though it does have some reddish colors on its cap and stem. It has powdery warts on the cap, and a very distinct ring on the stem, but no obvious volva. Edibility is questionable. The little green plants are native perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) in the Rubiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2010)

File.Name: d125a_d125-019.jpg
Notes:
8414814951 We've been having Banana Slug weather!

This is a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) prowling through the woods. They get 6-8 inches long, though this is a small one - finger-size rather than hand-size. This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains, and we only see them after rains. They have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower air provides a tactile sense and smell. They move surprising fast - fast enough that I have to bump up the ISO to capture them. They slide over the rocks just fine. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2013)

We've had over 2 inches of rain since it started two nights ago - mostly slow drizzle that sinks in. The new Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter is done and off to the printer. I'm looking forward to my walk in the woods tomorrow!

File.Name: d125b_d125-002.jpg
Notes: 8415874360 This is a new flower bud on native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. It's growing in a place that burned a decade or more ago. It's a "fire follower" plant that lingers. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The foliage is as handsome as the flowers. This photo isn't quite sharp, but it captures the moment - in the rain, with my raincoat covering my camera. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 25 January 2013)

File.Name: d125c_d125-006.jpg
Notes: 8414756161 This is a tiny Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops. I guess all lupines catch the rain! This tiny lupine-to-be is growing with the new non-native Filaree (Erodium) plants that grow everywhere. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 25 January 2013)

File.Name: d125d_d125-011.jpg
Notes: 8415822238 Here is another photo of the new cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. They have greenish-yellow gills, but a deep brown spore color. They are beautiful, but poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I wish all poisonous mushrooms were so obliging! (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2013)
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d125e_d125-014.jpg
Notes: 8415806322 These pretty hand-sized mushrooms are long-stemmed and pink-spored Entoloma rhodopolium or one of its many kin in the Entolomataceae family. They have a dry light brown cap and "pink" or flesh-colored spores when mature. The mushrooms have a "farinaceous" odor like linseed oil, but no chlorine smell like some other Entolomas. They are not edible. These are growing in deep leaf litter under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2013)

File.Name: d125f_d125-005.jpg
Notes: 8414671771 This is a strange Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The "rays" of the fruiting body fold back to lift up the spore sack inside when it's damp. When it's dry, they fold back up to enclose the spore sack. It's been wet lately! They are modified puffballs with a thick outer skin that splits and unfolds into a star-like shape that lifts up the spore sack within. You can see the terminal pore of the inner sack. The old rinds last in the woods for years, but this is a new one with spore sack still attached. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2013)

File.Name: d125g_bc15-019.jpg
Notes:
8414619279 While we're on the topic of mushroom cookery...

Here's a pan of yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms "stewing in their own juices". It's really remarkable how much juice these mushrooms make in a hot pan, especially after a rain. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2011)

We "dry sautÈ" them. Clean your Chanterelles with as little water as possible - they are like sponges. Then slice them into a pan on high heat with no oil, and they will make their own sauce as the water cooks out of them. Stirring with a hand-whittled wood spoon must help! Let the juice cook down until it's almost gone, and add a bit of olive oil, salt, and garlic and let it simmer... delicious!

This old photo from the archives makes me hungry! We haven't seen a single Chanterelle or good Bolete all winter, but every mushroom year is different. Maybe tomorrow?

File.Name: d125h_bc21-063.jpg
Notes:
8414585819 Since flygrl67 asked, here is how we prepared the King Bolete (Boletus edulis) - or is it the White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii)? - mushroom in my [Previous] photo.

We cut out the bad spots, and sliced the mushroom to sautÈ with kale and garlic in a bit of olive oil and lemon juice - served with lamb chops. It is remarkably solid and white, like an apple or potato. It doesn't get much better than this! This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

File.Name: d126a_d126-005.jpg
Notes:
8418081823 These are odd Eyelash Cup Fungi in the woods, probably Scutellinia scutellata. They have tiny raindrops on their eyelashes! These are growing on old bark of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Nature is so bizarre and fanciful if you look close - you just have to smile and accept it! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)

It was mostly gloomy today, but it didn't rain. We ended up with 2.3 inches of rain from recent storms.It's still dry for January, but not as bad as it was!
CommonNames: Eyelash Cup Fungi; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d126b_d126-001.jpg
Notes: 8418016595 I guess this odd mushroom is the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin), but I really don't know. It's quite small - maybe 1/2 inch across. I found a number of these under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods today. The cups expand an become wavy as they age. (Don't we all!) There is no stalk and no ribs at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)
CommonNames: Spreading Brown Cup Fungus; Coast Live Oak

Notes: 8419061868 I found this is a large and solid Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today. Here it is posed with my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife for scale. It looked quite brown on top, and I first thought it was a bolete. I reckon it's subgenus Phlegmacium, with a viscid cap and a swollen stalk - but without a rimmed base. I'm not sure what species it is. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)

File.Name: d126f_d126-015.jpg
Notes: 8418909054 I don't know what this white Cortinarius mushroom is, but it's definitely in the subgenus Bulbopodium of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. It was dark and breezy today, so I had to bump up the ISO to get a photo. Even so, I took many photos to get this one keeper.

File.Name: d126h_d126-011.jpg
Notes:
8417710897 If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - be very careful with Thistles!

This is a new plant of native Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has beautiful violet flowers. (See this photo.) The weedy Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) looks very similar when it's just sprouting. It would be terrible to pull the beautiful natives by mistake. If you have any doubts, don't pull it! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)
