File.Name: d127b_d127-015.jpg
Notes: 8422510314 These are beautiful new leaves of the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I found in the woods today. The broad leaves come first, then whither away before they send up flower stalks in summer - though I never saw flowers in our last dry year. Those are white flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2013)

File.Name: d127c_d127-029.jpg
Notes: 8421397897 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Ever so slowly, spring is coming to the mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2013)

File.Name: d127d_d126-038.jpg
Notes:
8422450014 Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) in the garden!

Well, it's the wild edge of the garden beyond the wall, mostly a tangle of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family and California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The deer was mostly interested in the Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family, not visible in this photo. This is the mama deer, and she's keeping watch on her two almost-yearling kids not too far away. See this photo and this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)
CommonNames: Mule Deer; Wild Blackberry; California Honeysuckle; Wild Sweet Pea
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: d127e_d127-004.jpg
Notes:
8422341980 This is a small group of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a still-living Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. They are said to glow in the dark, but our west coast mushrooms don't seem to do that. They are quite poisonous - don't eat them! There's no reason to confuse them with chanterelles - they are tough, with greenish colors on the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2013)

I didn't realize until after I posted this, but they are probably the same mushrooms I showed recently in this photo. Well, they are quite photogenic!

File.Name: d127f_d127-017.jpg
Notes: 8422301244 Hah - here is a Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushroom on the left and a dark "oak apple" gall on the right. The puffball has a terminal pore, and the galls have exit holes. I keep getting them confused! The gall is made by the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera) on twigs of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2013)
CommonNames: Dark Puffball; California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Lycoperdon foetidum; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Lycoperdon foetidum; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d127g_d126-030.jpg
Notes: 8422262798 This is a another photo of the slimy Cortinarius mushroom I found in the woods yesterday - it's almost too slimy to pick up. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places it in the subgenus Myxacium. I believe this is part of the Cortinarius cylindripes clan. It has beautiful lavender colors on both cap and stem. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Myxacium; Cortinarius cylindripes; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Myxacium; Cortinarius cylindripes; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d127h_d126-036.jpg
Notes: 8422203926 I'm still sure exactly what species of Agaricus mushroom this is, but here it is! It has a large flaring partial veil ring that looks like a corkscrew in this photo. It also has a characteristic "double ring" that first tears free from the top of the cap, then from the edge. You can see the scars here; also see this photo. Maybe they are unusually dark Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) mushrooms? They have a mild smell and taste, and may well be good for the table. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)

File.Name: d128a_d128-001.jpg
Notes:
8426076820 Here's a little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family, trying to warm up on a cold morning. It was windy, so I was lucky to get any photo! It looks like an Ichneumon Wasp in the family Ichneumonidae, though it doesn't have a long ovipositor. I guess it's a male. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)

It was cold and windy again today, but not quite as cold or windy as yesterday. I have hopes for tomorrow.

We had some excitement tonight! I'm pretty sure we heard a California Spotted Owl aka Southern Spotted Owl (Strix occidentalis occidentalis) in the canyon at home. These are uncommon owls, and it's always exciting to hear one. My first thought was a coyote, but we could hear it moving around in the canyon. We alerted someone who knows.
Locality: Strix occidentalis occidentalis) in the canyon at home. These are uncommon owls
CommonNames: Wasp; Canyon Sunflower; Ichneumon Wasp; California Spotted Owl; Southern Spotted Owl
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Ichneumonidae; Strix occidentalis occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Ichneumonidae; Strix occidentalis occidentalis

File.Name: d128a_d128-002.jpg
Notes: 8424923987 This is a tangle of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family that's climbed past the top of a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. Where does it go from here? (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Sweet Pea; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d128c_d128-020.jpg
Notes: 8424830133 I believe these are the "Lackluster Laccaria" (Laccaria Laccata) mushrooms in the woods today. They don't look so lackluster here, with these pretty violet colors to the gills - but they pale before Laccaria amethysteo-occidentalis with deeper colors under pine, and the Amethyst Laccaria (Laccaria amethystina) with very deep violet colors in Oregon. (Or are they both L. amethysteo-occidentalis?) (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)
ScientificName: Laccaria Laccata; Laccaria amethysteo-occidentalis; Laccaria amethystina; L. amethysteo-occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Laccaria Laccata; Laccaria amethysteo-occidentalis; Laccaria amethystina; L. amethysteo-occidentalis

File.Name: d128d_d128-008.jpg
Notes: 8425874440 I guess these are part of the Cortinarius fulmineus group - at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with yellow colors. They are definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. They have yellow caps, obscured by the rusty-red spores that collect here. The spore-stained cortina on the stems is beautiful. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)

File.Name: d128e_d128-005.jpg
Notes: 8425845650 This is a large Agaricus mushroom in the woods, growing in a clump of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family. I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. The mushroom has a large partial veil ring, but it doesn't seem to be a "double ring" . It has a mild smell and taste, and may well be good for the table. I'm really confused by our woodland Agaricus species, and I leave them be. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)

File.Name: d128f_d128-012.jpg
Notes: 8424720043 This is another pink-spored Entoloma rhodopolium or one of its many kin in the Entolomataceae family. They have a dry light brown cap and "pink" or flesh-colored spores when mature. These mushrooms have a "farinaceous" odor like linseed oil, but no chlorine smell like some other Entolomas. They are not edible. Entoloma mushrooms are very common in the woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)

File.Name: d128g_d128-015.jpg
Notes:
8424643921 I found these beautiful red Russula emetica (or kin) mushrooms in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Emphasize the "or kin"! The mushrooms may not be red enough for the real "Russula emetica" and there are other possibilities - Russula cremoricolor, Russula bicolor, and Russula californiensis to name three. I guess I really don't care, as long as I can recognize them when I find them in our woods. These are the first red-capped Russula mushrooms I've found this year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. They are one of our most beautiful creatures!
ScientificName: Russula emetica; Quercus agrifolia; Russula cremoricolor; Russula bicolor; Russula californiensis
associatedTaxa: Russula emetica; Quercus agrifolia; Russula cremoricolor; Russula bicolor; Russula californiensis

File.Name: d128h_d127-007.jpg
Notes: 8424579785 Here's another photo of the beautiful new flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Orobanchaceae plant family. (They used to be considered Scrophulariaceae.) These flowers look orange, but they are often deep red. The flowers are 2-lipped - the upper lip 2-lobed, and the lower lip 3-lobed with neat yellow tips. (So it has a primal semblance of 5 petals.) The pistil is exerted from the upper lip, and the stamens are included within the tube - visible in

File.Name: d129a_d129-029.jpg
Notes:
8428003327 These are new flowers of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah macrocarpus in the Cucurbitaceae family) growing in the mountains. These are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat the spiny fruit. The flowers look like they've been dipped in sugar! The plants are "monoecious" with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. The upper flowers are male or pollen-bearing. The lower flower is female or seed-bearing. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2013)

At least I believe this is M. macrocarpus and not M. fabaceus - the species are similar, and confusing. But Marah fabaceus has yellowish flowers and mostly grows further back in the mountains.

Today started windy, but then the wind died down - and changed directions, coming from the ocean instead of from the north! It's calm now.
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; Marah fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus; Marah fabaceus

File.Name: d129b_d129-023.jpg
Notes: 8429068436 Here are the first new flowers I've found of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. This early flower seems to be missing a lobe - it has five pairs of green glands, but only four petal lobes. I reckon this is the species, with long hairs. We also have var. hoffmanii with nearly glabrous stems. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2013)

File.Name: d129c_d129-036.jpg
Notes:
8429033220 Like snow in the mountains!

This is a mass flowering of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the mountains on the south-facing slope of the Santa Ynez Mountains, with zillions of individual flowers. In a really good year, you can see them from the beach. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 29 January 2013)

File.Name: d129d_d129-003.jpg
Notes:
8427906953 These are new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I found these flowers on the Pass today, along West Camino Cielo road. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2013)

Notice that these shrubs have alternate leaves. Most of these Ceanothus species with thick leaves have opposite leaves, but this one is unusual. It only grows on the south-facing side of our east-west trending mountains. On the north-facing slopes, it is replaced by Ceanothus cuneatus.

File.Name: d129e_d129-013.jpg
Notes:
8427838703 I wish this were a better photo, but sometimes you have to accept what you get, and it's a record-photo. I hope I get more chances for a better photo this year.

This is a beautiful little diurnal (daytime) moth resting on a dead stem in the woods. I'm pretty sure it is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. You can see its "snout". It has brilliant red "underwings" that makes them easy to spot in the air, but you only get a glimpse in this photo. It led me on a chase through these dead plant stems, so I was happy to get this photo! Its a nearly 1:1 crop from my much larger original photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2013)

I have better photos here, here, here, and here.

File.Name: d129f_d129-021.jpg
Notes: 8427775063 I guess these are different Tricholoma mushrooms. I won't try to guess what species they are, but they are large handsome mushrooms and they seem to have viscid caps when moist. (Tricholoma resplendens comes to mind.) They were growing in deep leaf litter under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, with a Coulter Pine nearby. I think I've found these before - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2013)

File.Name: d129g_d128-016.jpg
Notes: 8427754059 I guess these are more Tiger Tricholma (Tricholoma pardinum) mushrooms in the woods. They are growing in deep oak mulch under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The almost monochrome streaks on the caps are striking. They have notched gills and a mealy "farinaceous" smell. They are large, attractive mushrooms that are said to be poisonous. There were quite a few of them in this one place in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2013)

File.Name: d129h_d129-002.jpg
Notes: 8427706393 These are "JARs" - "Just Another Russula" - as my mushroom book puts it. They are growing in a mixed forest of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees. I guess they are the Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes or kin) mushroom, but I really don't know. Note the typical centrally depressed cap in the upper-left. They are said to be "edible, but better kicked than picked." They do make great "mushrumps" in the leaves, full of expectations! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2013)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Madrone; Short-Stemmed Russula
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Russula brevipes
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Russula brevipes

File.Name: d130a_d130-014.jpg
Notes:
8431914012 This is another photo of the remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema). It moved, and so did I. It has spiny legs, a dinosaur-like head, and a mighty proboscis folded under its head. This one was perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Sure, it's a predator. They look fast and fierce, but they move _v_e_r_y__s_l_o_w_l_y_. They must sneak up on their prey, below the level of their motion sensors. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)

Not a bad photo day - interesting insects, new flowers, and (recent) mushrooms!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d130b_d130-011.jpg
Notes: 8430787739 This is a remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema). It has spiny legs, a dinosaur-like head, and a mighty proboscis folded under its head. This one was perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Sure, it's a predator. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak

associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d130c_d130-004.jpg
Notes:
8430771253 This is another furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's cute! You can see the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)

There are LARGER bee flies and smaller ones. I think they may be female and male, rather than different species. This is one of the small ones.

File.Name: d130d_d130-002.jpg
Notes:
8431805820 This is a small fly (order Diptera) perched on a stem of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I got this photo in deep shade above the retaining wall on the native side of our garden on a cold morning. I don't know what kind of fly this is, but the wing venation is clear. If you figure it out, please let me know. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)

I like to begin the day looking close above this retaining wall, which is at perfect tripod height. There's no sun at this time of year, but I sometimes find interesting things - like this!

File.Name: d130e_d130-018.jpg
Notes: 8430672719 These are new flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceaeplant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. (Like Flickr, I have to learn new ways!) The red color is mostly from leafy bracts, but there are a few real flowers mixed in - green, with an exerted pistil. It's a subshrub with woolly white hairs. Yes Mark, I'll keep my eyes out for interesting color variations. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)

File.Name: d130f_d130-007.jpg
Notes: 8430645221 Here is a forest of tiny umbrella-like sporophytes of Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. They might be the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. These are sexual reproductive structures, but the details are complicated! It's interesting that other liverworts don't bother making sporophytes. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)

File.Name: d130g_d130-010.jpg
Notes: 8431671302 Here are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. They might be the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. I think the bumpy tongue-like structures on the "leaves" might be the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. These liverworts are not producing umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction like those in my [Next] photo. It's interesting that different groups of plants in the same place have different reproductive strategies. Liverworts are primitive plants, but complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2013)

File.Name: d130h_d126-035.jpg
Notes: 8431651404 These are "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms in the woods, after the rain a few days ago. They have viscid caps and brown spores. I usually find these mushrooms covered with tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects that get stuck in the slime - I consider this a field mark! Like the common name suggests, don't eat them. They are said to cause mild to severe gastrointestinal distress. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2013)

File.Name: d131a_d131-027.jpg
Notes:
8433587081 This is "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods today. I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. It's large and beautiful. It develops pink colors on its cap and stem with age and handling. In some years, it's an early mushroom - but it fruits again later in the year, like now. It's definitely an Amanita mushroom, though this one doesn't seem to have a volva. It did have powdery flacks at the base when I dug it up. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

Mushrooms, new flowers, spider, and insects! It was a good day for photos.

File.Name: d131b_d131-014.jpg
Notes: 8434640230 This is another pink-spored Entoloma rhodopolium or one of its many kin in the Entolomataceae family. It's pushing up a large "mushrump" - that mound of leaves and woodland detritus. If you look close, you can see a small Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera on the mushroom stem.
Locality: 8434640230 This is another pink-spored Entoloma rhodopolium or one of its many kin in the Entolomataceae family. It's pushing up a large "mushrump" - that mound of leaves and woodland detritus. If you look close, you can see a small Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera on the mushroom stem.

File.Name: d131c_d131-003.jpg
Notes: 8433518487 This is a beautiful Agaricus mushroom in the woods - maybe a Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola)? This one has a pure white cap without brown scales. The cap slowly stains yellow, but the base of the stem doesn't. It has a mild smell and taste, and may well be good for the table - but I'm wary of all our woodland species. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

File.Name: d131d_d131-010.jpg
Notes: 8434586710 Here are odd bedfellows - a succulent Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family growing under a plant of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family! I really like this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)
ScientificName: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae; Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae

File.Name: d131e_d131-006.jpg
Notes: 8434547656 These are new just-opening flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. These trees form their flower buds early, so the flowers represent last year's rainfall, which wasn't much. The flowers are surprisingly intricate. I'm sure I'll have another photo in a few days. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

File.Name: d131f_d131-029.jpg
Notes: 8434530690 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It's hard not to over-expose these brilliant little spiders. Sure, this is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

File.Name: d131g_d131-001.jpg
Notes: 8433416377 I'm sorry this photo isn't sharper, but it's still pretty cool. This is a tiny moth (Lepidoptera) - "microlepidoptera" for sure - perched on the concrete retaining wall on the native side of our garden. If you look close, you can see its eye - like a spoked wheel. It has remarkable camouflage in this dark and shady place! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

File.Name: d131h_d131-035.jpg
Notes: 8433416377 This is another photo of a remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema). I got this photo a day later than my [Previous] photos on the same little Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. Yeah, it's not quite sharp - it was moving, it was breezy, all the usual excuses... You can still see this interesting predator pretty well! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d201a_d201-003.jpg
Notes:
8437291474 Here is one more photo of a remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema). This one was perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family this morning. They don't move much for predators! It looks so dinosaur-like.

It was dead calm this morning, so I got this perfect photo using my self-timer to trigger the camera. Then the wind kicked up and the rest of the day was a struggle, waiting for just the right moment when the wind died down to get the photo. The weather is changing, but there is no rain in the forecast.
Locality:
Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family this morning. They don't move much for predators! It looks so dinosaur-like.

It was dead calm this morning
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
