File.Name: d201b_d201-026.jpg
Notes:
8437236034 I found these new flowers of native Milk Maids aka California Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the woods today - the first I've seen this year. It's a woodland native in the Brassicaceae) plant family. It's related to the weedy yellow mustards that cover the California hills, but this one is native. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2013)

Like all mustards, they have four long stamens and two shorter ones. The flowers have a pleasant radish-like taste and are good in a salad. It's known as Toothwort, but you have to dig it up to find the tasty "tooth" hidden underground. Don't even think about it with these first flowers!

It's great fun trying to spot the first new wildflowers that appear in the woods. It's a bit like a ball bouncing from a height - bump....bump....bump...bump..bump.bum.bu.b.bbbbbb! I like finding the early spring flowers and the late summer flowers, but I always lose it in the middle.

File.Name: d201c_d201-014.jpg
Notes: 8437217422 These are pretty new flowers of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family - the first I've seen this year. This shrub is not at all glandular like the more-common Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) that grows in the canyon at home, but it becomes more widespread slightly higher in the mountains. Beautiful leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2013)

File.Name: d201d_d201-024.jpg
Notes: 8437199580 These are the remarkably intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. I think I can make out a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with six orange glands at the base, finally surrounded by a circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2013)

File.Name: d201e_d201-009.jpg
Notes:
8436077847 Here are the strange "flowers" of a native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. These are the female seed-bearing pistillate catkins. The shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The male pollen-bearing catkins look quite different. (See this photo.) The leaves are tomentose underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2013)

Like many native plants, the males mature before the females. This is known as protandry - male function comes before female function. I wonder how pollen gets to the pistils?

File.Name: d201f_d131-022.jpg
Notes:
8437133520 This is another Cortinarius mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. I guess it's part of the Cortinarius fulmineus group - at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with yellow colors. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. They have mostly yellow caps, sometimes obscured by the rusty-red spores that collect here. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

These are large satisfying mushrooms, but give them a few days, and they all look the same. None of them should be eaten because of that. But they are great to photograph!

File.Name: d201g_d131-020.jpg
Notes: 8437115998 This is a different Cortinarius mushroom I found in the woods yesterday. It appears to have a dry, fibrillose cap, so I guess it's subgenus Cortinarius. I didn't unearth it, so I don't know if it has a bulbous base. It's a striking mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

File.Name: d201h_d131-023.jpg
Notes: 8437100066 This is another photo of "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. This shot is "in situ" showing the mushroom as I found it, though I did clear away the oak leaves. It develops blushing-pink colors on its cap and stem with age and handling. It's large and beautiful. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2013)

File.Name: d202a_d202-023.jpg
Notes:
8440155456 This is Lace Lichen (aka California Spanish Moss and Treebeard Lichen, my namesake!) (Ramalina menziesii hanging on a winter-bare tree of deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata). It also hangs on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees, but it seems to prefer these Valley Oaks - maybe because they are deciduous and let some sunlight in? (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

I had an errand in the Valley today, so I stopped by Lake Cachuma on my way. It's one of my favorite short hikes. It was cloudy but warm with nice soft light. There's no rain in the forecast until maybe the end of the week. Darn, we need it!
CommonNames: Lace Lichen; California Spanish Moss; Treebeard Lichen; Valley Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Ramalina menziesii; Quercus lobata; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Ramalina menziesii; Quercus lobata; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d202b_d202-009.jpg
Notes: 8439028675 This is a new frond of native California Maiden-Hair (Adiantum jordanii) fern in the Polypodiaceae plant family growing in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. The black stems are so smooth you can wear them as earrings if you have pierced ears! They are related to the common Maidenhair Fern (Adiantum capillus-veneria) that grow in wet places, but these grow in the woods. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d202c_d202-021.jpg
Notes: 8440090972 This is a handsome line of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Orobanchaceae plant family, with flowers. (They used to be considered Scrophulariaceae.) They are partial root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. I believe that's native Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua) in the in the Poacea plant family in the background. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Chamise; Nodding Needle Grass
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Nasella cernua; Poacea
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Nasella cernua; Poacea

File.Name: d202d_d202-004.jpg
Notes: 8440047954 I'm still waiting for the first open flowers of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. This bud feels like a hard marble. It's growing in a place that burned a decade or more ago as a "fire follower" plant that lingers. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The red-tinged foliage is as handsome as the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d202e_d202-022.jpg
Notes: 8438903787 This is the shoreline of Lake Cachuma looking east towards Old Man Mountain in the backcountry. The exposed rocks on the shore are Monterey Shale. Lake Cacuma is the third reservoir down from the source of the Santa Ynez River, which is the main water source for Santa Barbara. It's down about 23 feet because of the dry weather this year and last. We've had nice small storms this year, but nothing that would fill a reservoir. February is historically our wettest month, so there's still hope! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d202f_d202-016.jpg
Notes: 8439960170 Here is an absolutely beautiful Cortinarius mushroom that I found in the woods today. I guess it's part of the Cortinarius fulmineus group - at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with yellow colors. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. You can see remnants of the "cortina" of this mushroom on the stem and the margin of the cap, stained reddish-brown from falling spores. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d202g_d202-015.jpg
Notes: 8438817197 I believe these are Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushrooms in the woods growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I sliced the gills with my knife to show the white latex "milk" which doesn't change to yellow. It has a spicy taste that not quite pleasant. The stems are "scrobiculate" with darker and lighter spots. The cap on the left also looks scrobiculate! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d202h_d202-011.jpg
Notes: 8439874326 These are sure look like beautiful Ivory Waxy Cap (Hygrophorus eburneus) mushrooms in the woods, though neither caps nor stems are slimy-viscid. There are some leaves stuck on the mushroom to the right, so maybe they once were slimy? This dry weather makes mushroom identification difficult! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d203a_d203-004.jpg
Notes:
8442391429 Here are more tiny but pretty Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on soil and dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I was surprised to find them today because it feels quite dry, despite the good rain last week. These slime molds are still forming their sporangia, and I won't try to guess what kind they are. They are really tiny, not much larger than the sand grains. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2013)

Julie and I attended a showing this morning of filmmaker Michael Love's new movie The Santa Ynez River Wilderness: a Natural History as part of the 2013 Santa Barbara International Film Festival. I'm in the credits for helping with plant/mushroom/insect names. It's a wonderful film about a place just down the road from us that I know very well. It will be showing again at the San Luis Obispo International Film Festival, March 6-10, 2013. There's a trailer on YouTube. Highly recommended for anyone who loves the Las Padres.

We got up (too) early this morning to have breakfast with the filmmaker and cast. I got home after noon, but still managed to get a couple of photos.
Locality: too) early this morning to have breakfast with the filmmaker and cast.  I got home after noon
CommonNames: Slime Molds; Coast Live Oak; The Santa Ynez River Wilderness: a Natural History

File.Name: d203b_d203-001.jpg
Notes: 8442362439 I found more "mystery mushrooms" in the woods today, growing from a crack in the stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. They are "pleurotid" - growing without a stalk, with a tuft of white mycelium at the base. They are small. I believe you can see a natural spore print below the caps on the left. I have no idea what kind of mushroom they are. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2013)

File.Name: d203c_d202-002.jpg
Notes: 8443403076 I guess these odd mushrooms are the Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin), but I really don't know. They are strange and beautiful, like little hot tubs for gnomes. I found a number of these under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. I picked the cup on the right, and you can see there is no stalk and no ribs at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2013)
CommonNames: Spreading Brown Cup Fungus; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d203d_d202-006.jpg
Notes: 8443373848 I'm not sure, but I think these might be Russula bicolor mushrooms. You can see the typical "strawberries and cream" colors on the caps. These specimens were a bit over the hill so I didn't taste them, but other similar mushrooms I've nibbled are hot or acrid, a bit like wasabi. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d203e_d202-018.jpg
Notes: 8442241015 This is another photo of the stunning Cortinarius mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. I guess it's part of the Cortinarius fulmineus group - at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with yellow colors. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. The remnants of the "cortina" of this mushroom on the stem and the margin of the cap are striking. You can see some root-like "rhizomorphs" - those thick white root-like things at the base of the stalk. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d203f_d202-020.jpg
Notes: 8442224267 These are pretty new flowers of native Milk Maids aka California Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the woods around Lake Cachuma. They are just starting to flower on the pass, but they've been going for a while in the Valley. It's a woodland native in the Brassicaceae) plant family. It's related to the weedy yellow mustards that cover the California hills, but this one is native. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 February 2013)

File.Name: d203g_d201-025.jpg
Notes: 8443304562 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I'm sure there will be many more. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2013)

File.Name: d203h_d201-017.jpg
Notes: 8443279682 Here is a mass of new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the woods. It our earliest flowering plant of the new rain year, with one of the longest flowering periods of any native. I photographed the first flowers this year on October 10 - that's 4+ months so far, and they'll be around for some time yet! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2013)

File.Name: d204a_d204-009.jpg
Notes:
8446981162 This is a small Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today.. I reckon it's the first really new butterfly I've managed to photograph this year, though I did manage to capture a Mourning Cloak in the woods a couple of weeks ago. I guess it was just visiting, but this one is a resident. It can be recognized by the absence of strong markings on its wings, and little chevrons (like army stripes) along the wing margins. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)

Today was another beautiful beautiful day in paradise! There was little wind, and I got photos of a butterfly, an interesting fly, new flowers, mushrooms, and slime molds - all on the same day!
CommonNames: Spring Azure; Coast Live Oak; Mourning Cloak

File.Name: d204b_d204-021.jpg
Notes: 8446956756 I noticed this slender but interesting fly (order Diptera) perched on a dead stem in the woods today. It has hairy legs and its eyes scooped out, so maybe it's a little Robber Fly (family Asilidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)

File.Name: d204c_d204-006.jpg
Notes: 8446921098 Here are more remarkably intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. There's a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with six orange glands at the base, finally surrounded by a circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)

File.Name: d204d_d204-029.jpg
Notes:
8445803213 This is a single new flower of non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. It's a common weed along the roads in the woods, but I don't think it causes any problems. You can add it to your salads. The flowers are tiny, with divided petals. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)

I want to document weeds in our mountains this year. They won't all look as pretty as this one!

File.Name: d204e_d204-003.jpg
Notes: 8446850392 Here is a large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) - crawling over an old cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. Sure, I moved the slug onto the mushrooms (less than a foot away) for a photo. The slug wasn't interested in the mushrooms. Maybe it knows that they are poisonous, with a bitter taste? (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Ariolimax columbianus; Ariolimax californicus; Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax columbianus; Ariolimax californicus; Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d204f_d204-023.jpg
Notes: 8446800984 My first thought was that this is another Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushroom in the woods. But it has yellowish latex when I cut the gills and a white natural spore print on the leaves below, so maybe it's something else? It's definitely genus Lactarius. This one is growing under a clump of grass-like Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family, which does very well in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)
ScientificName: Lactarius rufulus; Lactarius; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Lactarius rufulus; Lactarius; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: d204g_d204-036.jpg
Notes: 8446717764 These are different tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The sporangia are sessile. You see the lime crust of the fruiting bodies breaking up. They seems to have two layers - an outer crusty layer and an inner membranous layer. I guess they are order Physarales, but I won't try to guess beyond that. I believe these are different slime molds from my [Previous] photos, though I found them just a few inches away. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)

File.Name: d204h_d204-033.jpg
Notes: 8446690928 This is another photo of the tiny but pretty Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on soil and dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), taken a day later. Now they have got some color, and it looks like they are starting to develop a coat of lime on their caps. See my [Previous] photo. I'll get another photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2013)

File.Name: d205a_d205-011.jpg
Notes:
8448623905 This is native Deer Grass (Muhlenbergia rigens) in the Poaceae plant family. It's a large perennial bunch grass - three feet tall and twice that across. It does grow on San Marcos Pass in a few secluded places, but it's more common in the interior backcountry. This grass likes a well-watered spot by a creek, but not in the creek. It was an important plant for native American basketry, and it's widely used as a landscaping plant. (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

The weather changed today. It started sunny, but soon became overcast and gloomy. I went to the river hoping to escape the gloom, but it was the same. I walked up the lower part of the Aliso Trail, and found my photos for the day. There's a chance of rain - maybe snow - starting Thursday!

File.Name: d205b_d205-017.jpg
Notes:
8449630794 This is a small Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on the whitish bark of a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) in the Platanaceae plant family. It's just getting started, and you can see the point of attachment. This one is flowering - look at the bottom of the branch on the right. The stumper is how do they get there - seeds fall down, not up! (See below.) (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

Mistletoes have evergreen leaves that do photosynthesis, so they use the host tree mainly for water and mineral nutrients. They have sticky white berries that pass right through birds, who poop them out, maybe onto the trunk of a new tree. I've always wanted to try to start a plant, though I hope pooping is not essential!
ScientificName: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: d205c_d205-022.jpg
Notes:
8448526177 I guess these are "Cortinarius fulmineus" mushrooms - but that's just the generic name I give all these yellow-colored cortinarius mushrooms with viscid caps and a rimmed basal bulb in the subgenus Bulbopodium. They start quite yellow, and end up quite brown. That's the fate of all cortinarius mushrooms. (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

They are large satisfying mushrooms, but give them a few days, and they all look the same. None of them should be eaten because of that. But they are great to photograph!

File.Name: d205d_d205-002.jpg
Notes:
8449591630 I guess these are "Cortinarius glaucopus" mushrooms - but that's just the generic name I give all these blue-colored cortinarius mushrooms with viscid caps and a rimmed basal bulb in the subgenus Bulbopodium. They start quite blue, and end up quite brown. That's the fate of all cortinarius mushrooms. (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

They are large satisfying mushrooms, but give them a few days, and they all look the same. None of them should be eaten because of that. But they are great to photograph!

File.Name: d205e_d205-009.jpg
Notes:
8448464977 These are good-sized Cortinarius mushrooms growing in a deep mulch of oak leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see the rusty-red spore accumulation on the middle cap where the upended mushroom originally grew. These don't seem to have viscid caps, and they don't have a basal bulb. I reckon they are subgenus Leprocybe. (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

These are large satisfying mushrooms, but give them a few days, and they all look the same. None of them should be eaten because of that. But they are great to photograph!

File.Name: d205f_d205-027.jpg
Notes: 8449514398 These are the Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. They are typical "bolete" mushrooms with pores instead of gills. The pores and flesh stain blue when bruised, and the pores are rather large - but it's the reddish cracks in the cap that are most distinctive. It's said to be edible, but not choice - "mushy and insipid". I haven't tried it - there are better boletes in the woods! (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

File.Name: d205g_d205-007.jpg
Notes: 8448393849 How can such a small mushroom such up such a huge "mushrump" - that mound of leaves and woodland detritus! I think it might be a Russula bicolor mushroom with those "strawberries and cream" colors on the cap. The mushrooms are hot or acrid when you nibble them, a bit like wasabi. (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

File.Name: d205h_d205-006.jpg
Notes: 8449445714 Here are more Tricholoma mushrooms in the woods today. I won't try to guess what species they are, but they are large handsome mushrooms with viscid caps when moist. (Tricholoma resplendens comes to mind.) They were growing in deep leaf litter under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I've found them before - see this photo and this photo. (Aliso trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 February 2013)

File.Name: d206a_d206-002.jpg
Notes:
8451109689 This is a tiny spider trying to warm up on a leaf of (I think) native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden this morning. Looking at its markings and eyes, it may well be a Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae, though it didn't see attached to a web. If you look close at its head, you can see pedipalps, chelicera, and fangs! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2013)

There's a really useful page on Spider Eye Arrangements at BugGuide.

I'm posting early and just three photos today because Julie and I are stepping out tonight to hear local singer/songwriter Wil Ridge perform at The Maverick Saloon in Santa Ynez. We've known Wil since he was born - he's another mountain kid born about the same time as our first kid, and they grew up together. He's an essential part of our Live Oak Music Festival music jams every year!

File.Name: d206b_d206-016.jpg
Notes: 8452168328 This is a new flower head of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family just starting to flower today. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia or Southern Umbrellawort, but it deserves a better common name. It has beautiful foliage, and later in the summer it will develop tasty caraway-like seeds. You have to look for it, but it is pretty common in the woods. Once you notice it, you'll find more. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2013)

File.Name: d206c_d206-023.jpg
Notes:
8451052809 Here is an update on the tiny but pretty Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on soil and dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). See my photos here and here. Now they have pretty violet colors. I'm sorry I missed yesterday, but I'll keep taking photos as long as they're interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2013)

These are not little mushrooms, though they look mushroom-like. See this photo for more info about slime molds. They are wonderful to find and photograph. They might be my favorite organisms.

File.Name: d207a_d207-016.jpg
Notes:
8454008833 These look like real red Russula emetica mushrooms in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees, but they may well be Russula mairei, our west coast version. Absolutely beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful creatures!

It was clear and breezy today, and it's become cloudy and windy tonight. There's a chance of rain tonight, and a chance of snow tomorrow! The snow level is forecast to drop to 2500 feet, which includes us.
Locality:
and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful creatures!

It was clear and breezy today
ScientificName: Russula emetica; Quercus agrifolia; Russula mairei
associatedTaxa: Russula emetica; Quercus agrifolia; Russula mairei

File.Name: d207b_d207-007.jpg
Notes: 8455079278 This is a small group of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. They are growing in a bed of moss and Goldback Ferns (Pentagramma triangularis). They are said to glow in the dark, but our west coast mushrooms don't seem to do that. They are quite poisonous - don't eat them! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)
ScientificName: Omphalotus olivascens; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Pentagramma triangularis
associatedTaxa: Omphalotus olivascens; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Pentagramma triangularis

File.Name: d207c_d207-028.jpg
Notes: 8454997508 I found these handsome mushrooms growing under native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family today. They are especially interesting because few mushrooms (or anything else) grows there. Clitocybe comes to mind because of the decurrent gills, but maybe these are Leucopaxillus albissimus? They feel light and airy. If you know about this, please leave a comment. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)
ScientificName: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Clitocybe; Leucopaxillus albissimus
associatedTaxa: Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Clitocybe; Leucopaxillus albissimus

File.Name: d207d_d206-007.jpg
Notes:
8453876263 This is a tiny bullet-headed Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Look close - it's blowing a bubble from its rear end! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2013)

Insects are still scarce in our winter woods. I have to get what I can!

File.Name: d207e_d207-039.jpg
Notes: 8454953082 These are the first flowers I've seen this year of the well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflowers in the Primulaceae plant family. This photo was the best I could get in a difficult situation - deep shade and windy, a tough combination. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

File.Name: d207f_d207-013.jpg
Notes: 8454927684 Here is a handsome group of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Orobanchaceae plant family, with flowers. (They used to be considered Scrophulariaceae.) They are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place, though there was also Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Adenostoma fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Adenostoma fasciculatum

File.Name: d207g_d207-002.jpg
Notes: 8454918184 Here are pretty new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Ever so slowly, spring is coming to the mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

File.Name: d207h_d207-018.jpg
Notes: 8453809243 This is a small forest of different umbrella-like sporophytes of Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on soil close to bedrock sandstone. They might be the same California Liverwort (Asterella californica) I've been showing, but they might be something else, since they have divided star-like sporangia. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

File.Name: d208a_d208-014.jpg
Notes:
8456603045 I believe these are the Deer Mushroom or Fawn Mushroom (Pluteus cervinus) growing from old wood chips dating back to tree work a few years ago. You can see its pink or flesh-colored spores staining the gills. The wood chips are mostly Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's said to be edible and quite good when fresh and firm. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)

We only got a bit over 1/10 inch of rain from our storm today, but we did get a bit of snow! It didn't stick, but it did turn me around from my walk today, and it was enough to keep the weather people honest. It's been cold all day, never more than a few degrees above freezing.
CommonNames: Deer Mushroom; Fawn Mushroom; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d208b_d208-007.jpg
Notes:
8456558971 I believe this is a Yellow-stemmed Pluteus (Pluteus lutescens) mushroom growing from dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It has a textured brown cap, and you can see its pink or flesh-colored spores staining the gills. It broke from the wood during the recent weather, so I posed it for a photo. It's said to be edible, but too small to collect. Very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)

The lichens in the photo are also interesting. They seem to be sprouting little yellow cups of Yellow Fairy Cups or Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae family of fungi. Or are they apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen? Or maybe the fungi are preying on the lichen? I don't know, I'm just the photographer!
Locality: spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen?  Or maybe the fungi are preying on the lichen?  I don't know
CommonNames: Yellow-stemmed Pluteus; Coast Live Oak; Yellow Fairy Cups; Lemon Discos
ScientificName: Pluteus lutescens; Quercus agrifolia; Bisporella citrina; Helotiaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Pluteus lutescens; Quercus agrifolia; Bisporella citrina; Helotiaceae; apothecia

File.Name: d208c_d208-023.jpg
Notes:
8456523897 These appear to be different tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on dead bark of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I tried and tried to get a sharper photo, but this is the best I could do. Maybe it was so cold that my shivering transmitted to my tripod! You can make out the thread-like capillatum in these tiny mushroom-like caps. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)

I found these just a foot from the slime mold in my [Previous] photo, when I was already down on my knees. I get the feeling I could get down on my knees anywhere in the woods and find them! These are not little mushrooms, though they look mushroom-like. See this photo for more info about slime molds. They are wonderful to find and photograph. They might be my favorite organisms.

File.Name: d208d_d208-002.jpg
Notes:
8456493923 Here is another update on the tiny but pretty Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on soil and dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The violet colors have turned to a rich Burgundy wine color. What's next? (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)

See my photos here and here and here.

File.Name: d208e_d208-010.jpg
Notes: 8457577346 Here is another photo of the remarkable Eyelash Cup Fungi in the woods, probably Scutellinia scutellata. They have tiny raindrops on their eyelashes! These are growing on old bark of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). They look like somthing under the sea. You can compare my photo from January 26 of the same little mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)
CommonNames: Eyelash Cup Fungi; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d208f_d208-018.jpg
Notes: 8456460537 I believe these are part of the Psathyrella longipes group of mushrooms in the dark-spored Coprinaceae family. You can see a few white veil remnants on the caps. They grow on debris in the woods, under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The black spore-covered gills don't turn to ink like the genus Coprinus. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)
ScientificName: Psathyrella longipes; Coprinaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Coprinus
associatedTaxa: Psathyrella longipes; Coprinaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Coprinus

File.Name: d208g_d207-032.jpg
Notes: 8457524236 These are the same rusty-spored Cortinarius mushrooms as my [Previous] photo. They have beautiful yellow colors on the caps and stems, as well as the mycelium at the base. It kind of looks like they have a basal bulb, but I think that's just the clump of mycelium. I'm pretty sure they are subgenus Phegmacium with a viscid cap and no basal bulb, but I might be wrong. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

File.Name: d208h_d207-033.jpg
Notes:
8456383591 This is a rusty-spored Cortinarius mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. It's pushing up a remarkably large "mushrump" - that mound of leaves and woodland detritus that the mushroom pushes up. See my [Next] photo for the same mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

Now how do the falling spores cover the mushroom cap like this? It's a stumper! I think of the spores falling like salt from a shaker, but spores are much tinier that salt grains. I guess they are so tiny that falling into air is like jumping into water - they fall up as much as down.

File.Name: d209a_d209-010.jpg
Notes:
8460533748 Here are the first new "flowers" - staminate catkins - of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family - at least the first I've seen this year. These trees in the sun by our mailbox usually flower early, and I don't expect most of our oaks to flower for a few more weeks. Spring is coming, despite the calendar! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2013)

The small storm cleared out today. It was sunny, but cold and windy. I always give the new mushrooms a day or two after a rain. Maybe tomorrow!

File.Name: d209b_d209-008.jpg
Notes: 8460510650 This new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family is in a choke-hold by new tendrils of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. You can see how glandular the flowers are, which distinguishes then from other native gooseberries. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2013)
ScientificName: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; en pointe
associatedTaxa: Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; en pointe

File.Name: d209c_d209-004.jpg
Notes: 8460497704 I believe these are seedling plants of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing in the canyon. They have skinny leaflets, but they are recognizable as lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2013)

File.Name: d209d_d209-005.jpg
Notes: 8459355495 This is a different native Moss (Bryophyta) in the canyon, growing next to our ordinary mosses. It has a sporophyte - or is that coming up from the other moss? This moss has duller green leaves that are more tightly wrapped. I have no idea what species either of these mosses are. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2013)

File.Name: d209e_d209-014.jpg
Notes: 8459317423 This is a small mushroom with yellow cap and gills and reddish-brown stains on both the cap and stem. I think it's an Inocybe mushroom - maybe Inocybe sororia or one of its many kin, though this one doesn't have any particular odor. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2013)
ScientificName: Inocybe; Inocybe sororia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Inocybe; Inocybe sororia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d209f_d208-009.jpg
Notes:
8460350082 This pure white Russula mushroom in the woods has me stumped. It's unnaturally white - very beautiful. Note the other cap coming up next to it, just showing a bit of its gills. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2013)

It seems strange after all the discussion on my previous red-capped russula mushroom photo, but I think this might be Russula cremoricolor - like Russula emetica without the red cap. If you look on the web, you'll find photos of both white and red capped forms. Those darn taxonomists!

File.Name: d209g_d207-023.jpg
Notes: 8459218705 This are pretty Russula mushrooms in the woods. It doesn't come through so well in my photo, but they had delicate yellow colors on the cap. There is a yellow-capped Yellow Swamp Russula (Russula claroflava), but not in California. So this is another mystery mushroom. They are growing under (mostly) Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrubs in the Ericaceae plant family. You can see a few spent flowers among the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)
ScientificName: Russula; Russula claroflava; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Russula; Russula claroflava; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: d209h_d207-025.jpg
Notes: 8460267332 I believe these mushrooms are the Yellow-Staining Milk Cap (Lactarius xanthogalactus, formerly known as Lactarius vinaceorufescens). They have a slightly zoned reddish cap, and white latex that quickly turns yellow when exposed to air. I cut the gills of the mushroom on the left with my pocket knife, and you can see the yellow "milk". They have a spicy hot taste if you nibble, though they are said to cause severe gastrointestinal upset, so don't nibble too much! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2013)

File.Name: d210a_d210-008.jpg
Notes:
8462895487 This is an elegant branch of a native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family, with flowers. The flowers are beautiful, and so is the foliage. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

No mushrooms today. I'll give them another day to grow after our recent rain before I go looking for them. Today started pleasant, but clouds and wind soon moved in, and it ended up quite cold. I'll admit, I'm ready for warmer weather!

File.Name: d210b_d210-037.jpg
Notes: 8463974984 This is an elegant branch of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family, with flowers. The leaves are very pretty. What you first notice in the flowers are the red sepals pointing up. The white petals are below, pointing down. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

File.Name: d210c_d210-039.jpg
Notes: 8462853269 These are new flowers of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family, showing the spiky fruit to be. They have "inferior ovaries" below the flowers (above in this photo) that will swell up to become the berries. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

File.Name: d210d_d210-014.jpg
Notes:
8463888866 This is Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's another of the white-flowered Ceanothus shrubs that cover our mountains every spring. It's not quite flowering, but the flower buds are swelling. There's an out of focus Chaparral Yucca in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

This one is distinctive. It has two-toned opposite dentate leaves that are green on top and white underneath. It also has "corky stipules" (like all subgenus Cerastes shrubs) - those round dark things that look like little brown coconuts at the base of the opposite leaves. It doesn't grow as low as our canyon, but it does grow a couple of miles up East Camino Cielo road past Painted Cave.
Locality: like all subgenus Cerastes shrubs) - those round dark things that look like little brown coconuts at the base of the opposite leaves. It doesn't grow as low as our canyon

File.Name: d210e_d210-005.jpg
Notes: 8463859968 Here is a rather small Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera. It's only about 1/2 inch long, but its legs go way beyond the photo frame. This one was perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. It was cold, and it's trying to get warm. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

File.Name: d210f_d210-050.jpg
Notes: 8462697467 This is an odd group of buds on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are growing from the swelling of an integral stem gall - maybe a gall of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). These galls are notable for integral stem galls because they cause little damage to the growth beyond. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d210g_d210-044.jpg
Notes: 8462647761 I don't know what these odd flaps are on this leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like pancakes with pepper flakes! I guess they are some kind of gall, but they are not in my books, so I'm not sure what critter caused it. I saw several more on the same tree after I found these. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

File.Name: d210h_d210-043.jpg
Notes: 8463693278 I noticed that the odd fang-like gall growing on a leaf bud of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family has an exit hole. See my earlier photo. This kind of oak doesn't usually host many galls in our mountains. It's no doubt caused by a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae) gall, but this gall is not in my books, so I'm not sure what caused it. It's the only one I've found. I've been noticing this gall for two years now, so it's about time something happened! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasp

File.Name: d211a_d211-003.jpg
Notes:
8466001725 This is a beautiful light-colored Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. It has a collar-like volva that adheres to the base except for a free rim. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest, but the are not dangerous to handle or photograph!

Today was another beautiful day in paradise! I have photos of interesting mushrooms, insects, and flowers. *Sigh* Life is good.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: d211b_d211-005.jpg
Notes:
8465973267 This is a small, light-colored Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. It has a collar-like volva that adheres to the base except for a free rim. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest, but the are not dangerous to handle or photograph!
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: d211c_d211-023.jpg
Notes: 8467026776 This is "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods today. You can see my fingerprints outlined in "red blush" color where I picked the mushroom! It doesn't seem to have any visible volva, though it has an unmistakable "amanita" aura. It looks a little fuzzy under the cap - maybe the first signs of a Hypomyces fungus infection? (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

File.Name: d211d_d211-015.jpg
Notes: 8466970866 This is the same remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes). This one was perched on a dead leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family this morning. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under it's head. It's a bit underexposed, but it fits the subject, like film noir. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d211e_d211-002.jpg
Notes: 8465846941 Here is another photo of the small Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera that I showed yesterday - but I got this photo this morning! The fly moved a couple of leaves over on its perch on Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This one appears to have an ovipositor, so I reckon it's a female. It has a dour philosopher's face. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

File.Name: d211f_d210-034.jpg
Notes: 8465813877 Just to complete the inventory of local Ribes species, here's another photo of new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the woods. The individual flowers don't hang pendant like those in my [Previous] photos, but whole clusters of them do. These flowers are also glandular. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

File.Name: d211g_d211-012.jpg
Notes: 8466895872 This is a beautiful flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The whole plant is glandular, which distinguishes it from other local gooseberries. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

File.Name: d211h_d210-011.jpg
Notes: 8466873944 This is another photo of the same native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in my [Previous] photo, covered with flowers. The branch I showed before is in the top left. Just beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2013)

File.Name: d212a_d212-014.jpg
Notes:
8468996831 Here is a beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata perched on a twig of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's our winter resident dragonfly, though this is the first one I've seen this year. Spring is still a month away by the calendar, but it's coming! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)

It was a beautiful day today - there's still a chill in the air, but it's definitely warmer. It feels like spring. Today's photo are insects, mushrooms, and new flowers.
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d212b_d212-004.jpg
Notes: 8470075360 This little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) is grooming its wings on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) this morning. I got this quick photo and it flew away. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2013)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d212c_d212-025.jpg
Notes:
8468923339 I found there remarkably colorful mushrooms in the woods today. I think they might be the Blue-green Stropharia (Stropharia aeruginosa) mushroom, though these specimens were old. They have blue-green stalks and caps. I'm taking a spore print right now, but it's going slow with those old caps. They are beautiful and surprising! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)

If you're thinking "Stropharia cubensis" - well, so was I! But I don't think these mushrooms are that Stropharia or any Psilocybe mushroom. For one thing, they don't have that "fleeting blue" color reaction - they are blue! And the spore color is wrong. (See below.)

*Update* I did a spore print overnight, and the spores look dull brown. I thought as much from the gills and stains on the caps. I really don't know what this mushroom is, but it's striking!
Locality:
See below.)

*Update* I did a spore print overnight

File.Name: d212d_d212-032.jpg
Notes:
8468864951 I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in this photo to show how large the mushroom stalk on the right is! When I first saw it, I really thought it was a mushroom growing on top of a tree stump. I guess these are Cortinarius mushrooms, but I have no idea what kind they are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)

The green plants in the photo are seedlings of Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a non-native weed that has become a nasty invader of the woodlands in our mountains. It seems especially bad in the Paradise area.

File.Name: d212e_d212-030.jpg
Notes: 8469925084 These are different Tricholoma mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. They have a faint "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. They might be part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group, not recommended for the table. They are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk. The mushroom on the right broke when I tried to dig it out. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)

File.Name: d212f_d212-021.jpg
Notes:
8469878850 These are sweet-smelling white flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family, just starting to open. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! They have opposite leaves and (mostly) yellow floral disks. I've been seeing the flowers on hillsides for the last week or two, but I had to wait to find a flowering shrub near a road or trail. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)

It's interesting that these are a dominate shrub on the north-facing slopes of the mountains, but they are replaced by Ceanothus megacarpus on the south-facing slopes with alternate leaves. (See this photo.) They look almost identical.

You can make soap from the flowers - a handful of flowers will make suds when you rub them in water! It's a fun trick when you're taking kids in the woods.

File.Name: d212g_d212-015.jpg
Notes: 8468732267 These are tiny flowers of non-native Shepherd's Purse (Capsella bursa-pastoris) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's another weedy mustard plant that has become part of our flora. It has neat little heart-shaped seed heads that reminded someone of little purses. Flowers and fruit are tasty if you strip them from the tough stems. You have to think not to overexpose these little white flowers. This photo was down 3 stops from what my camera thought was right. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)

File.Name: d212h_d212-006.jpg
Notes:
8469798786 If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - be very careful with Thistles!

I believe this a new plant of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has beautiful red flowers in late spring and early summer. (See this photo.) The weedy Bull Thistles (Cirsium vulgare) looks superficially similar when they are young. It would be terrible to pull the beautiful natives by mistake. If you have any doubts, don't pull it! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)
CommonNames: Thistles; Red Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Bull Thistles
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium vulgare

File.Name: d213a_d213-011.jpg
Notes:
8471653511 This is a pretty new flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. I had to prop the flower up with a stick because they always hang downward, facing the Earth. The perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2013)

Today marks the first anniversary of my Flickr account, though I posted photos on Fotolog for many years before this. I've been trying to post 8 photos every day, partly just to see if it's possible, mostly within a few miles of home. It is possible. We live in a remarkable natural area. I'm retired now, and there's always something interesting to photograph in the woods!

File.Name: d213b_d213-019.jpg
Notes: 8472734638 This must be the smallest native plant in our woods - Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's a rarely-noticed tiny succulent that grows on bare ground in the woods. I put my pen in the photo for scale with a 0.07 mm ball, so the leaves are just 1-2 mm long. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2013)

File.Name: d213c_d213-014.jpg
Notes: 8471618541 Here are shiny new leaves and flower buds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch if you are sensitive! It is just beginning to leaf out at home. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2013)

File.Name: d213d_d213-004.jpg
Notes: 8471584913 These are the tiny new flowers and fruit of native Common Peppergrass (Lepidium nitidum) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The whole plant is only an inch or two tall. The notched seed pods are much larger than the flowers. True to it's name, the speckled seeds have a pleasant spicy taste. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2013)

File.Name: d213e_d213-017.jpg
Notes: 8471563287 Here are more pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms in the woods today. They have dark caps which I associate with the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin mushrooms, but I didn't notice that characteristic chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool. So I guess they fall into the "Entoloma rhodopolium" group. These are growing underCoast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2013)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Entoloma nidorosum; Entoloma rhodopolium; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Entoloma nidorosum; Entoloma rhodopolium; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d213f_d211-030.jpg
Notes: 8472632908 This is another Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus) mushroom, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. They are slimy mushrooms with the remains of a universal slime veil on the cap and stem. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places them in the subgenus Myxacium. You can see remnants of the "cortina" hairs on the stems. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

File.Name: d213g_d211-024.jpg
Notes:
8472632908 This is a good-sized Cortinarius mushroom growing in a deep mulch of oak leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I put my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. It appears to be subgenus Cortinarius or Phlegmacium - there is debris stuck to the cap, so maybe it was once viscid. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2013)

It's a large satisfying mushroom, but give it a few days, and it looks like any other Cortinarius mushroom. None of them should be collected for the table because of that, unless you feel like experimenting. But they are great to photograph!
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius; Phlegmacium; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius; Phlegmacium; Cortinarius

File.Name: d213h_d212-012.jpg
Notes: 8471466793 Here is another photo of the beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata I found yesterday. This time it's perched on a twig of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. (It kept moving as I bugged it!) It's our winter resident dragonfly, though this is the first one I've seen this year. Spring is still a month away by the calendar, but it's coming! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 February 2013)
CommonNames: Variegated Meadowhawk; Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: d214a_d214-005.jpg
Notes:
8474210255 These are surprisingly beautiful grape-sized galls of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, coated in a layer of rain. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. There is also a new bud of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family in the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

I've photographed these same galls before - see this photo.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Nuttall's Snowberry
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: d214b_d214-012.jpg
Notes: 8475285300 These are pretty sporophytes of a native Moss (Bryophyta) in the canyon today. They are growing on a sandstone rock. I don't know anything about the taxonomy of mosses, but I can recognize beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

File.Name: d214c_d214-018.jpg
Notes: 8475247768 These are different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon. They are smaller and more tightly packed than California Liverworts (Asterella californica) I've been showing. They are turning black from the dry weather, but it's not fatal - they will green up quickly after new rain. They are not dead, just going dormant with pretty purple colors. These are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

File.Name: d214d_d214-021.jpg
Notes: 8475233858 This is a handsome "crustose" lichen growing on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I assume the disks are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. They can't reproduce the lichen - only the fungus. There's a different lichen in the photo with little red caps that may well be the apothecia of their fungi.
Locality: 8475233858 This is a handsome "crustose" lichen growing on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I assume the disks are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. They can't reproduce the lichen - only the fungus. There's a different lichen in the photo with little red caps that may well be the apothecia of their fungi.

File.Name: d214e_d214-010.jpg
Notes:
8475196678 I can't resist posting yet another photo of this remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes). It's perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family this morning. You can see its mighty proboscis tucked under it's head. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

Insects are still scarce in the woods, and this is the most interesting one I've seen for a while! There are also Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterflies in the woods, but they haven't wanted to pose for me.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Sara Orangetip
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Anthocharis sara
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Anthocharis sara

File.Name: d214f_d214-001.jpg
Notes: 8474095083 This is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae). It's munching a tiny fly on its inner web. There's no mistaking the bottle-neck shape of this spider's body! I know it's not quite sharp, it was breezy this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

File.Name: d214g_d213-013.jpg
Notes: 8474083367 Here's another photo of the pretty new flowers of native Milk Maids aka California Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the woods around Lake Cachuma. It's a woodland native in the Brassicaceae) plant family. It's related to the weedy yellow mustards that cover the California hills, but this one is native. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2013)

File.Name: d214h_d213-018.jpg
Notes: 8475147906 Here is a whole plant of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. There are flower buds, but no open flowers like my [Previous] photo. The perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers. They also have beautiful foliage. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 February 2013)

File.Name: d215a_d215-014.jpg
Notes:
8476973369 Call them what you will - Russula emetica, Russula cremoricolor, Russula mairei, Russula bicolor, or Russula californiensis - these are absolutely beautiful mushrooms in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees! They have deep red caps like apples. I just want a name I can hang on these mushrooms, and "Russula emetica" works for me! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. They are one of our most beautiful creatures!

I didn't notice when I took the photo, but there are tiny slime molds on the leaf next to the mushroom on the left.

*Sigh* Today was another beautiful day in paradise, but the weather continues very dry. We're down to 68% normal rain this year. Our next chance of rain comes early next week.
Locality:
and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. They are one of our most beautiful creatures!

I didn't notice when I took the photo

File.Name: d215b_d215-006.jpg
Notes: 8478039332 I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. They are strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushrooms in the woods today. Sometimes when you brush against the caps, you can see them release a little puff of spores. They are said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. (Any reports FlyGrl67?) (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

File.Name: d215c_d215-028.jpg
Notes:
8477981710 Bolete mushrooms have been scarce this year, and when I find one I can't ID it!

Well, maybe this is the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus)? It has a dry pink to tan cap, rather large pores, and a red stem that it yellow at the apex. The pores darkened a bit when I scratched them, but they didn't turn blue. This mushroom has a "fungus infection" - the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus is starting on the upper-right. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

File.Name: d215d_d215-017.jpg
Notes:
8476833047 I would have taken this for moss, but RandomTruth enlightened me that they are really different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the genus Fossombronia. They have a ruffled thallus that looks like lettuce. See RandomTruth's photo of the sporophytes. I was hoping the dry weather would bring out the seed heads, but not yet. It might be uncommon in our mountains, but it's easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

There's a neat timelapse YouTube video of the little seed capsules.

File.Name: d215e_d215-020.jpg
Notes:
8477887372 We had lizard weather today!

This is a small Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) catching some rays on a sandstone outcrop. It seems to be missing part of its tail. You can see its "third eye" on the back of its head, and a "mystery spot" that looks like a perfect letter "e" just behind its rear legs. I added "notes". (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

Today was the first day this winter when I didn't at least start out with a heavy flannel shirt to keep warm. Darn, the woods are drying out.

File.Name: d215f_d215-001.jpg
Notes: 8476784867 This is an impressive Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae hiding under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Insects and spiders are just starting to appear in our winter woods. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

File.Name: d215g_d215-018.jpg
Notes: 8476762209 Here is a beautiful yellow flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's a fantastic head-high (or more) native shrub that has usually has flowers most of the year, though they took a break in this dry year. If you look close, there's a tiny spider stretched out on the edge of the lower-left petal. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

File.Name: d215h_d215-007.jpg
Notes: 8477836854 These are pretty new flowers of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Lamiaceae plant family starting to flower. Pretty as they are, it's a non-native weed that does spread into the woods where it shouldn't be, though it usually sticks to disturbed places like trails and roadsides. They sometimes grow like wildflowers, making colorful splashes in the fields. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2013)

File.Name: d216a_d216-007.jpg
Notes:
8481022616 This is a beautiful little moth (Lepidoptera) - "microlepidoptera" for sure - perched on a twig of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Julie says it looks like a fish. It would be perfectly camouflaged on a grayer twig, but I noticed it on this redder one. It has white-dotted scales, like the prized hackles of a rooster for fly fishing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

It was unnaturally warm today - up to 74 ∞F according to a neighbor's weather station, ridiculous for February. But the forecast is for rain on Tuesday and Wednesday with snow levels down to 2000 feet, well below us!

File.Name: d216b_d216-003.jpg
Notes:
8480986538 With that petioled abdomen, I guess this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are waking up on a warm day after a long winter nap. This one is perched on pretty foliage of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

It may just be waking up, but it's not groggy! I only had one chance at the photo before it took off!
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: d216c_d216-001.jpg
Notes: 8479875197 Here is another Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae hanging from its webbing. This one is a lady - you can see her dingus-like epigynum in the middle, just behind where her abdomen meets her cephalothorax. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

File.Name: d216d_d216-031.jpg
Notes:
8479838253 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I think my favorite part of them is the pinhead-dotted glandular calyces and bracts in the whorls of the flowers. Hummingbirds like them. Very beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

I'm sure I'll get a photo of the whole plant with flowers in the future. It's a low-growing perennial that sprouts from underground almost-woody stems. They have fragrant leaves that make decent tea, especially with a bit of honey. It's one of my favorite plants.

File.Name: d216e_d216-027.jpg
Notes: 8479795643 This is a compound umbel of native Tauschia arguta flowers in the Apiaceae plant family. It's an umbel of umbels! These humble umbels are quite attractive, almost as pretty as the foliage. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia or Southern Umbrellawort, but it deserves a better common name. I'll post a photo of the whole plant in a day or two. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

File.Name: d216f_d216-002.jpg
Notes: 8480862038 These are new galls of the Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) growing on stems of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This is another "integral stem gall" that is part of the stem, so you can't break it off. These stems haven't ruptured yet. So far at least, the branches beyond the galls seem fine. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)
ScientificName: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d216g_d214-024.jpg
Notes:
8480815368 This is a fallen gall of Gouty Stem Gall Wasps (Callirhtis quercussuttoni) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera riddled with the exit holes of the tiny gall wasps, their inquilines, and parasites. It's really an apartment building for wasps with many inhabitants! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

I found this one on the ground under native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. It's fairly large, like a small potato. It's an "integral stem gall" - the kind you can't break off without breaking the branch. It usually kills the branch beyond the gall. I often find them on the ground like this, so I guess it kills the stem behind as well.
Locality: Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. It's fairly large
CommonNames: Gouty Stem Gall Wasps; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: d216h_d214-023.jpg
Notes:
8479699565 This is another apple-red Russula emetica-type mushroom. I know it's probably not the real Russula emetica - maybe it's Russula cremoricolor, Russula mairei, Russula bicolor, or Russula californiensis - take your pick! Whatever they are, they are absolutely beautiful mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2013)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. There are rumors of people pickling them, and I'm interested in that. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. They are one of our most beautiful creatures!
ScientificName: Russula emetica; Russula emetica; Russula cremoricolor; Russula mairei; Russula bicolor; Russula californiensis; Quercus agrifolia
