File.Name: d217a_d217-007.jpg
Notes:
8483796387 I found this furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today, perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I don't know anything else about it, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

Today was another unseasonably warm day in the 70s, but the weather is changing. Clouds are moving in and the temperature is falling tonight. Much-needed rain is in the forecast, and maybe even snow. It's a good reality check for these warm days we've been having.

File.Name: d217b_d217-039.jpg
Notes:
8483749353 This is one of those tiny Flies (Diptera) working a female pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek today. See my [Previous] photo. I wasn't expecting this photo to bee so good! The flowers were to high for my tripod, so this is a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

The flies look like Dance Flies in the family Empididae, but they might also be atypical Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae. I don't know. I have a better photo of the (I think the same) fly here.
CommonNames: Flies; Arroyo Willow; Dance Flies; Bee Flies
ScientificName: Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae

File.Name: d217c_d217-040.jpg
Notes: 8484779120 These are new female pistillate catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek today. There are tiny Flies (Diptera) working the flowers. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

File.Name: d217d_d217-016.jpg
Notes:
8483598673 I believe this is a "Dog-Lichen" in the genus Peltigera, but I'm not sure. You can see one the typical tooth-like structures along the edge of the lichen on the right. These are apothecia, the fruiting bodies of the fungal part of the lichen. What's more remarkable is those "teeth" hanging from the ribbed underside of the lichen. They are tiny. I don't know what those are. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

These lichens are uncommon in our mountains. I only know them from this one place, though you have to get down on your hands and knees to see them. The photo isn't as sharp as I like, but you can see it well enough!

File.Name: d217e_d217-044.jpg
Notes:
8484613778 Here is another update on the tiny but pretty Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on soil and dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). They have developed a spongy capillatum in the tiny mushroom-like caps, like the fluff in a vacuum cleaner bag. Maybe they are Arcyria denudata or kin? Thanks to Claude Kaufholtz-C. for the suggestion! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

Here are my photos of this cluster on slime molds, from the beginning. It's an interesting transition. Some slime molds mature overnight, but these are slow.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; capillatum; Arcyria denudata
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; capillatum; Arcyria denudata

File.Name: d217f_d217-029.jpg
Notes: 8483487635 These are unusual pure white flowers of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflowers in the Primulaceae plant family. Usually the flowers are pretty maroon or violet colors, see this photo. Well, that happens. I wonder if they breed true? Maybe White Flower Farm would be interested! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

File.Name: d217g_d217-011.jpg
Notes: 8484546636 The topmost flower of this native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family seems to be forgetting what kind of flower it is! Pistil and stamens are sticking out, but usually they are contained within the tubular flowers so that only the tip of the pistil sticks out. Look at the flowers at 2 and 4 o'clock. I saw this a few times today. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

File.Name: d217h_d217-020.jpg
Notes: 8483437819 These are beautiful new leaves of the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I found in the woods today. The broad leaves come first, then whither away before they send up flower stalks in summer. I never saw the flowers in our last dry year, but I never hiked through here in the summer. I wish I had. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

File.Name: d218a_d218-003.jpg
Notes:
8487016243 I believe these are new flowers of Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The flowers are quite tiny, only a mm or two across. The seed pods become long cylinders. I believe this tiny mustard is native, but it grows like a weed in our canyon. It's apparently native to California. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2013)

This was my only keeper photo today. The weather turned gloomy and breezy as our new storm started to move in. Much needed rain (and maybe snow!) is expected later tonight and tomorrow.

File.Name: d218b_d217-037.jpg
Notes: 8488035040 I believe these are new staminate flowers of our other Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae family growing in the mountains. These are "cucumber" plants, but you wouldn't want to eat the spiny fruit. These flowers are smaller and flatter and more yellow than the very similar Marah macrocarpus, compare this photo. Marah macrocarpus grows as a fire-follower on the south-facing slopes of our mountains, while Marah fabaceus grows more in the back mountains. Up here on San Marcos Pass, we get them both. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)
ScientificName: Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah macrocarpus; Marah macrocarpus; Marah fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Marah macrocarpus; Marah macrocarpus; Marah fabaceus

File.Name: d218c_d216-025.jpg
Notes: 8486898523 Here is most of a whole plant of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family with flowers. It has beautiful foliage and makes tasty seeds later in the year. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia or Southern Umbrellawort, but it deserves a better common name. See this photo of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

File.Name: d218d_d217-001.jpg
Notes: 8486865845 This is a rocky bank covered with the different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants that I've shown before. They are turning black from the dry weather we've been having, but it's not fatal - they will green up quickly after the new rain we get later tonight. They are not dead, just going dormant. These are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

File.Name: d218e_d217-014.jpg
Notes: 8487925486 This is the same "Dog-Lichen" in the genus Peltigera that I showed yesterday. If you look close, you can see the strange "teeth" hanging from the ribbed underside of the lichen - on the left edge, the bottom, and the right edge. These lichens seem to be uncommon in our mountains. I only know them from this one place. They are easy to miss - you have to get down on your hands and knees to see them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

File.Name: d218f_d216-011.jpg
Notes:
8487804116 This is a dead Woodlouse curled around the stem of a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess this is the woodlouse that can roll up in a ball, so maybe it's genus Armadillidium with those smaller rear segments? These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda of the subphylum Crustacea. They are known as pill bugs, sow bugs, roly-polys and many more common names. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

Usually these common critters are gray - so why is this one colored white with some blue? Maybe it was infected with a fatal Iridovirus that changes their color as it kills them. I've found bluer ones - see this photo. I've also heard stories about internal parasites that change the color and behavior of woodlice so that they seek sunlight and develop bright colors so that birds will eat them. The birds then get the parasite and pass it back to the woodlice through their droppings. Ain't nature strange!
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Armadillidium; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Iridovirus
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Armadillidium; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Iridovirus

File.Name: d218g_d216-010.jpg
Notes: 8486684997 This is an odd little nest - like a white blister pack - on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I don't know whose nest it is. There's a hole on the side, but is an exit hole or the entrance hole of some parasite? It's about 1/4 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2013)

File.Name: d218h_d217-019.jpg
Notes:
8487711796 The dry weather is taking a toll on our mushrooms. These are creamy white Russula mushrooms - very beautiful. I think they might be Russula cremoricolor - like Russula emetica without the red cap, though they have a different aspect - taller. Looking at the photo on my computer screen, I wish I had gone in for a very close shot the the wavy gills on the mushroom on the right which is showing the effects of the dry weather. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2013)

The forecast is for rain starting late tonight. We need it.

File.Name: d219a_d219-030.jpg
Notes:
8490213613 I had a close encounter with this handsome Bobcat (Lynx rufus) today! Here it is, looking to the side in a mighty pose. It wasn't worried, but more annoyed by the encounter. It has a remarkable face, not quite like a kitty cat. If you look close, you can make out the black tufts on its ears. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2013)

This bobcat is standing behind native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius, Fabaceae) in a clump of non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) in the same family. I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens. I was that close!

I was hoping to wake up with rain this morning, but it was just gloomy and breezy. I went for a drive in the mountains. The rain started about 4:00 when I got back. Not much rain - about 2 tenths of an inch so far. It's cold, at freezing as I write this. It may well snow tonight.
Locality:
Spartium junceum) in the same family. I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens. I was that close!

I was hoping to wake up with rain this morning

File.Name: d219b_d219-031.jpg
Notes:
8490149479 I had a close encounter with this handsome Bobcat (Lynx rufus) today! Here it is, looking right at me. It wasn't worried, but more annoyed by the encounter. It has a remarkable face, not quite like a kitty cat. If you look close, you can make out the black tufts on its ears. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2013)

This bobcat is standing behind native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius, Fabaceae) in a clump of non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) in the same family. I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens. I was that close!

File.Name: d219c_d219-002.jpg
Notes: 8491224166 Here is a tiny Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I first thought it was a bee, but I see no sign of pollen-collecting hairs on its rear legs. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2013)

File.Name: d219d_d219-006.jpg
Notes: 8490101659 These are pretty new flower heads of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceaeplant family - formerly considered in the Scrophulariaceae. (Like Flickr, I have to learn new ways!) The red color is from the leafy bracts. The real flowers are yellow-green, with an exerted pistil. It's a subshrub with woolly white hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2013)

File.Name: d219e_d219-023.jpg
Notes:
8491134588 Here are new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's another of the white-flowered Ceanothus shrubs that cover our mountains every spring that is just starting to flower. It has opposite leaves and "corky stipules" - those dark things at the base of the leaves. The leaves are toothed and whitish underneath. If you look close, you can spot a small green beetle in the center of my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2013)

The different white-flowered Ceanothus species seem to have the mountains covered - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits.
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius

File.Name: d219f_d219-011.jpg
Notes: 8489992743 These are the sunny new flowers of native composite Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae) plant family. I don't think they really belong in the mountains. They usually grow below 1500 feet. I first noticed them up here a few years ago after the Gap Wildfire, and I suspect they were introduced by the earth-moving equipment. But here they are in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2013)

File.Name: d219g_d219-019.jpg
Notes: 8491070100 This is another beautiful new flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. This one is growing on San Marcos Pass in a place that burned ten or more years ago. The flowers are walnut-sized, and dense as a nut. They are not common in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2013)

File.Name: d219h_d219-021.jpg
Notes: 8491054748 These are new flowers of well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family. They are really beautiful, especially that crinkly purple tissue at the base of the anthers. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2013)

File.Name: d220a_d220-011.jpg
Notes:
8494328152 This is another photo of "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods today. I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. The mushroom is large and beautiful. It develops pink colors on its cap and stem with age and handling. In some years, it's an early mushroom - but it fruits again later in the spring, like now. It doesn't seem to have any visible volva, though it has an unmistakable "amanita aura". (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2013)

We ended up with just 1/4 inch of rain last night, and it was very windy today as the storm blew through. The rain year started promising through December, but January and February (so far), which should be our wettest months, have been dry. This is a repeat of last year. I hope we also have a wet March and April, like last year.

Two photos are fine tonight. I had a SYVNHS board meeting tonight, and I'm tired and hungry.

File.Name: d220b_d220-001.jpg
Notes: 8494273960 This is the same rocky bank covered with different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants that I showed two days ago. Try flipping back and forth between the two photos. In the earlier photo they are quite black from the dry weather we've been having. We had a bit of rain last night, and in this photo taken today they are already greening up. They are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2013)

File.Name: d221a_d221-021.jpg
Notes:
8495911623 This might be our most uncommon woodland habitat. It's a dry "grassland" composed almost entirely of native sedges in the Cyperaceae plant family - maybe the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae). It's a beautiful place, but I never find mushrooms or wildflowers here. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

It was still frosty this morning, but the sun was out and the wind died down. I went looking for mushrooms in remote places in our canyon, but most of what I found were old.

File.Name: d221b_d221-001.jpg
Notes: 8497001056 This is another surprisingly beautiful grape-sized gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It is growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know why the galls on this little shrub are so pretty, but they are. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

File.Name: d221c_d221-004.jpg
Notes: 8496954658 Please don't buy these shiny mylar helium-filled "birthday balloons". They always get loose, and a surprising number on them end up in our woods in the mountains. I'll bet I could find 20 of the balloons today if I really looked. The mylar finally breaks up into smaller pieces, but I don't think it ever breaks down. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

File.Name: d221d_d221-012.jpg
Notes: 8496924260 This is a cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the wood of a fallen tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. They are said to glow in the dark, but our west coast mushrooms don't seem to do that. They have a greenish tinge as a warning. They are quite poisonous - don't eat them! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

File.Name: d221e_d221-025.jpg
Notes: 8495780875 I guess this is the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) mushroom dropping its veil in the woods today. It has a pure white cap without brown scales. The cap slowly stains yellow, but the base of the stem doesn't. It has a "double-ring" with patches on the underside in a cogwheel pattern. It has a mild smell and taste, and may well be good for the table - but I'm wary of all our woodland species. "Once bitten, twice warned!" (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

File.Name: d221f_d221-007.jpg
Notes: 8495733477 I guess these are Leucopaxillus albissimus mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Species of Leucopaxillus are said to be resistant to rot, and these feel light and airy, like they are made of foam. Clitocybe also comes to mind because of the decurrent gills, but they are more solid. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)
ScientificName: Leucopaxillus albissimus; Quercus agrifolia; Leucopaxillus; Clitocybe
associatedTaxa: Leucopaxillus albissimus; Quercus agrifolia; Leucopaxillus; Clitocybe

File.Name: d221g_d221-014.jpg
Notes:
8495667409 These are the strange antler-like fruiting bodies of Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) - or do they look more like the grasping hands of the dead? I'm sure they are growing from buried wood under the leaves. There are many other species that you need a microscope to distinguish, so this ID is really just a placeholder. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

My main mushroom book - David Arora's "Mushrooms Demystified" - says that "the powdered appearance of young specimens is caused by masses of asexual spores (conidia) that form directly on hyphae instead of in asci. Later on, the masses of asexual spores form inside "flasks" (perithecia) at the top of the fruiting body." I guess these are the pink fingers on the tips of the fungi. Compare my earlier photo from December of the same fungi.
Locality: conidia) that form directly on hyphae instead of in asci.  Later on

File.Name: d221h_d221-010.jpg
Notes: 8495645411 This is a another photo of "The Blusher" - a different well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods today. It develops blushing pink colors on its cap and stem with age and handling. If you look close, you can see that the flesh of the cap also blushes. It's beautiful. It might be edible, but it's definitely not recommended. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)

File.Name: d222a_d222-028.jpg
Notes:
8498547291 This is another beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly - the first I've seen this year. This one is perched on a twig of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family, though I reckon that's just a place to perch. It's a stunning butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

I walked up the high ridge above Paradise Creek today looking for hill-topping butterflies, and I found these. I don't know if they're hill-topping or just on a hill top. I reckon they are waiting for the native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius, Fabaceae) to flower, but they'll have to wait longer. The cold weather has been slowing everything down.
ScientificName: Callophrys dumetorum; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Callophrys dumetorum; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: d222b_d222-024.jpg
Notes: 8498519481 This is a beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly - the first I've seen this year. It's perched on a twig of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), though I reckon that's just a place to perch. It's a stunning butterfly. The wings look like they've been inlaid with tiny chips of turquoise! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d222c_d222-010.jpg
Notes:
8499571618 I found this beautiful Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera) this morning on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Maybe it's the genus Platypedia? This photo looks like flash, but this is just the usual brilliant morning sunlight. I don't like flash, and rarely use it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

We have different cicadas later in summer.

File.Name: d222d_d222-023.jpg
Notes: 8499542704 This is a "Woolly Bear" - the caterpillar of a Tiger Moth (family Arctiidae) in the order Lepitoptera. It crossed my path today over sandy soil. It was in a hurry, moving right along! This one looks quite brown, but other ones are black. Its head is on the left. I reckon the bristly hairs are defense against Tachinid Flies. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)
CommonNames: Woolly Bear; Tiger Moth; Tachinid Flies

File.Name: d222e_d222-037.jpg
Notes: 8498406401 This is a native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) getting *something* from a galled or rolled leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The ant was quite insistent - even when I knocked the branch with my camera, it came right back. Note that the flowers are getting close to opening. I've seen them lower down in the mountains, but not yet on the Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d222f_d222-002.jpg
Notes: 8498368357 These are native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on a flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. You can see how glandular the flowers are. It's curious how the ants are attracted to a single flower, when there are many to choose from. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d222g_d222-006.jpg
Notes:
8498353743 This is a flock of tiny Whitefly pupae on the underside of a leaf of a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi) in the family Aleyrodidae in the old order Homoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

See p.44-5 of "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" at:
http://www.fs.fed.us/psw/publications/documents/psw_gtr197/psw_gtr197.pdf
This is a useful document!

I just realized that I posted almost the same photo last October. They live a pretty boring life!
CommonNames: Whitefly; Coast Live Oak; Stanfordís Whitefly
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tetraleurodes stanfordi; Aleyrodidae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Tetraleurodes stanfordi; Aleyrodidae; Homoptera

File.Name: d222h_d222-034.jpg
Notes: 8498330845 I guess these bumps are "Erineum Galls", probably made by Eriophyid mites in the genus Eriophyes of the Acari. I found them today on a shiny leaf of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are rather pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d223a_d223-016.jpg
Notes:
8501436279 These are a group of different Lichens growing together on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. I don't know much about the taxonomy of lichens, but they were about the only things not blowing around in the wind today! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2013)

It was very windy today - a tough day for photos, but I took a walk and got a few. *Sigh* the wind is sucking out all the moisture from our recent small rain. It's a tough time for new mushrooms.

File.Name: d223b_d223-010.jpg
Notes: 8501375409 This is a handsome Crustose Lichen - actually several different lichens - growing on a sandstone outcrop in the woods today. I guess the little orange cups are the apothecia - the fruiting bodies of the fungal part of the lichen. I don't know much about the taxonomy of lichens, but they were about the only things not blowing around in the wind today! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2013)

File.Name: d223c_d223-034.jpg
Notes: 8501359237 These are the first new flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family I've seen this year - at least the first I've seen up here in the mountains. Some shrubs have bluer flowers, and some have almost white flowers. They are quite variable. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2013)

File.Name: d223d_d222-017.jpg
Notes: 8501345605 Here are new flowers of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. Well, I guess they are still buds, but they're almost flowers. The leaves are quite distinctive - toothed on top, but not below. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d223e_d223-026.jpg
Notes:
8501283627 Snug as a bug in a ... cellophane wrapper??

I guess this is a little beetle grub in the order Coleoptera, with frass. It's hiding under a transparent cover on the underside of a leaf of a different native Scrub Oak - Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in the Fagaceae plant family. The leaves of this scrub oak are thinner and shiny underneath, quite different from Q. berberidifolia or Q. chrysolepis. I have no idea what kind of beetle this is, but it has a remarkable nest. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2013)

*Sigh* I took about 40 pictures to get this one keeper. It was windy today, and everything was moving. I couldn't afford to do this with film!
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Q. berberidifolia; Q. chrysolepis
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Q. berberidifolia; Q. chrysolepis

File.Name: d223f_d222-041.jpg
Notes: 8502328938 This is the tiny nymph of a Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. It's on a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I know this isn't the best photo, but this critter was really tiny! This is a 1:1 crop from my original photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d223g_d222-003.jpg
Notes: 8502313738 Look close! There is a native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) at the bottom of the photo going after *something* on the pistils of this flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2013)

File.Name: d223h_d221-016.jpg
Notes: 8502266264 This is an odd Crampball (Daldinia grandis or kin) fungus growing on the dead bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks and feels like a lump of charcoal. The lumps on the surface are the spore-bearing "perithecia" of this odd member of the Pyrenomycetes group of the fungi. There are little green mosses growing on the broken part. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2013)
ScientificName: Daldinia grandis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; perithecia; Pyrenomycetes
associatedTaxa: Daldinia grandis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; perithecia; Pyrenomycetes

File.Name: d224a_d224-022.jpg
Notes:
8506354436 These are beautiful new flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family. They are the first I've seen this year. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 February 2013)

There were Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterflies working these flowers in the woods today, but I couldn't get a photo. They are very camera shy! Maybe tomorrow...
Locality: Anthocharis sara) butterflies working these flowers in the woods today
CommonNames: Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea; Sara Orangetip

File.Name: d224b_d224-030.jpg
Notes: 8505221875 There is no blue quite like the blue of this Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii). This native flower is part of the difficult Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now placed in the now even more difficult Boraginaceae plant family. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 February 2013)

File.Name: d224c_d224-033.jpg
Notes: 8505138653 I guess these are new flowers of Common Lomatium (Lomatium utriculatum) in the Apiaceae plant family - though the leaves look almost too dissected. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 February 2013)

File.Name: d224d_d224-046.jpg
Notes: 8505109215 Here are new flowers of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia - aka Amsinkia intermedia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. The flowers are pretty enough, but they are kind of weedy. I found these growing with weedy grasses near my trail head today. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 February 2013)
ScientificName: Amsinkia menziesii; intermedia; Amsinkia intermedia; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Amsinkia menziesii; intermedia; Amsinkia intermedia; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d224e_d224-005.jpg
Notes:
8505030901 This is a small fly (order Diptera) perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Despite the bristly abdomen, I guess it's just a Muscid Fly in the family Muscidae, but it is a bit sharper than most. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2013)

I love photographing flies - so much structural detail! This is the only insect I managed to capture today. The weather has been too dry for mushrooms and too cold for insects.
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Muscid Fly

File.Name: d224f_d223-005.jpg
Notes:
8506091912 I guess this is a Spreading Brown Cup Fungus (Peziza repanda or kin). It looks like a yawning gate to the underworld! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2013)

Mushrooms may be out of time in our woods, depending on what the weather does next. We had a bit of rain a few days ago, but it was followed by drying winds. We'll see what happens next.

File.Name: d224g_d224-039.jpg
Notes: 8506043832 These are different Lichens growing together on a twig of native winter-deciduous Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. It looks peaceful at first, but maybe there's a slow war going on. The gray lichens at the bottom seem to be overgrowing the orange lichens, which seem to be overgrowing the yellow lichens in turn. I don't know much about the taxonomy of lichens. I really need to get a book. On the other hand, it's nice to find them without worrying about what they are! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 February 2013)

File.Name: d224h_d224-041.jpg
Notes: 8504907793 This is a remarkable orange Crustose Lichen growing on a sandstone boulder. It looks like a culture in a petri dish, growing outward at the edges. Well, I guess it is! I don't know much about the taxonomy of lichens. I really need to get a book. On the other hand, it's nice to find them without worrying about what they are! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 February 2013)

File.Name: d225a_d225-006.jpg
Notes:
8508172831 This is a beautiful Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly working the same native flowers of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family that I found yesterday. I love the peaches 'n cream colors of these butterflies! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 February 2013)

I went back to the same flowers I saw them at yesterday and waited for almost 1/2 hour until one finally came by. The focus isn't quite right, but I only had a moment for the photo. These butterflies are very camera shy, and they mostly fly up and down the trails without landing.
CommonNames: Sara Orangetip; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea

File.Name: d225b_d225-001.jpg
Notes: 8509256136 This is a black Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a glandular flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The bee is snuggling into the flower, and it's hind legs are picking up pollen from the anthers. I know, it's dark. These bees move quickly between the flowers, and it's hard to get everything right. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2013)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: d225c_d225-003.jpg
Notes: 8508126419 Here is a rather small Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) approaching a glandular flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I know, the bee's not in focus - but it's an action shot! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2013)
ScientificName: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: d225d_d225-014.jpg
Notes:
8508067717 Early signs of a drought year...

This is a tiny flower head of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae) plant family. It's only about 1/2 inch or less across. In a wet year, the umbrella-like upper leaves are several inches across. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 February 2013)

File.Name: d225e_d225-016.jpg
Notes: 8509160112 Here are new flowers and seed pods of Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The flowers are quite tiny, only a mm or two across. The seed pods become long cylinders. I believe this tiny mustard is native, but it grows like a weed in our mountains. It's easy to walk right past it without noticing. It's apparently native to California. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 February 2013)

File.Name: d225f_d225-017.jpg
Notes: 8509111300 I guess these are new flowers of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family. You can see a bit of the long gray pinnate leaves below the flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 February 2013)

File.Name: d225g_d225-023.jpg
Notes: 8507964337 These are new flowers of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. The flowers are pretty - if you find them after 4:00 pm! Here they are, still puckered up around 1:30 in the afternoon. I usually get my photos in the morning, but I'll try to get back in the afternoon to photograph these guys. I'll bet they get the name "Wishbone Bush" from the dichotomously branched stems. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 February 2013)

File.Name: d225h_d225-010.jpg
Notes: 8507939437 There are still a few mushrooms in the woods if you scratch in the deep leaf litter under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees, despite the dry weather. These are Tricholoma mushrooms. They have a faint "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. They might be part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group, not recommended for the table. They are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk. I've found them before. See this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 February 2013)

File.Name: d226a_d226-020.jpg
Notes: 8512209696 I believe this is a new flower of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family. The stems and leaves are almost glabrous, with just a bit of "peach fuzz" on them. Compare this photo from San Marcos pass, where the stems and leaves are quite hairy. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. I like the painterly background of this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)

File.Name: d226b_d226-007.jpg
Notes: 8512196398 Here is a new flower of California Buttercup (Ranunculus californicus) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The petals look varnished. This photo looks like flash, but this is just the usual brilliant morning sunlight. I don't like flash, and rarely use it. The stamens are hiding the pistils in the center. Compare my previous photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)

File.Name: d226c_d226-003.jpg
Notes: 8511070683 This is a mature flower of California Buttercup (Ranunculus californicus) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The petals look varnished. You can see the pollen-dusted pistils in the center. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)

File.Name: d226d_d226-009.jpg
Notes: 8511028779 These are new flowers of Common Lomatium (Lomatium utriculatum) in the Apiaceae plant family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma. They have rather prominent leafy bracts at the base of the flower clusters, but I also see them in my previous photo. If you look close, you can see a little ant navigating the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)

File.Name: d226e_d226-019.jpg
Notes: 8510999205 This is a beautiful new flower of native Red Maids (Calandrinia ciliata) in the Portulacaceae plant family. It get kind of weedy, but they are apparently native. We also have Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri) in our mountains, see this photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)

File.Name: d226f_d226-018.jpg
Notes: 8510965575 This is another photo of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family, with flowers. You can see into the backlit flowers, with their internal stamens and exserted pistil. They are beautiful and strange partial root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae) in this place. I love photographing these early flowers! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum, Rosaceae

File.Name: d226g_d226-014.jpg
Notes: 8512047646 This is a grand winter-bare tree of deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's hung with Lace Lichen (aka California Spanish Moss and Treebeard Lichen, my namesake!) (Ramalina menziesii). The trees are starting to show some signs of life for the new year. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2013)
CommonNames: Valley Oak; Lace Lichen; California Spanish Moss; Treebeard Lichen

File.Name: d226h_d224-004.jpg
Notes: 8512047646 This rocky bank covered with different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants has become part of my home weather station. Here they are almost completely black from the dry weather. Compare my photo from a week ago when they greened up after a small rain. They are real "resurrection plants". they don't just measure humidity, but soil (or sandstone) moisture. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2013)

File.Name: d227a_d227-012.jpg
Notes:
8514018897 This is a tiny pollen-dusted fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. It has remarkable eyes. I guess it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae. Other little flies on the willow flowers have a longer proboscis, so I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)

I nudged my tripod closer and closer, and finally got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my camera as an expensive micrometer. The fly is about 1160 pixels long on my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.4x1160/4800 = 5.7 mm, less than 1/4 inch. It's not large!

I guess this little fly has been working male staminate flowers of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family, but all the plants nearby are pistillate. I wonder where it's been?
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Dance Fly; Bee Fly; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: d227b_d227-011.jpg
Notes:
8513982433 This is a tiny pollen-dusted fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. I guess it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae. Other little flies on the willows have a longer proboscis, so I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)

I guess it's been working male staminate flowers of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family, but all the plants nearby are pistillate. I wonder where it's been?
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Dance Fly; Bee Fly; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: d227c_d227-006.jpg
Notes: 8515057924 This is another remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes). It was on the flowers of this native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family until I scared it back to the stem. These predator bugs like these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; California Bay
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: d227d_d227-025.jpg
Notes:
8515024834 This fungus isn't beautiful, but it is interesting! This is a strange Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing in our woods, in a place where our neighbors cut and chipped the chaparral down to the ground. I'm not sure what kind of coral mushroom it is - maybe the Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom (Ramaria botrytis) or kin? It looks kind like a cauliflower coming from the earth. It's fairly large, this is just a small corner of it. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)

Actually, any fungus in the woods is interesting right now - it's been so dry!

File.Name: d227e_d227-028.jpg
Notes: 8514959698 Here are new flowers of the native grass-like Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family, which does very well in our canyon. The yellow stamens are the male staminate flowers, but if you look close, you can also see the white plumes of the female pistillate flowers down lower on the stems. I really like this dry land sedge, partly because it's so tough and goes beyond all expectations for a sedge. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)

File.Name: d227f_d227-030.jpg
Notes: 8514920794 This is a whole clump of native grass-like Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family, which does very well in our canyon. There are a few new flowers on the left, see my [Next] photo. There are also a couple of my favorite summer flowers here - Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the foreground, and new leaves of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)
CommonNames: Roundfruit Sedge; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Weed's Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: d227g_d227-009.jpg
Notes: 8514905748 This is a small Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae in its nest in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)

File.Name: d227h_d227-001.jpg
Notes:
8513729139 This is part of the web of a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae). The spider came off its perch for a moment, and I got the photo. The entire web is about the size of a CD. The thick white band through the center of the web is the spider's stabilimentum. Its function is still a subject of debate. See the Wikipedia entry for web decorations. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)

The web starts like the Yellow Brick Road in Oz. If you look at about 2:00, you can follow it around and around. It's hard not to overexpose spiders on webs. I got this photo at at -3.3 stops from what my camera thought was right.

File.Name: d228a_d228-019.jpg
Notes: 8517787988 I found this remarkable little Fly (order Diptera) in the woods today. I first thought from its general hairiness that it might be a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae), but It doesn't have a trace of a proboscis, and antennae look wrong. Several people have suggested Therevidae, and it does look like the images of genus Pandivirilia at BugGuide. With those stubby antennae, it has an odd face - like a snout. Sorry I can't show the wings - it dropped away a moment after I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d228b_d227-004.jpg
Notes: 8517767416 This is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera trying to warm up on a new leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. Look how it's holding its petioled abdomen up on its wings! These are our common paper wasps. They look wicked, but they nest under our eaves and cause no harm. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2013)
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: d228c_d228-030.jpg
Notes:
8517706700 These are the first new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers come in compact clusters rather than the more open clusters of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) and the leaves are three-veined underneath. They are the deepest blue of our native Ceanothus species in our mountains. They appear to have a green nectar disk. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

This photo completes my inventory of native Ceanothus species growing on San Marcos Pass, including Ceanothus spinosus, Ceanothus crassifolius, Ceanothus cuneatus, and Ceanothus megacarpus. There is also Ceanothus leucodermis higher in the mountains, but I'll have to drive out hunting for those.
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus; Ceanothus; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus crassifolius; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus leucodermis
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus; Ceanothus; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus crassifolius; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus leucodermis

File.Name: d228d_d228-013.jpg
Notes: 8517666982 This is a pretty new flower of Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri) in the Portulacaceae plant family, growing in a recently cleared place in the mountains. It usually grows as a fire follower, but sometimes clearing the chaparral is enough to trigger these plants. It is different from the much more common Calandrinia ciliata, and much less common. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d228e_d228-006.jpg
Notes: 8516526723 I guess this is another Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora) in the Portulacaceae plant family growing in a drying up moss bed. It has narrower basal leaves than the usual Miner's Lettuce. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d228f_d228-007.jpg
Notes: 8516488879 I believe this photo shows two different kinds of Miner's Lettuce in the Portulacaceae plant family, in the same frame. On the right is Claytonia perfoliata - the usual Miner's Lettuce. On the left is the Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora) which has narrower basal leaves that are usually reddish or liver-colored. If you look close, both plants have the typical umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that is really two separate leaves grown together and surrounding the new flower stalks. These are growing in a place that didn't burn, but was recently cleared. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d228g_d228-027.jpg
Notes: 8517572682 These are the first new flowers of (I believe) the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family. They were growing along West Camino Cielo road today, and you can see a bit of broken glass on the right. *Sigh* (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d228h_d228-029.jpg
Notes: 8516445739 Here are lots of new flower heads of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia or Southern Umbrellawort, but this beautiful native deserves a better common name. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d301a_d301-018.jpg
Notes:
8519503817 A Horny Toad in the hand is worth...

This is a Coast Horned Lizard aka "Horny Toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii) in my hand. I held my left hand out with the lizard, and got the photo with my 105 mm macro lens. It's a bit of a stretch! What an amazing spiny appearance - but they feel quite soft. I know, I shouldn't catch these lizards, but I was very careful holding it, and it's irresistible! I found this remarkable critter on the ridge above Paradise Creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

It was another beautiful unnaturally warm day today, but breezy. I did see new butterflies - including a new Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon), but I couldn't get any photos. There's always tomorrow!
CommonNames: Coast Horned Lizard; Horny Toad; Pale Swallowtail

File.Name: d301b_d301-024.jpg
Notes: 8520579478 This is a Coast Horned Lizard aka "Horny Toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii) trying to be invisible in the non-native grasses on my trail today. It almost succeeds, but I was following it! I got this photo on the ridge above Paradise Creek. It's the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

File.Name: d301c_d301-017.jpg
Notes: 8519438229 These are mating Crane Flies in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera . I got this photo today in a leafing-out plant of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Their legs go on from here to there! I believe that's the female on top with the large abdomen swollen with eggs, and the male on the bottom with a skinny abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

File.Name: d301d_d301-011.jpg
Notes: 8519421089 Here are more tiny flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family just starting to open from their brown-paper husks. The flower buds look like little origami treasure boxes. If you look close, you can spot a little beetle or thrip on the open flower. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

File.Name: d301e_d301-009.jpg
Notes:
8519387715 These are new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family just starting to open from their brown-paper husks. The shrubs have opposite toothed leaves and "corky stipules" - those dark cones at the base of the leaves. The leaves are whitish-tomentose underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

The different white-flowered Ceanothus species have the mountains covered - Ceanothus megacarpus along the south-facing slopes, Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes, and this Ceanothus crassifolius on the summits.
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Ceanothus megacarpus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus crassifolius

File.Name: d301f_d301-034.jpg
Notes: 8519375015 This is a new flower bud of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family, with the sun behind. There are no flowers yet, but they are coming soon! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: d301g_d301-037.jpg
Notes: 8520460538 These are pretty new flowers of native Succulent Lupine (Lupinus succulentus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The flowers are pretty enough, but the plants are kind of weedy. I mostly see them along roads, where they are a welcome distraction. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

File.Name: d301h_d301-006.jpg
Notes: 8519314201 I believe this is a new frond of the Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia) in the Pteridaceae plant family. We also have the Bird's-Foot fern (P. mucronata) in our mountains, but that one has leaf segments with a small point at the tip, which this one lacks. It's a pretty little fern, growing in a rocky place in the mountains. I have no idea what the common name means. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

File.Name: d302a_d302-004.jpg
Notes:
8523521488 I guess this is the female Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, clutching an unfortunate little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera. I believe the wasp is a Sawfly in the family Tenthredinidae of the "Symphyta" wasps. It has no sign of a "wasp-waist". This spider was sharing a web with the male spider in my [Previous] photo. This one has a larger abdomen, but it's about the same size across. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2013)

It was windy this morning - nothing to do except bump up the ISO and wait for a relatively calm moment. I took many photos to get this one keeper. It's better than I expected.
CommonNames: Sheetweb Spider; Coast Live Oak; Wasp; Sawfly

File.Name: d302b_d302-005.jpg
Notes:
8522385107 I guess this is a male Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It was sharing a web with the female spider in my [Next] photo. This one has a smaller abdomen, but it's about the same size across. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2013)

It was windy this morning, nothing to do except bump up the ISO and wait for a relatively calm moment..

File.Name: d302c_d302-014.jpg
Notes:
8523439150 I found this handsome Amanita mushroom in the woods today, when I wasn't expecting to find any mushrooms because it's been so dry. It has a yellowish cap with some white volval remnants on top, a partial-veil ring on the stem, and a saclike volva at the base. I reckon its a beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom. I'm surprised to find it now, since they usually fruit in the fall in our woods. This mushroom is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2013)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous! There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods, but mushroom spores can travel around the planet.

File.Name: d302d_d228-001.jpg
Notes: 8523406230 There are still a few new mushrooms in the woods, but the dry weather is making it hard for them. I'm sure this is an Entoloma mushroom in the woods. If you look close, you can see some residue of the pink spores. It has a dark cap which I associate with the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin mushrooms, but I didn't notice that characteristic chlorine bleach smell like a swimming pool. So I guess it falls into the "Entoloma rhodopolium" group. I found this one growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)
ScientificName: Entoloma; Entoloma nidorosum; Entoloma rhodopolium; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Entoloma; Entoloma nidorosum; Entoloma rhodopolium; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: d302e_d302-009.jpg
Notes: 8522216993 We have too many different yellow-flowered non-native "wild mustard" plants in the in the Brassicaceae plant family growing in our mountains, and theyare not easy to tell apart. I believe this one is the Tumble Mustard aka Jim Hill Mustard (Sisymbrium altissimum). It seems to be getting into the woods away from the roads, so it's a weed to watch. It has very long Menorah-like seedpods. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2013)

File.Name: d302f_d228-028.jpg
Notes:
8522199131 So many lupines!

I believe this is a new plant of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family. They have skinny leaflets that are "truncate" - square on the tips. This one isn't flowering yet, but give it time. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d302g_d223-030.jpg
Notes:
8523276608 So many lupines!

I believe this is foliage of a native Stinging Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It doesn't really sting like a nettle, but it does have long stiff hairs on its leaves like one. This one isn't flowering yet. The red leaves are striking. There's a leaf of tiny Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) next to it, in the lower-left. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2013)

File.Name: d302h_d228-016.jpg
Notes: 8523259828 This is a "collector's plant" in our mountains - native Fairy Mist (Pterostegia drymarioides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It grows close to the ground. It's an easy plant to miss, though the red foliage is attractive. It's a fire-follower, though this one is growing in a place that was cleared and not burned. I can make out a couple of new flowers in there. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2013)

File.Name: d303a_d303-003.jpg
Notes:
8527164736 I found this cute little Cricket (family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) this morning. Maybe it's a Chaparral Camel Cricket (Gammarotettix genitalis)? It's quite small, only 1/2 inch long or so. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

It was quite breezy when I got this photo in the morning, and it was perched high up in the tree, at the limit of my tripod. I did the best I could. It has impressive "long horns"!
CommonNames: Cricket; Coast Live Oak; Chaparral Camel Cricket
ScientificName: Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Gammarotettix genitalis
associatedTaxa: Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Gammarotettix genitalis

File.Name: d303b_d303-020.jpg
Notes: 8525996209 I found this impressive Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom in the woods today growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains. There were also native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family nearby. The dry weather is taking a toll, and this mushroom is not typical. You can see the thick volval cup at the base, and the thin "partial veil" that covers the gills. The stem looks sculpted. I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. This mushroom is large! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: d303c_d303-027.jpg
Notes:
8527038424 Watch out for Amanita mushrooms masquerading as puffballs!

I sliced this new Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom button in half. The outside on the right looks just like a puffball, but you can make out gills in the open half. This could be a fatal mistake! These amanitas happen to be edible, but others are deadly poisonous.

I found this one growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains today. There were also native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: d303d_d303-017.jpg
Notes: 8526994884 This is an odd "foliose gall" on native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The shrub is trying to make a flower bud, and failing. I believe this is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera - maybe the species Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)
CommonNames: Hairy Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi

File.Name: d303e_d303-013.jpg
Notes:
8526945492 These are absolutely beautiful new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Look at that flower on the left, and you can see what those "extra petals" are for - they enclose the new stamens like little tissue hoods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

The flowers come in compact clusters rather than the more open clusters of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) and the leaves are three-veined underneath. They are the deepest blue of our native Ceanothus species in our mountains. They appear to have a green nectar disk. The shrubs may well be fire-followers. They are my favorite.
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus

File.Name: d303f_d303-030.jpg
Notes: 8525809193 Here are pretty new flowers of Popcorn Flower (genus (Cryptantha or Plagiobothrys) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I suspected Plagiobothrys nothofulvus, though this one didn't have purple dye in the leaf veins. You need a microscope to tell these flowers apart, and I really don't care. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

File.Name: d303g_d303-004.jpg
Notes:
8525764425 Here is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. They are pretty, but I rarely find fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

The blackberries look water stressed in this very dry winter. We're down to 59% of normal rainfall for the year. I was happy to see this flower.

File.Name: d303h_d303-009.jpg
Notes: 8525706279 These are brand new flowers of native winter-bare Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family along San Jose Creek a mile from home. New flowers and leaves erupt from the large buds. These were pretty high up in the tree. I'm sure I'll get closer photos as the season progresses. They are not common here. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

File.Name: d304a_d304-008.jpg
Notes:
8529384815 As the flowers of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family get older, they start to fall apart. (Don't we all!) Those strange yellow "candy corn" things at the base of the flower are part of the nectary disk these flowers have. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 4 March 2013)

I didn't have much chance for new photos today because I was taking filmmaker Michael Love into the woods to photograph a few special flowers, including these. Michael will be showing his film on "The Santa Ynez River Wilderness" at the the San Luis Obispo International Film Festival at 10:00 am on March 10, 2013. There's a trailer on YouTube. Highly recommended for anyone who loves the Las Padres!
CommonNames: California Peony; "The Santa Ynez River Wilderness

File.Name: d304b_d304-010.jpg
Notes: 8529352649 This is another beautiful new flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The flowers are walnut-sized, and dense as a nut. They are not common, but they appear after wildfires and linger for a while. The flowers face the earth. I propped this one up for the photo with a dead branch that's visible on the left. I've read that the perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 4 March 2013)

File.Name: d304c_d301-038.jpg
Notes: 8529326909 These are new flowers of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiaceae plant family. They are just starting to flower in the mountains, though they've been going lower in the mountains for some time. The flowers always seem to look brown like this by the time they open. The shrub has strong smelling leaves that can be used in cooking - but I'd rather not! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2013)

File.Name: d304d_d303-012.jpg
Notes:
8530404128 This is another beautiful cluster of new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can see that the leaves are 3-veined from the base, unlike the more common Ceanothus spinosus which is 1-veined. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

These are the deepest blue of our native Ceanothus species in our mountains. They appear to have a green nectar disk. The shrubs may well be fire-followers. They are my favorite.
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus

File.Name: d304e_d304-001.jpg
Notes: 8529253315 I found this different Russula mushroom in the woods this morning. It has violet colors on it's cap instead of the usual red, though it's showing the effects of the dry weather we've been having. It's got colors like Russula cyanoxnatha or Russula xerampelina, but it doesn't have a "shrimpy" taste. (Well, I assume it doesn't - I didn't want to taste this one!) (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2013)

File.Name: d304f_d303-022.jpg
Notes:
8530309890 This is a family group of Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushrooms that I found yesterday growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains. There were also native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family nearby. The dry weather is taking a toll, and these mushrooms are not typical. You can see the thick volval material covering the caps and making a thick cup at the base, and the thin "partial veil" that covers the gills. I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2013)

These mushrooms are past their prime, but they are said to be edible when fresh. They are one of those mushrooms you must be VERY CAREFUL with to avoid confusion with poisonous species! I haven't tried them, but I'll admit they are distinctive.
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: d304g_d304-004.jpg
Notes: 8529158633 I believe this swelling is an integral stem gall - probably the gall of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). It's growing on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like this gall has killed the branch beyond it. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2013)
CommonNames: Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d304h_d227-014.jpg
Notes: 8530242328 Here is a forest of tiny umbrella-like sporophytes of drying-out Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. I guess they are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. The dried out "thalli" of the liverworts gets pretty blue colors. They are not dead, just resting during dry weather. They might come back if it ever rains again. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2013)

File.Name: d305a_d305-002.jpg
Notes:
8532253575 Raindrops on a spider web - the web of a Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae. It was breezy today, and I took many photos to get this keeper. It's sharp enough! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

It was cold, gloomy, drippy, and breezy today. I didn't really expect to get any new photos, but I got photos of a few new mushrooms, bookended by spider webs. There's a bit of rain in the forecast tonight.

File.Name: d305b_d305-006.jpg
Notes:
8532152919 This is the first - and so far the only - Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom that I've found in the woods this year. It's small, only about 3 inches long, growing along our driveway where I've found them before. You can recognize it as a chanterelle because the blunt "gills" are really just folds in the fertile surface. This one will become part of our dinner tomorrow! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

It's odd that they are so late this year, since they have been early mushrooms in the few years. See this photo from December 4, 2010.

File.Name: d305c_d305-017.jpg
Notes:
8532135625 Here is another photo of the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) growing in sandy soil under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. It has an attractive yellow cap with powdery white "warts" which are the remnants of the universal veil, an obscure skirt-like ring on the stem, and a thick collar-like volva at the base. It looks a bit like a yellow Amanita muscaria, but the volva is different. It's an attractive mushroom, but definitely not edible. It may not be edible, but it's quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

Compare my photo from last year, which has a clearer yellow cap:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6878391705/
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: d305d_d305-021.jpg
Notes: 8532090235 I believe this is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) growing in sandy soil under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. It has an attractive yellow cap with powdery white "warts" which are the remnants of the universal veil, an obscure skirt-like ring on the stem, and a thick collar-like volva at the base. It looks a bit like a yellow Amanita muscaria, but the volva is different. It's an attractive mushroom, but definitely not edible. It may not be edible, but it's quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: d305e_d305-010.jpg
Notes: 8532030631 I believe these are small Inocybe mushrooms in the woods today. The have broadly umbonate silky caps, and brown spores, and white-edged gills which are a sign of "cystydia" - special cells on the gill edges. These mushrooms have no particular smell. They push up unusually large "mushrumps" in the oak leaves for their size. They might be Inocybe geophylla, but there are hundreds of species that need microscopic study to distinguish. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

File.Name: d305f_d305-025.jpg
Notes: 8532002587 I found these white Russula mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I guess they are the Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes or kin) mushroom, but I'm not sure. They really do have short stems, just what you see. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

File.Name: d305g_d305-028.jpg
Notes: 8531981623 This is another photo of the strange Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing in our woods, in a place where our neighbors cut and chipped the chaparral down to the ground. I'm not sure what kind of coral mushroom it is - maybe the Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom (Ramaria botrytis) or kin? It looks kind like a cauliflower coming from the earth. It's fairly large. I've been finding them here every year, but I've never found them anywhere else in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

File.Name: d305h_d305-004.jpg
Notes: 8533061936 Raindrops and a Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae in a shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I know, it's not quite sharp - I got this photo shooting almost straight up, on a gloomy day, with some drizzle - but it captures the moment! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2013)

File.Name: d306a_d306-021.jpg
Notes:
8536065782 This is a perfect tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly trying to get warm on an old "flower" of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo. The iridescent speckles on its upper wing are wonderful. I usually find them in late summer, so I had no idea they appear this early - but they are easy to miss. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

We had a bit of rain last night and showers today, but the total is still less than 1/10 inch. That's the way it's been since December. What should be our wettest months have been dry. But drought or deluge, there's always something to photograph in our woods!
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed

File.Name: d306b_d306-023.jpg
Notes: 8536037644 Look! This is the same tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly that I show in my [Next] photo, leaning at an angle to catch the sun rays on a cold day! It's perched on an old "flower" of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed

File.Name: d306c_d306-014.jpg
Notes: 8536010132 These are wonderful new flowers of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. The flowers normally open in the afternoon (around 4:00 pm?) and close in the morning, but I guess they stay open late on gloomy days, like Evening Primrose. Look at that lower flower - certainly pollinating itself! I suspect most flowers do this, most of the time. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d306d_d306-013.jpg
Notes: 8535961924 Here are beautiful new flowers of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family, with the long pinnate leaves. It looks tropical. This plant used to be known as a locoweed, but there's nothing about it that will get you high or crazy. *Sigh* The flowers are usually born well above the foliage. I had to pluck a flower head and place it for this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d306e_d306-009.jpg
Notes:
8535911750 I believe these are new flowers of native Gambel's Phacelia (Phacelia viscida var. albiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now placed in the now even more difficult Boraginaceae plant family. This is an interesting flower. It looks like a smaller white version of Phacelia grandiflora. It's a fire-follower plant, growing in a place that burned a few years ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

I believe I have a photo of the species plant (Phacelia viscida var. viscida ) way back in the archives from 2003 - though it's misidentified in the caption. The species plants have blue flowers. They are more beautiful, but much less common in our mountains.
Locality: Phacelia viscida var. viscida ) way back in the archives from 2003 - though it's misidentified in the caption. The species plants have blue flowers. They are more beautiful
ScientificName: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia grandiflora; Phacelia viscida; viscida
associatedTaxa: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia grandiflora; Phacelia viscida; viscida

File.Name: d306f_d306-015.jpg
Notes: 8535886218 These are brilliant red flowers of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Actually the flowers are mostly green - you can see them sticking out of the flower heads - and the brilliant red comes from bracts and calyces. I got this photo down by the river. They're not flowering at home yet. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d306g_d306-011.jpg
Notes: 8535847592 These are striking new waxy flowers finally opening on native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The buds form early and wait on the plant for months to flower. See this photo from last October! These are the first open flowers I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d306h_d306-026.jpg
Notes:
8535811656 I believe this large Russula mushroom is the Blackening Russula (Russula albonigra) or kin. My mushroom book says "Better punted than hunted" - meaning don't eat it. It feels very solid and massive, and develops dark stains as it ages. It's quite large - I put my my 3-1/2 inch pocket knife in the photo for scale. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

This is only "new" mushroom I found today. We had a bit of rain last night, and a few showers today, and mushrooms don't usually seem to come up in the rain. They wait a few days so they can release spores. Somehow they know!

File.Name: d307a_d306-029.jpg
Notes:
8538252174 Here are striking new flowers of well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family. They are really beautiful, especially that deep-purple crinkly tissue at the base of the anthers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

I'm posting early - and just four photos - because there's a lecture tonight for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. It was too cold and gloomy for photos today anyhow. It rained all morning and we're up to 4/10 inch of rain for this storm, with more predicted tonight. Still not much, but better than it was.

File.Name: d307b_d306-018.jpg
Notes: 8537128791 Here are new female pistillate flowers of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has willow-like leaves, but composite flowers. Apparently the plant gets its charming common name because it provides good forage for livestock. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d307c_d306-020.jpg
Notes: 8537113141 Here are new flowers on a Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family. The flowers are either very early or very late! I think of them as coming out in late summer - July to November. I don't know what these flowers are doing out now, but here they are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d307d_d306-024.jpg
Notes:
8537089373 "Drifting along with the tumbling tumbleweeds..."

This is a whole "tumbleweed" plant of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family - the food plant of the butterfly in my [Previous] photo. It's a non-native weed. The plant dries out as it matures, then breaks off at the base of the stem and is carried about by the wind, the dry fruits and seeds dropping off as it rolls. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d308a_d308-010.jpg
Notes:
