8541202172 These are tiny new flowers of some kind of Popcorn Flower (genus (Cryptantha) in the Boraginaceae plant family. You need a microscope to tell these tiny flowers apart, and there are many different species. I really don't care too much what they are. They are surrounded by even tinier succulent plants of native Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) in the Crassulaceae plant family that are just starting to flower! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2013)

I put my pen in the photo for scale. The ball of the pen is listed as 0.07 mm. That makes the Cryptantha flowers 2 or 3 mm across and the Pygmyweed about 1 mm across. These are tiny flowers, but they have character. The Cryptantha plants look like tiny prickly pear cacti!

We had surprisingly good rain last night - with some slushy snow around midnight! We ended up with 1-1/4 inch of rain. I hope this is starting a repeat of last winter with a wet fall, not much in January and February, but a wet spring. That was good for our summer flowers.

File.Name: d308b_d308-001.jpg
Notes: 8540069165 Here is a new flower of a spiny shrub of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family, with raindrops. The flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2013)

File.Name: d308c_d308-011.jpg
Notes:
8541127424 This is the same yellow-capped Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) that I showed a few few days ago. It's growing in sandy soil under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2013)

Whenever I unearth a mushroom for a photo, I always "replant" it in the same place so that it can reconnect with it's mycelium. In this case it worked. Please do the same. It's a matter of mushroom etiquette.

We had good rain last night, and I didn't really expect to find new mushrooms today. Mushrooms don't usually come up in the rain - maybe because they can't disperse their spores. Somehow they know!

File.Name: d308d_d308-006.jpg
Notes: 8540004739 I guess this is an Inocybe sororia mushroom or one of its many kin, though this one didn't have any particular odor. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It was hopping with tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects. I'm sure it's not a new mushroom, but I found it today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2013)
ScientificName: Inocybe sororia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Collembola
associatedTaxa: Inocybe sororia; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Collembola

File.Name: d308e_d308-008.jpg
Notes: 8541065394 These are probably the same drying-out Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants I showed a , with ripening umbrella-like sporophytes. I guess they are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. Look how they've already greened up the day after a rain. I love these little "resurrection plants"! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2013)

File.Name: d308f_d306-003.jpg
Notes: 8541031520 This is an annual native Phacelia in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family just starting to flower - but which one? I guess it's the Distant Phacelia (Phacelia distans), but I can't rule out the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida). These are difficult flowers, and I think I can tell better when it gets older. Any advise Lynn? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)
ScientificName: Phacelia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia distans; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida
associatedTaxa: Phacelia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia distans; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida

File.Name: d308g_d306-010.jpg
Notes:
8539891293 Here are more new flowers of native Gambel's Phacelia (Phacelia viscida var. albiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now placed in the now even more difficult Boraginaceae plant family. It looks like a smaller white version of Phacelia grandiflora. It's a local fire-follower plant, growing in a place that burned a few years ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

Marlin Harms posted a nice photo of the species plant (Phacelia viscida var. viscida ) yesterday.
ScientificName: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia grandiflora; Phacelia viscida; viscida
associatedTaxa: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Phacelia grandiflora; Phacelia viscida; viscida

File.Name: d308h_d306-025b.jpg
Notes: 8539891293 Here is yet another native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The flowers are so beautiful that I can't resist! At least this one is from a different place, and I didn't have to prop it up! The open flowers usually face the earth, but this one was naturally lifted up. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 March 2013)

File.Name: d309a_d309-018.jpg
Notes:
8544011298 I found these beautiful Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods today. They are edible and tasty. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan like the one on the right is starting to do. These fresh specimens are unusually colorful. Yet they always have a "blewitt aura" that is recognizable with experience. We also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks similar. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

It was very windy today after our rain for the past few days. The wind makes me jittery, and I guess it transfers to my tripod as a few photos that should have been sharp weren't. The wind seems to be dying down tonight, and I have hopes for tomorrow.

File.Name: d309b_d309-027.jpg
Notes:
8542871527 This is the same beautiful Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa) as my [Previous] photo before I picked it. Note that it has a "bird splat" volval remnant on the tan cap and a distinctly striate margin. I found it growing with the weeds on the side of Stagecoach Road today. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

These mushrooms are said to be "edible and incredible" - but I'm leery of eating any amanitas. This one does seem pretty distinctive though.

File.Name: d309c_d309-028.jpg
Notes: 8543919642 I'm pretty sure this is a Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa). Note that it has "bird splat" volval remnants on the cap, no obvious ring on the stem, and a voluminous vovla. Compare my [Previous] and [Next] photos. If it's the same mushroom as my [Previous] photo, this demonstrates how different the same kind of mushrooms can look. Be careful! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

File.Name: d309d_d309-015.jpg
Notes: 8543886694 I found this rather large Amanita mushroom in the woods today. I don't have my pocket knife in this photo, but judging from another photo with my knife, the cap is about 6 inches across and the stem is longer. Because of the "bird splat" volval remnants on the tan cap and the striate margin, I think it may be an older Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa). Compare my [Next] two photos. I don't know about those rings on the stem though. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

File.Name: d309e_d309-011.jpg
Notes: 8542749817 These are beautiful yellow flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's a fantastic head-high (or more) native shrub that has usually has flowers most of the year, though they took a break in this last dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

File.Name: d309f_d309-037.jpg
Notes: 8543828752 Here are new flowers of climbing Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family growing over a blue-flowered shrub of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Clematis is a climbing vine that makes a place on top of other shrubs. It's a rich combination. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: d309g_d309-036.jpg
Notes: 8543794692 Here is a pretty single new flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family, just starting the flower in the mountains. The petals look furry up close. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

File.Name: d309h_d309-022.jpg
Notes: 8542660887 This is the shiny new sprout of a Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family. Live long and prosper, little lily. There's lots of potential enemies between now and flower time, including deer, little grubs that eat the flower buds, and people that fancy the flowers. If you don't know the amazing flowers, see this photo from last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

File.Name: d310a_d310-009.jpg
Notes:
8546522371 This is the tiny nymph of a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae. I spotted it on a new leaf of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's only about 1/8 inch long, except for those amazing antennae which go on from here to there! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2013)

The wind didn't die down completely today, but it was manageable. I went for a walk today up the Aliso Canyon trail, and found interesting sanicles and a phacelia that I have to think about.
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: d310b_d310-007.jpg
Notes: 8546459181 This is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae), perched on it's inner web with its thick white stabilimentum webbing. There's no mistaking the bottle-neck shape of this spider's body! You have to remember not to over-expose spiders. I got this photo at 3 stops less than my camera thought was right. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d310c_d310-001.jpg
Notes: 8547533082 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's not looking at me with it's main eyes, but I'm sure it's watching my every move! (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d310d_d310-003.jpg
Notes:
8546378459 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The flower buds are especially pretty, with some violet colors. It's a perennial mustard. It has an edible root (the "tooth"), but I really don't care to dig it up. It tastes like a radish, but that's not worth the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2013)

If you look at the left-most open flower, you can see a tiny Midge fly, probably in the family Chironomidae of the order Diptera with those plumose antennae.

File.Name: d310e_d310-011.jpg
Notes:
8546337673 These are the tiny flowers and fruit of non-native Shepherd's Purse (Capsella bursa-pastoris) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's another weedy mustard plant that has become part of our flora. It has neat little heart-shaped seed heads that reminded someone of little purses. Flowers and fruit are tasty if you strip them from the tough stems. You have to think not to over-expose the tiny flowers. I like this photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)

If you look close, you can see that the new flower buds are purple. See my [Next] photo of a different native mustard.

File.Name: d310f_d310-015.jpg
Notes: 8547358170 I guess this is another Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae plant family. (Those darned botanists!) Instead of being all sky-blue, this one has sky-blue honey-guide lines to guide pollinators in against a paler background. Very beautiful! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d310g_d310-012.jpg
Notes: 8547319278 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae plant family. (Those darned botanists!) You can stick the flowers and leaves to your shirt, ready for fiesta! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d310h_d309-006.jpg
Notes: 8546167333 These are old Puffball mushrooms in the woods, releasing their spores through a terminal pore. I'm pretty sure these are the same Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushrooms that I've found before, though they've lost all their dark color and look kind of silvery here. They remind me of full vacuum cleaner bags. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2013)

File.Name: d311a_d311-002.jpg
Notes:
8550908848 "And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom."

These are open new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland. It's one of the shrubs that keeps the woods interesting year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2013)

File.Name: d311b_d311-004.jpg
Notes: 8549795893 These are open new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland. It's one of the shrubs that keeps the woods interesting year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2013)

File.Name: d311c_d311-008.jpg
Notes: 8549778503 These are pretty sporophytes on a drying up Moss (Bryophyta) in the mountains today. They are growing on a sandstone rock outcrop. I don't know anything about the taxonomy of mosses, but I can recognize beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2013)

File.Name: d311d_d310-038.jpg
Notes: 8550822214 I guess these are the strange native flowers of Owl's Clover (Castilleja exserta) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. The fuzzy backs of the flowers look like lavender pipe cleaners! I have a sharper photo of the flowers - but look at that perfect little nymph of a Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera on the left! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)
ScientificName: Castilleja exserta; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Castilleja exserta; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera

File.Name: d311e_d310-029.jpg
Notes: 8550803262 I believe these are new flowers of native Sanicula arguta in the Apiaceaeplant family. The leaves are sharply toothed and look almost palmate. We have a number of different Sanicle plants in the mountains that all have similar balls of yellow flowers but different foliage. If you know I have the wrong one, please let me know! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d311f_d310-013.jpg
Notes: 8550772810 I believe these are new flowers of native Poison Sanicle (Sanicula bipinnata) in the Apiaceaeplant family. The common name sounds scary, but the leaves taste just like cilantro, and I've been munching them for years. The parsley-like foliage is distinctive. We have many different kinds of sanicles in our mountains. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d311g_d310-039.jpg
Notes:
8549621635 I believe this is a new flowering plant of the native Coast Sanicle (Sanicula laciniata) in the Apiaceaeplant family. I apologize in advance for the contrasty light. It's not the kind of flower you would cut for your dining table, but it is native and it does have its charms. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2013)

The Sanicles are kind of a "collector's genus". We have 5 to 7 species in our mountains. The flowers all look the same - little yellow balls - but the foliage is different. If you know I have the wrong one, please let me know!

File.Name: d311h_d310-030.jpg
Notes: 8550679474 This is the whole plant of an annual native Phacelia in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess that it's the Distant Phacelia (Phacelia distans). It's not looking so good in the dry year, but life is tough. As Lillian Gish says at the end of the film "The Night of the Hunter" - "They abide, and they endure." (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 March 2013)

File.Name: d312a_d312-020.jpg
Notes:
8552593427 I've been seeing these beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies for a few weeks, but always in the air. I found this one snuggling into a flower in the front garden of the Paradise Store today. I guess the flower is some kind of hardy terrestrial orchid, because of the twisted flower stalks. The butterfly likes it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

It's not quite spring yet, but today was another beautiful spring-like day. It was windy though. I saw several different butterflies blowing by on my ridge-line trail today, including a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice).

File.Name: d312b_d312-006.jpg
Notes: 8552562775 This little Spider is clutching an unfortunate little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera. I believe the wasp is a Sawfly in the family Tenthredinidae of the "Symphyta" wasps. It has no sign of a "wasp-waist". You can see the spider's jaw-like orange chelicerae. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but maybe it's a Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae. The action took place in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)
CommonNames: Spider; Wasp; Sawfly; Sheetweb Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Tenthredinidae; chelicerae; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Tenthredinidae; chelicerae; Linyphiidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d312c_d312-002.jpg
Notes: 8552534205 This was a close encounter between a Spider and a little native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera). I had my camera focused on the spider when the ant wandered into my photo frame. Both the spider and I jumped! I know, it's not quite sharp - but this is an action shot! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

File.Name: d312d_d312-015.jpg
Notes: 8553611218 Look close! This is a well-camouflaged Coast Horned Lizard aka "Horny Toad" (Phrynosoma blainvillii) hiding among the grasses. It looks prehistoric! What an amazing spiny appearance - but they feel quite soft. I didn't try to catch it. I found this remarkable critter on the ridge above Paradise Creek today, where I often find them. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

File.Name: d312e_d312-007.jpg
Notes: 8553592506 This is an elegant branch of a native Fuchsia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family, with flowers. The flowers are beautiful, and so is the foliage. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. As long as they keep flowering, I'll get photos now and then! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

File.Name: d312f_d312-012.jpg
Notes: 8553562438 Here are fully-open new flowers of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. The leaves are quite distinctive - toothed on top, but not below. The flowers will be followed by feathery plumed seeds in summer. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

File.Name: d312g_d312-013.jpg
Notes: 8552442059 This is a new flower bud on a native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a variety of "Cobweb Thistle" with white flowers, but there are also violet and bright red versions. I love these native thistles. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you don't pull these by mistake! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

File.Name: d312h_d312-004.jpg
Notes:
8552385325 This is new growth on native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Look how the stem on the right is swollen compared to the stems on the left. I reckon it's been galled by an insect, possibly a Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge (Neolasioptera diplaca) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. My book on galls only mentions this gall on Mimulus aurantiacus, but I believe they are now considered the same species. The leaves appear different on the galled and ungalled stems. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2013)

I know, I'm behind the curve with Latin names. Those darned botanists keep changing the names I know! But I figure I'm OK as long as the new floras index synonyms.
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Monkey Flower Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Mimulus aurantiacus
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Mimulus aurantiacus

File.Name: d313a_d313-015.jpg
Notes:
8556558328 These are brilliant blue native flowers of (I think) Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. We also have Delphinium patens in our mountains near home, but it's not flowering yet. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2013)

Today was another beautiful almost-spring day - the temperature got into the 80s. It's been hard to concentrate on photo captions tonight, because TCM has been showing the brilliant documentary films of Lionel Rogosin - "On the Bowery" and "Come Back, Africa". These are amazing films, highly recommended.

File.Name: d313b_d313-013.jpg
Notes: 8556514174 This is a pretty new almost-spring flower of native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma today. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2013)

File.Name: d313c_d313-018.jpg
Notes:
8556461212 I believe these are new flowers of native Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis) in the Apiaceaeplant family. The leaves are palmate and toothed, but relatively simple. We have a number of different Sanicle plants in the mountains that all have similar balls of yellow flowers but different foliage. This is the one we mostly have at home, though I haven't seen flowers yet. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2013)

Compare my recent photos of Sanicula arguta, Sanicula bipinnata, and Sanicula laciniata.
ScientificName: Sanicula crassicaulis; Apiaceae; Sanicula arguta; Sanicula bipinnata; Sanicula laciniata
associatedTaxa: Sanicula crassicaulis; Apiaceae; Sanicula arguta; Sanicula bipinnata; Sanicula laciniata

File.Name: d313d_d313-020.jpg
Notes: 8556435472 These are new leaves and catkins of deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. There's new life in the woods! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2013)

File.Name: d313e_d313-007.jpg
Notes:
8555273201 This little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) rode with me for a while today on my hand. I got out of the the car at Lake Cachuma and set up my tripod, and the bee only moved from finger to finger. I finally got the photo! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2013)

Are my fingers really that pink? Nah, I'm well-tanned even after winter. It must be a white balance thing!

File.Name: d313f_d313-004.jpg
Notes: 8555209543 I noticed this tiny beetle in the order Coleoptera perched on a new leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's only about 1/8 inch long. They have secret lives that we know little about... (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2013)

File.Name: d313g_d313-006.jpg
Notes: 8556269182 I noticed this tiny beetle in the order Coleoptera because it landed on the web of the Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae in my [Previous] photo. It was just "hanging out" too. The spider wasn't interested, and the beetle easily got away. I guess its a little Click Beetle (family Elateridae) because of the points on its thorax - look behind the "elbow" of its front left leg. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2013)

File.Name: d313h_d313-002.jpg
Notes: 8555146475 This is a beautiful Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae "hanging out" on its web this morning. You have to be careful not to overexpose these spiders. Watch your histogram! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2013)

File.Name: d314a_d314-006.jpg
Notes:
8559271505 This is a spectacular White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth on the window screen at night. Of course I used flash for this photo, taken in the dark. I think I've also been seeing a few of these in the daytime. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2013)

Just two photo today, posted the next morning. I had a dinner engagement and got home late.

File.Name: d314b_d314-007.jpg
Notes:
8560362814 "Here's looking at you, kid."

This little white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for prey on an old leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2013)

File.Name: d315a_d314-013.jpg
Notes:
8560328091 This is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a dead stem of native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. It's missing most of its left wings - probably a bird attack. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I never did get a photo last year. They are usually pretty uncommon in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2013)

I have another dinner engagement tomorrow night. I'll catch up on photos on Sunday.

File.Name: d315b_d315-015.jpg
Notes:
8561391338 Here are wonderful new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. They're now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family, though I don't understand why. Those darned botanists! Our plants in the Santa Ynez mountains are different because they have green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals, and they are more robust. I love these native flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

It's a fire-follower plant that's growing in a place that was was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break. All that some fire-follower plants need is to clear the soil. Other native plants need actual fire.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: d315c_d315-017.jpg
Notes:
8561353846 I believe these are new flowers of the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's a fire-follower in our mountains, but here it is growing in a place that was was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break. All that some fire-follower plants need is to clear the soil. Other native plants need actual fire. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

Look close! There's a cute little Springtail (order Collembola) insect on the left of the opening flower on the top-left.

File.Name: d315d_d315-013.jpg
Notes: 8560205615 I believe this is the Woolly-fruited Lomatium (Lomatium dasycarpum) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a distinctive native growing on the red soil of the "Sespe Formation". It has tightly clustered leaves. This is growing in a place that I guess was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - a couple of years ago, but it's really a fire-follower in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

File.Name: d315e_d315-003.jpg
Notes:
8561289840 "Seeds of life"

I heard the frogs last night, and sure enough there were new eggs in our "water garden" today. I guess these are the eggs of the Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla). Our "water garden" is just an old fiberglass tub filled with water and plants, but these tubs are important for the frogs. I've been worried about them in this dry winter. I hope they make it. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

File.Name: d315f_d315-004.jpg
Notes: 8560160259 These are unfolding new fronds of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family, growing in a thicket of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the edge of our garden. It's remarkably difficult to get a sharp photo of these ferns! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)
ScientificName: Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d315g_d315-008.jpg
Notes:
8561250858 I found this little Fly (order Diptera) on an unfolding new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the woods today. The fly is quite bristly, so maybe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae? (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

I love photographing flies even when I don't know what they are - most of the time. They have so much structural detail!
CommonNames: Fly; Western Bracken Fern; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: d315h_d315-024.jpg
Notes:
8561193432 This is a tiny Planthopper nymph in the insect order Homoptera. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper - family Issidae of the superfamily Fulgoroidea. This one is perched on a tendril of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, and this is a 1:1 crop from my original photo. I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The critter is about 500 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.4x500/4800 = 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. It's small!
CommonNames: Planthopper; Issid Planthopper; Wild Sweet Pea
ScientificName: Homoptera; Issidae; Fulgoroidea; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Homoptera; Issidae; Fulgoroidea; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d316a_d316-008.jpg
Notes:
8564872583 I happened to notice this tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods today perched on an old seed head of grass. It may well be a Western Black-Legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), which is said to be the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. (Compare tick photos here). This was the sharpest photo I could get in the shade and breeze. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2013)

It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me, though these are the ticks you find on lizards in their early stage. Ticks can spend weeks just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that. If you get ticked, just pull the damn thing out! Use tweezers if you have to. The first sign of a tick bite is a persistent irritation. The bites usually swell up and itch for a day after you remove the tick. No big deal.

I had a busy day yesterday, so just two photos, posted the next day.
Locality: and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me

File.Name: d316b_d316-009.jpg
Notes: 8565928750 I found this little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a new leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The wasp has no sign of a "wasp-waist", so I believe it is some kind of Common Sawfly in the family Tenthredinidae of the "Symphyta" wasps. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2013)
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Tenthredinidae; Symphyta
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Tenthredinidae; Symphyta

File.Name: d317a_d317-019.jpg
Notes:
8566278625 Here is another beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a dead stem - or is it the same one I showed two days ago? It's missing the same chunk of its left wings, and it has the same scars on its right wing. They are usually pretty uncommon in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2013)

It started out breezy, and got downright windy today. But there's always tomorrow! It's been hard to concentrate on photo captions tonight - Saint Paddy's Day, and TCM has been showing the rarely seen John Ford film "The Rising of the Moon" (1957). It's a remarkable movie, highly recommended if you can find it. They're showing "The Quiet Man" (1952) next, which may well be my favorite film ever - and I'm done with photos in time to enjoy it!

File.Name: d317b_d317-002.jpg
Notes: 8566222163 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the woods today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched on an old leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. These flies are built like jet planes, but they fly like helicopters. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2013)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d317c_d317-006.jpg
Notes: 8566177555 This yellowish Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for prey on a furry new bud of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's missing one its "grabbing legs" on the left. That seems to be a common affliction of the spiders of these spiders that grab their prey and hang on. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2013)

File.Name: d317d_d317-005.jpg
Notes:
8566096717 This is only the second Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom that I've found in the woods in this dry year. Even last winter, which was also dry, we found lots of them - but this year they are downright scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2013)

Chanterelles are edible and delicious, and they're easy to recognize once you've see a few. Find a mushroom guru to learn from. Flygrl67 is the Chanterelle Queen this year!

File.Name: d317e_d317-003.jpg
Notes: 8567145084 Here is a white patch of a Fungus growing on the still-damp soil in the woods. I first thought this was fungus mycelium, but I'm sure it's the whole fungus. It's not growing on a turd. I have no idea what it's doing here, but it's pretty common on bare ground. If you know anything about this critter, please let me know. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2013)

File.Name: d317f_d317-011.jpg
Notes: 8567123084 Here are bronze new leaves and flower buds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It really is a beautiful plant, and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2013)

File.Name: d317g_d315-014.jpg
Notes: 8565983017 I believe this is a new flowering plant of the native Coast Sanicle (Sanicula laciniata) in the Apiaceaeplant family. The Sanicles are kind of a "collector's genus". We have a number of species in our mountains. The flowers all look the same - little yellow balls - but the foliage is different. This one has sharp points on its mostly-palmate leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

File.Name: d317h_d315-021.jpg
Notes:
8567037724 Here is a colony of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. They're now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family, though I don't understand why. Those darned botanists! Our plants in the Santa Ynez mountains are different because they have green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals, and they are more robust. I love these native flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2013)

It's one of those wildflowers that's "rare", but that really means rarely common. It's a fire-follower plant that's growing in a place that was was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break. All that some fire-follower plants need is to clear the soil. Other native plants need actual fire.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: d318a_d318-039.jpg
Notes:
8569773477 This is a wonderful Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched on a flower of the non-native Filaree (Erodium) plants that grow everywhere. It's one of our most beautiful butterflies. They feed on many plants, but their host plants are different species of Dudleya in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family. Just beautiful. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

It was quite windy today and cloudy as our newest weather system moved in. It doesn't sound like we'll get much or any rain from this storm. *Sigh*

File.Name: d318b_d318-007.jpg
Notes:
8570846378 This is another Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

Look close! This butterfly is committing the crime of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through a hole in the side of the petals instead of through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen.
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea

File.Name: d318c_d318-027.jpg
Notes: 8569734223 This is a striking Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family, shot from below. I know, it's not quite sharp. It was windy today, and I did the best I could. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea

File.Name: d318d_d318-006.jpg
Notes: 8570793676 I apologize in advance for this photo - it was my first photo of the day, it was windy, my tripod slipped - all the usual excuses! But you can clearly see that it is a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly - the first I've managed to photograph this year. It's working a flower head of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family. I hope for many more! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)
CommonNames: Silvery Blue; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea

File.Name: d318e_d318-020.jpg
Notes:
8570756770 This is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on new foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing in the photo. Sometimes they shroud the penstemon leaves like "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

The little caterpillars first spin their webs on the host plants in summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They come back about now in the Spring to feed and pupate into adults. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. I saw an adult butterfly pass by today.

File.Name: d318f_d318-042.jpg
Notes: 8570729670 This is the perfect husk of a Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera) insect clutching a twig in the woods today. The larvae climb the twigs and leave as adults. I'll bet I have a photo of the adult here. I don't think these early cicadas sing at all, but we have different cicadas later in summer that do sing. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d318g_d318-023.jpg
Notes: 8569570195 These are insect galls on a textured leaf of Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla) in the Labiatae plant family. I reckon that's an exit hole on the left side of the top gall. Other Salvia species in our mountains have leaf galls made by Gall Midges in the genus Rhopalomyia of the family Cecidomyiidae in the order Diptera, so that's what I suspect these are. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)
ScientificName: Salvia leucophylla; Labiatae; Salvia; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia leucophylla; Labiatae; Salvia; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: d318h_d318-030.jpg
Notes: 8570623744 This looks like a flower bud, but it's a gall on a new plant of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. The galls are not common this year, but I've seen a few. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d319a_d319-006.jpg
Notes:
8573891674 Here are new flowers and leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family along San Jose Creek a mile from home. The red bits of the flowers will become the wings of the seeds later on. It's our only native maple, though we also have the related native Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) growing along the river. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)

The flowers are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same raceme - or sometimes bisexual with both stamens and pistils in the same flower. The female (or bi-) flowers have those pretty red structures that will become the characteristic wings of the samara seeds that flutter down like little helicopters when you shake them from the branches in autumn.

Tonight at 4:02 am is the Vernal Equinox, the real start of Spring! The seasons change, life goes on...
Locality:
or bi-) flowers have those pretty red structures that will become the characteristic wings of the samara seeds that flutter down like little helicopters when you shake them from the branches in autumn.

File.Name: d319b_d319-030.jpg
Notes: 8572763375 These are beautiful new urn-like flowers of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. They look like little lanterns! They've been flowering for a while, but at the tops of the trees, out of reach. The flowers are Manzanita-like. They remind me of those fancy easter eggs that can you look into. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)

File.Name: d319c_d319-041.jpg
Notes: 8572724113 Here is a brand new stalk of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family with its first flowers. They're now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family, though I don't understand why. Those darned botanists! Our plants in the Santa Ynez mountains are different because they have green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals, and they are more robust. This one almost looks like a little Corn Lily! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Corn Lily
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: d319d_d319-025.jpg
Notes: 8572691865 These are beautiful new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - with an interesting foliose gall right below the flowers. I believe this is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera - maybe the species Ceanothus Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) or kin? I also find them on Ceanothus spinosus, though not so robust. I wouldn't be surprised if different species of flies are involved. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)
CommonNames: Hairy Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: d319e_d319-019.jpg
Notes:
8573713022 These are insect galls on the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. I guess they are the galls of the Honeysuckle Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia lonicera) of the family Cecidomyiidae in the order Diptera. I don't believe I've ever noticed them before. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)

The shrub is interesting. This is a narrow-leaf version of Lonicera subspicata var. denudata which is pretty common in the interior mountains. Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen in 1831 from the coast, but the inland variety is much more common. But his named collection is official, so his name stands. It's interesting that this species also has salmon-colored berries, while the more common variety has red berries.
CommonNames: galls; Santa Barbara Honeysuckle; Honeysuckle Bud Gall Midge
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Rhopalomyia lonicera; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Lonicera subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Rhopalomyia lonicera; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Lonicera subspicata; denudata

File.Name: d319f_d319-018.jpg
Notes:
8573676438 I believe these are new flowers of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Everything comes in fours with these tiny flowers and shiny leaves on the whorled plant stems. The new flower buds look like little insect eggs - they also come in fours. The flowers are tiny - less than 1/8 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)

Plants in the genus Galium are another kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds.

File.Name: d319g_d318-033.jpg
Notes: 8573659302 This is the whole plant of a little native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family. It's a tough year for wildflowers with just 55% normal rainfall, but they are trying. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d319h_d318-038.jpg
Notes: 8572538481 This is Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a sandstone outcrop. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this. This plant looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places. I've heard they are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d320a_d320-012.jpg
Notes:
8575512663 These are new flower buds of Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. The flowers are not quite open yet, but still interesting. The new pinnate leaves are wonderful. This shrub grows mostly not too far from the river, but there is an odd plant up here in the mountains. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 March 2013)

Our latest "storm" delivered no rain but lots of wind. Bah! It was really too windy for photos today, but I managed to get a few.

File.Name: d320b_d320-006.jpg
Notes:
8576529990 This is a delicate rare plant of Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains. It is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere. It's a beautiful lily-relative, maybe 3 feet tall, like a skinny lily. I heard a report that they are flowering on the north side of Refugio Road, but here there are still just buds. I have a photo of the flowers here from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2013)

I'll "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

File.Name: d320c_d320-001.jpg
Notes:
8576484034 I believe this is a Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found in the woods today - but it doesn't look quite typical. I found the stem already picked along my trail today, and dug around in the soil to find the missing volva - the "death cup" at the base. I reassembled them for this photo. These usually common mushrooms have been missing this year, like the chantrelles. They should have a pure white cap; ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. They are beautiful, but deadly poison - don't eat them! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2013)

It sure looked like someone else pulled this mushroom from the ground, and left the "death cup" behind in the ground. That's dangerous. Never lift a mushroom - always dig it, so that you can find any volva!

File.Name: d320d_d320-009.jpg
Notes: 8575360631 I found these odd little Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushrooms in the woods today. They've been surprising scarce in this dry year, so I was happy to find them. I call these "brain mushrooms" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2013)

File.Name: d320e_d319-036.jpg
Notes:
8575346703 This is a perfect new flower of Brewer's Red Maids (Calandrinia breweri) in the Portulacaceae plant family, growing in a recently cleared place in the mountains. It usually grows as a fire follower, but sometimes clearing the chaparral is enough to trigger these plants. What beautiful colors it has, though you have to get down on your hands and knees to appreciate them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)

It is different from the much more common Calandrinia ciliata, and less common. Compare this photo.

File.Name: d320f_d319-016.jpg
Notes: 8576408088 Here are the first new flowers I've seen this year of (probably) native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There's some confusion about what species our plants really are. The real S. mollis is said to only grow a foot or two tall, and our plants are more like S. albus at 2-4 feet tall. It definitely some kind of Snowberry. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; S. mollis; S. albus

File.Name: d320g_d318-032.jpg
Notes: 8576375314 These are pretty little flowers of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. This plant has little opposite leaves on its flower stalks, but we also have the very similar Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) with little alternate leaves on the stem. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year. It's hard to photograph these small flowers on long stems that sway in the slightest breeze. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d320h_d318-049.jpg
Notes: 8575252655 This is the closest I've seen to a field of wildflowers in this dry rain year. That's Owl's Clover (Castilleja exserta) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - on the left, and Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) in the Fabaceae plant family on the right. Beautiful, but I had to look close to find this grouping. With just half normal rainfall, and last year the same, this is a tough year for wildflowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)
ScientificName: Castilleja exserta; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja exserta; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d321a_d321-003.jpg
Notes:
8579230764 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) picking up pollen from the stamens of a new flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see the pollen-basket hairs on its hind legs that make it a bee. It's small, 1/4 inch or less long. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2013)

This was my only keeper photo today. It was windy, and I've been distracted because Julie and I are taking the train up to Oregon for a couple of weeks, leaving tomorrow morning. Our cabin on the North Umpqua River is off the grid with no email or web access. I'm hoping for new mushrooms and early flowers. I'll be back on April 5.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d321b_d318-002.jpg
Notes: 8578100251 This is a pretty little Spider perched on new foliage and flower buds of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's a Sheetweb Spider in the family Linyphiidae, though it didn't have any web to speak of. It must be in a mating mood, judging from the enlarged palps. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d321c_d319-040.jpg
Notes:
8578083883 Here is the whole plant of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family with its first flowers. They're now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family, though I don't understand why. Those darned botanists!

Our plants in the Santa Ynez mountains are different because they have green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals, and they are more robust. This one is growing with native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. See this closer photo of the same plant. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Hummingbird Sage
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; Salvia spathacea; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d321d_d319-027.jpg
Notes: 8579160792 This is another photo of a foliose "bud gall" with beautiful flowers of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe this is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera - maybe the species Ceanothus Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) or kin? Look at that one blue flower poking through the galled leaves. You an see why I believe this is a bud gall. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2013)
CommonNames: Hairy Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi

File.Name: d321e_d318-043.jpg
Notes: 8579130730 I'm pretty sure these are new flowers and foliage of the Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family. If you compare this photo of (I think) Lupinus nanus, you can see some differences. The leaflets of this one are narrower and longer, the sepals of the flowers are much furrier, and the larger flowers are more spread out along the stalk. On the other hand, the individual flowers are very similar. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d321f_d318-015.jpg
Notes: 8578015285 This is the whole plant with flowers of native Poison Sanicle (Sanicula bipinnata) in the Apiaceaeplant family. The common name sounds scary, but the leaves taste just like cilantro, and I've been munching them for years. The parsley-like foliage is distinctive. We have many different kinds of sanicles in our mountains that all have similar balls of yellow flowers but different foliage. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d321g_d318-021.jpg
Notes: 8579106418 I believe these are new flowers of native Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis) in the Apiaceaeplant family. The leaves are palmate and toothed, but relatively simple. We have a number of different Sanicle plants in the mountains that all have similar balls of yellow flowers but different foliage. This is the one we mostly have at home, where it's just starting to flower. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d321h_d318-004.jpg
Notes: 8577955037 Along with ordinary weeds and weedy grasses, we have weedy shrubs. This is French Broom (Genista monspessulana) in the Fabaceae plant family growing on the side of Highway 154 on San Marcos Pass. The flowers are pretty enough, but this shrub doesn't belong here, and it is spreading into the woods - for example the woods of Kinevan Ranch. It's considered a noxious weed in California, rated CalEPPC List A-1 - the worst. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2013)

File.Name: d406a_d330-003.jpg
Notes:
8626701614 This is a single new flower of the Calypso Orchid aka Fairy Slipper (Calypso bulbosa var. occidentalis) in the in the Orchidaceae plant family. The genus name is from the Greek signifying concealment, as they tend to favor shaded areas on conifer forest floors. They are only a few inches tall, but the color gets your attention. It's wonderful to find these growing untended in the woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 March 2013)

We got back from our Oregon trip last night. I returned with 1,692 photos, and I actually kept 578! I'll be posting them for a few more days. We did get some rain at home in Santa Barbara while we were gone, but it's now very windy, sucking all the moisture from the ground. I'm glad I have these trip photos!

File.Name: d406b_d403-009.jpg
Notes:
8625518089 Here are brilliant new flowers of the Calypso Orchid aka Fairy Slipper (Calypso bulbosa var. occidentalis) in the Orchidaceae plant family, with even more flower buds coming up. The genus name is from the Greek signifying concealment, as they tend to favor sheltered areas on conifer forest floors. They are only a few inches tall, but the color gets your attention. Typically, there is only one flower for a plant. I don't know if this is a colony or exceptional. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 April 2013)

There were none of these when we first got to Oregon two weeks ago, but I started noticing them just before we left. Spring happened while we were visiting!

File.Name: d406c_d402-002.jpg
Notes: 8626603600 This is most of the plant of Heart-leaf Twayblade (Listeria cordata) orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family. The pair of opposite leaves on the stem is distinctive. This plant is tiny - only 3 or 4 inches tall, but I recognized it as an orchid as soon as I noticed it. Note deeply forked lip of the flowers. There were half a dozen plants in this one place near the parking area by our cabin, but they were the only ones I found. See my [Previous] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 April 2013)

File.Name: d406d_d402-005.jpg
Notes:
8626528410 These are tiny flowers of the Heart-leaf Twayblade (Listeria cordata) orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family - with a spider and even-tinier flies (Diptera) that might be pollinators. The whole plant is only 3 or 4 inches tall. Note deeply forked lip of the flowers and the two horn-like structures at the base of the lip. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 April 2013)

I tried and tried to get a sharper photo of the flowers, but this is one of those plants that seems to move even when there is no wind! I guess I needed flash, but I prefer not to use it. I was lucky to notice it near the parking area by our cabin. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: d406e_d326-060.jpg
Notes: 8626492214 This is a new flower of the Western Trillium (Trillium ovatum) in the Liliaceae plant family, with raindrops. They are also known as "Wake Robin" because they flower so early. Everything comes in threes with these flowers - three leaves, three sepals, three petals, three styles, and 3+3=6 stamens. They weren't flowering when we first got to Oregon two weeks ago, but there were many when we left. Spring came while we were there! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 March 2013)

File.Name: d406f_d403-022.jpg
Notes: 8626453144 I'm sure this is some kind of Trout Lily or Fawn Lily (Erythronium) in the Liliaceae plant family getting ready to flower. I got this photo of the last day of our Oregon time, and it still needs a few more days for the flowers to open. Que lastima. The spotted leaves are very beautiful. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 April 2013)

File.Name: d406g_d326-076.jpg
Notes: 8626381996 I believe this is the Snow Queen (Synthyris reniformis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantaginaceae. (Those darn botanists!) The blue or lavender flowers mostly have two stamens. This was the second wildflower I noticed in Oregon when we got to our cabin in the Oregon woods a couple of weeks ago. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 March 2013)

File.Name: d406h_d325-043.jpg
Notes: 8625247559 I guess these are new flowers of the Smooth Yellow Violet (Viola glabella) in the Violaceae plant family. These were the first wildflowers I noticed when we got to our cabin in the Oregon woods a couple of weeks ago. I know the local Santa Barbara wildflowers pretty well, but I'm always guessing on these Oregon flowers. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 March 2013)

File.Name: d407a_d326-059.jpg
Notes:
8629043841 This is a wonderful cauliflower-size Coral Mushroom in the genus Ramaria growing in the Oregon woods. It looks like it belongs in the ocean. There are many species of Ramaria in the woods, but maybe this is Ramaria magnipes which is said to fruit in the spring? I don't really care what it is - it's strange and beautiful! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 March 2013)

*Sigh* Today is another very windy day - much too windy for my kind of photos. This wind event is expected to continue for another day. There is a rumor of 80 mph gusts tonight.

File.Name: d407b_d328-015.jpg
Notes: 8630092426 I believe these are the odd fruiting bodies of the Wrinkled Coral Fungus (Clavulina rugosa). They are growing under conifer trees in the Oregon woods. They are said to be edible, but they're small - you would need a lot of them to make a meal, and why disturb such strange and beautiful creatures? The soft light under cloudy skies was perfect for this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 March 2013)

File.Name: d407c_d324-007.jpg
Notes: 8628916373 I believe these are the small mushroom caps of the Alpine Jelly Cone aka Poor Man's Gumdrops (Guepiniopsis alpinus) growing on a fallen conifer branch in the Oregon woods. The little caps really do feel like gumdrop candy and distinguishes them from Yellow Fairy Cups or Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae. Compare this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 March 2013)
CommonNames: Alpine Jelly Cone; Poor Man's Gumdrops; Yellow Fairy Cups; Lemon Discos

File.Name: d407d_d330-022.jpg
Notes: 8629993602 I believe this is the Golden Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe flavescens aka Hygrophorus flavescens) growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. It's said to be edible but watery and tasteless. What a brilliant mushroom! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 March 2013)

File.Name: d407e_d326-064.jpg
Notes: 8629934552 I found several groups of these rosy-red mushrooms in the Oregon woods, and I'm not sure what they are. They look a bit like the Rosy Gomphidius (Gomphidius subroseus) though the rosy stems without a yellow base seem wrong. Fortunately, I don't have to know all the mushrooms in Oregon! Whatever they are, they are attractive. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 March 2013)

File.Name: d407f_d325-067.jpg
Notes: 8629875008 This is a small harvest of Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum). They look pretty ordinary from above, but the teeth instead of gills or pores are distinctive. We cooked them up with our breakfast eggs - delicious! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 March 2013)

File.Name: d407g_d331-004.jpg
Notes: 8628646987 I found this stately mushroom growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. It was growing on a bank, so I managed to get under it for a photo. I'm not sure, but I think it might be genus Stropharia - maybe the "Questionable Stropharia" (Stropharia ambigua). It has distinctive cottony white veil fragments on the margin of the cap. But this mushroom does look quite different from the Stropharia ambigua I found last fall, and it may well be something else. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 March 2013)

File.Name: d407h_d329-008.jpg
Notes: 8628597429 These are cute little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family of fungi. The nests contain tiny "eggs" that are really spore sacs called peridioles. Look close to see how the eggs have splashed out of the nest by the action of raindrops. There are many different kinds of these fungi, and I won't try to guess which one this is - but maybe genus Cyathus? (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 March 2013)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes

File.Name: d408a_d325-040.jpg
Notes:
8632542883 Here is Grandma Julie and one of our twin grandkids hiking through the old-growth forest on the trail to the Fall Creek waterfall. I reckon from the moss that's a Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family on the right. Beautiful light. (Fall Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 March 2013)

Today's photos are all critters in the Oregon woods - 2, 4, 6, and no legs!

*Sigh* It was very windy again today, but maybe the wind is finally dying down. There are new flowers in the woods, and I haven't taken a single photo since we got home from Oregon on Friday evening. I'm ready!

File.Name: d408b_d327-015.jpg
Notes: 8633597372 This is a herd of large Elk (Cervus canadensis) grazing on a cleared powerline easement in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure if they are Rocky Mountain Elk (C. c. nelsoni) or Roosevelt Elk (C. c. roosevelti) or a cross between the two, but it hardly matters. I find their droppings all over our woods, but this is the first time I've seen the actual beasts. They are large, like horses. Their bearded faces are remarkable. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 March 2013)
CommonNames: Elk; Rocky Mountain Elk; Roosevelt Elk
ScientificName: Cervus canadensis; C. c. nelsoni; C. c. roosevelti
associatedTaxa: Cervus canadensis; C. c. nelsoni; C. c. roosevelti

File.Name: d408c_d331-001.jpg
Notes:
8632461621 Slugs have teeth?

Well, sort of. This giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch leaves in the Oregon woods. They remind me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! This hand-sized slug is spotted. Compare my [Previous] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 March 2013)

File.Name: d408d_d325-063.jpg
Notes: 8633536976 I guess this is another Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods. I was thinking it was something different - a bit smaller, without spots - but it does have the same longitudinal ridge along its back, and I don't know what else it could be. These slugs breathe through the "pneumostome" opening on their right side. Can you see that tiny cup fungus on a twig below it's left eye? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 March 2013)

File.Name: d408e_d328-001.jpg
Notes: 8633435452 Here are a couple of native terrestrial Land Snails (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods. The shell on the left is empty, but the shell on the right is alive. I posed them on this mossy log. When I came back in a few hours, the snail on the right was gone. Matching photos on the web, I think they might be Pacific Sideband (Monadenia fidelis) snails, but I really don't know. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 March 2013)

File.Name: d408f_d330-008.jpg
Notes: 8633353848 These are beautiful flowers of a native dry-land Sedge (genus Carex) in the Cyperaceae plant family. There is also a blurry Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) that flew into my photo during the 5 seconds while I waited for my self-timer to fire the shot! I'm not sure if the fly was visiting the flowers or just passing by. Sedges have interesting flowers with the yellowish stamens above and the white pistils below. I have no idea what kind of sedge these are. It's a difficult group. The key in my California flora runs to 14 large pages! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 March 2013)

File.Name: d408g_d328-006.jpg
Notes: 8632221095 This handsome Fly (order Diptera) is working a new staminate flower of Oso Berry (Oemleria cerasiformis) in the Rosaceae plant family. The shrub is said to grow in Refugio Pass in the Santa Ynez Mountains back at home, though I haven't noticed it yet. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 March 2013)

File.Name: d408h_d325-027.jpg
Notes: 8633283990 I wasn't even trying to photograph insects during our Oregon trip for the last couple of weeks, but who can resist when one sits and perches. I believe this handsome Fly (order Diptera) is a Golden Dung Fly (Scathophaga stercoraria) in the family Scathophagidae. We have the same flies at home - see this photo. Well, I guess their food source is pretty universal! I got this photo hand-held with my normal "kit lens". It's a 1:1 crop. Not bad, considering. (Fall Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 March 2013)

File.Name: d409a_d409-0108.jpg
Notes:
8635779653 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This isn't the best photo, but it's the first Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly I've seen this year. It's resting on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a hairstreak butterfly without tails, though it still rubs its hind wings together like any other hairstreak. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches (including violet) that combine to make an eye-catching brown. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2013)

Hooray, the wind didn't blow all the insects away! The wind let up today. It was still breezy, but there were enough calm moments to get a few photos. I hope for more tomorrow.
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; pointillist

File.Name: d409b_d330-020.jpg
Notes:
8636856062 I believe this is the sexual stage of a Moss (Bryophyta) in the Oregon woods. Instead up growing upward to make a sporophyte, these mosses are making little "splash cups" to spread their little sperm or eggs. They will mate to produce new plants that will make more asexual sporophtes to start the whole process over again. See my [Previous] photo. Life is complicated! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 March 2013)

*Sigh* It's been way too many years since I took a biology class!

File.Name: d409c_d330-015.jpg
Notes:
8636790894 These are beautiful sporophytes of a Moss (Bryophyta) in the Oregon woods. Moss reproduction is complicated, and I'm not sure I understand it. These sporophytes will produce asexual spores that will germinate to produce male and female protonemata, which become gametophores, the plants with a sexual difference that produce swimming sperm and egg, which mate to produce the plants that make more sporophytes. *Whew* It sounds like science fiction!

Anyway - these are beautiful sporophytes! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 March 2013)

File.Name: d409d_d326-050.jpg
Notes:
8635627469 It's interesting how some Oregon trees attract such thick growths of Mosses (Bryophyta). That make sense with deciduous tree like maples and ash because they let the light in, but this is an evergreen Pacific Yew (Taxus brevifolia) tree in the Taxaceae plant family. If you look very close, you can see that it has conifer foliage. This tree is hanging over the wonderful North Umpqua River. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 March 2013)

Maybe it has something to do with the large deciduous Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) trees behind me, towards the sun?
ScientificName: Bryophyta; Taxus brevifolia; Taxaceae; Acer macrophyllum
associatedTaxa: Bryophyta; Taxus brevifolia; Taxaceae; Acer macrophyllum

File.Name: d409e_d325-050.jpg
Notes: 8635599317 This seems to be a different "Dog-Lichen" in the genus Peltigera showing remarkable "air roots". Of course they aren't real roots, because this is a lichen and not a vascular plant. This one is growing on the ground. I've seen this before at home - see this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 March 2013)

File.Name: d409f_d326-070.jpg
Notes:
8636667614 This is an interesting Lichen growing on the ground in the Oregon woods. I believe it is the Membranous Felt Lichen aka Membranous Dog-lichen (Peltigera membranacea). The odd orange painted-toenail-like structures are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. This one is growing on the ground. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 March 2013)

This lichen is especially interesting because it is at least partly a Cyanolichen - a fungus and a "Nostoc" cyanobacteria, a blue-green bacteria or blue-green algae. "Blue-green algae" sound like "algae" but they are very different!
CommonNames: Lichen; Membranous Felt Lichen; Membranous Dog-lichen

File.Name: d409g_d402-024.jpg
Notes: 8636622926 These are a "British Soldiers" type of lichen in the Oregon woods - maybe the Toy Soldiers Lichen (Cladonia bellidiflora) with "squamules on the podetia". I think that means that the stalks are shaggy. I believe the red caps are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - and sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 April 2013)

File.Name: d409h_d328-017.jpg
Notes: 8636587948 This is an odd Lichen in the Oregon woods. Many lichens have apothecia - the fruiting bodies of the fungal part of the lichen, but this one seems to be sprouting real mushrooms! I don't know lichens as well as I should - especially Oregon lichens. If you have any idea what this photo shows, please leave a comment. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 March 2013)

File.Name: d410a_d410-036.jpg
Notes:
8638468485 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!
Butterflies first...

This is a stunning green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly perched on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Just beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

*Sigh* I've been taking many photos in the woods now that the wind has died down. I'm not sure how I'll ever post all my nice Oregon photos, but I'll keep trying. I'm at a disadvantage tonight because TCM is showing on of my very favorite movies as I type - "A Little Romance" (1979). Maybe I like it so much because I'm a retired middle school teacher, and this movie is about smart kids. Recommended.
Locality: 1979).  Maybe I like it so much because I'm a retired middle school teacher

File.Name: d410b_d410-046.jpg
Notes: 8639553830 Here is a mating pair of Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterflies in the non-native annual grasses along my trail today. These butterflies are kind of moth-like, but they are definitely butterflies, knobby antennae and all. They breed in the grass. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

File.Name: d410c_d410-009.jpg
Notes: 8638423133 This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly came to visit the Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the native edge of our garden this morning. It's so perfect that it must be freshly emerged. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

File.Name: d410d_d410-087.jpg
Notes: 8638394159 This is a new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly trying to hide from me among the new leaves of a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it thinks that if its face is in shadow, I can't see it? I've been seeing them for a while, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

File.Name: d410e_d409-039.jpg
Notes:
8638345347 Trip photos are nice, but new photos are always the best!
Butterflies first...

This is a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly with its abdomen hanging over the edge of a leaflet of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Maybe its been ovipositing? You have to hear "silvery blue" the right way. It's not that the butterfly is silvery-blue in color, but that it is a silvery Blue butterfly in the subfamily Polyommatinae. But the butterfly does have some blue color showing on its body. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2013)
