associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Polyommatinae

File.Name: d410f_d402-015.jpg
Notes: 8639418972 This is another Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera, with handsome little orange fruiting bodies of an Ascomycete fungus. I'm not sure if the mushrooms are invaders or "apothecia" - the spore-bearing structures of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. I don't see any signs of stress or decay, so I suspect they are apothecia. If so, then what he heck are those white things? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 April 2013)

File.Name: d410g_d329-011.jpg
Notes: 8639375354 This is a different Pelt Lichen in the genus Peltigera growing on soil in the Oregon woods. I guess it's Peltigera britannica or maybe Peltigera aphtosa. There are many lichens in Oregon! (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 March 2013)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes

File.Name: d410h_d403-003.jpg
Notes: 8638247619 This looks like the ghost of a Lichen! Thanks to Curtis Irish, I believe it is a Ball-headed Coral Lichen (Sphaerophorus tuckermanii). You can see the ball-like apothecia on the ends of the branches - the spore-bearing structures of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. This one was growing on a tree stump with mosses. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 April 2013)

File.Name: d411a_d411-031.jpg
Notes:
8641695364 For the scant few weeks that it flowers, we have no finer plant in our mountains than this native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family! The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. Note the little beetle. They're just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2013)

It used to be called Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - but it's so strange that a species native to southern Europe and the middle east is also a native shrub of Southern California. The name has been put right, but it's still an interesting shrub. This is an example of what has been called the "Mediterranean-American disjunction" - but the paper I used to cite about this has disappeared from the web. The author concluded that: "The only reasonable conclusion is that this genus was once widely distributed across the Northern Hemisphere and became restricted to certain smaller zone." This plant also has a curious local distribution. It's very common on San Marcos Pass in both woodland and chaparral, but where else do you find it in Santa Barbara County?

I'm posting early today, and just these two photos. I have a dinner engagement tonight.
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Styrax officinalis; redivivus
associatedTaxa: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Styrax officinalis; redivivus

File.Name: d411b_d410-015.jpg
Notes:
8640562177 This is the first open flower of the rare Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains. I have another photo from last year which shows the whole plant. It is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere. It's a beautiful lily-relative, maybe 3 feet tall, like a skinny lily. I got this photo in deep shade with a breeze, so I had to bump up the ISO a bit. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

File.Name: d412a_d412-020.jpg
Notes:
8644878239 Here is a fantastic Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly trying to hang on to a flower head of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family. It was breezy today, but there were moments of calm between the gusts. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)

The Flickr uploader was being cranky last night, so I'm finishing the day's photos on Saturday and changing the date.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea

File.Name: d412b_d412-042.jpg
Notes: 8645954844 This is the first new flower that I've seen of native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Liliaceae plant family - with beetles. It looks similar to the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (C. venustus), but lacks the purple spots on the petals. Very beautiful. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)
CommonNames: Catalina Mariposa Lily; Butterfly Mariposa Lily

File.Name: d412c_d412-025.jpg
Notes: 8644592288 This is the first new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family that I've seen this year. It's kind of an upside-down Mariposa Lily. See my [Next] photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)

File.Name: d412d_d412-034.jpg
Notes:
8644552140 This looks like one of the "Find the Animals" gags in the comic books when I was a kid. I can see a Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect in the lower left, a little beetle Coleopera on the right, and lots of little "true bugs" in the order Hemiptera!

The flower cluster is a Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family. They're now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. This one sure looks like var. inezianus with greenish glands, though I found it on the Aliso trail in the backcountry. Maybe that's why it's a variety and not a subspecies? See my [Previous] photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)
CommonNames: Snakefly; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Coleopera; Hemiptera; Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Coleopera; Hemiptera; Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: d412e_d411-014.jpg
Notes:
8643436167 These are brilliant new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family with its first flowers. They're now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family, though I don't understand why. Those darned botanists! Our plants in the Santa Ynez mountains are different because they have green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals, and they are more robust. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2013)

The stamens of these flowers do an interesting little dance as the mature. First they spread out like little carousal horses, but as they mature they tuck in to the center. Compare the new flower just left of center with the one below it. I reckon this is a way to insure self-fertilization if the insects fail their job.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae

File.Name: d412f_d411-009.jpg
Notes: 8643411077 I believe this is the real California Mustard (Thelypodium lasiophyllum aka Guillenia lasiophylla) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills. This is a truly native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. It has long seed-pods that spread out from the stem and then bend down to give it a shaggy look. It's not as common as those weedy mustards. I found this one in a place that was cleared (not burned) a couple of years ago. Sometimes clearing the ground is enough to bring these plants out. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2013)

File.Name: d412g_d411-017.jpg
Notes:
8644474698 This is beautiful flower of Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. The large glandular flowers are about an inch across. The pistil of the flower looks like a tuning fork! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2013)

This is another fire-follower plant that flowers in great profusion for a few years after a wildfire, and then disappears into the seedbank to wait for the next wildfire. I found this one in a place that was cleared (not burned) a couple of years ago. Sometimes clearing the ground is enough to bring these plants out.

File.Name: d412h_d412-060.jpg
Notes: 8643346333 These are remarkable native flowers of a Larkspur - I guess Delphinium patens, but cp. D. parryi - in the Ranunculaceae plant family. Compare this photo. These are growing at the bottom of our driveway. They seem more indigo than blue, the spur points up, and it has broader leaves. Just beautiful, but It's tricky to capture the intense color with a digital camera. This photo comes close, maybe a bit over-exposed. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2013)

File.Name: d413a_d413-011.jpg
Notes:
8647542910 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new flowers of native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 April 2013)

This is not the best photo, but it's fine for a record. We were on the edge of the fog bank at home, with "fog winds" caused by the instability between the marine layer and the dry upper layer of the atmosphere. So I went down to the river and... it was still windy! But I made do.

Too many photos, what a problem. I'm getting new photos every day, and I still have the rest of my Oregon photos to post. What a dilemma!
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d413b_d413-005.jpg
Notes: 8646389389 This is the first grasshopper I've seen this year. I first thought it was a Blue-legged Grasshopper (Lactista gibbosus) - a Band-winged Grasshopper (Oedipodinae) in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera apparently ovipositing (laying eggs) in the clumpy soil. But Aliceinwl1 says it's really a male California Orange-winged Grasshopper (Arphia ramona). I wonder what it's doing in the soil? (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2013)
CommonNames: Blue-legged Grasshopper; Band-winged Grasshopper; California Orange-winged Grasshopper

File.Name: d413c_d413-001.jpg
Notes: 8647439872 This is a little Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Rhagio. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Their abdomens remind me of those collapsible cups I used to carry when backpacking. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2013)

File.Name: d413d_d412-051.jpg
Notes:
8647388394 This is looking up at a mighty Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera. It's perched in the same Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family that I showed yesterday. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)

I actually more interested in the little "cleaner flies" that are attending it. Look close at the bug's neck. I guess these are tiny Freeloader Flies in the family Milichiidae of the order Diptera. The large bug dropped its prey when I got too close, but I reckon the little flies are cleaning up any remains.

File.Name: d413e_d413-029.jpg
Notes: 8646247143 This is a postcard shot. It's mostly native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. There are also a few blue flowers of Delphinium in the background, Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the foreground, and native grasses to the sides. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 April 2013)

File.Name: d413f_d413-006.jpg
Notes: 8646230863 These are brand new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, the first I've seen this year. They are beautiful. They remind me of those frosted curls on fine pastry. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2013)

File.Name: d413g_d413-026.jpg
Notes: These are native flowers of a Larkspur - I guess Delphinium parryi, but cp. D. patens - in the Ranunculaceae plant family. The two species are hard to tell apart. I think Delphinium parryi is a bit bluer and more likely to be found in a meadow like this one. I might be wrong. Either way, they are beautiful and surprising. The flowers are remarkable intricate for being in the relatively primitive Ranunculaceae. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 April 2013)

File.Name: d413h_d413-010.jpg
Notes: 8646160501 These are new flowers of the native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. They only grow near the river. The aromatic leaves of this fine shrub make healing tea, incense, and a smoke that lives up to its name. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 April 2013)

File.Name: d414a_d414-008.jpg
Notes:
8651096590 This is a beautiful little moth (Lepidoptera) - "microlepidoptera" for sure - perched on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It has metallic copper colors in it's wings. I won't try to guess what it is. I know, the plane of my focus isn't quite right, but at least the eyes are sharp. Not bad for a gloomy morning! (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2013)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The critter is about 1200 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.4x1200/4800 = 5.85 mm, about 1/4 inch. (I could have told you that without the calculator!)

It was gloomy and calm this morning - I'll take gloom over wind any day! Then the fog started to lift and we were right on the edge between gloom and sun, with resulting "fog winds".
Locality:

File.Name: d414b_d414-006.jpg
Notes: 8649958931 Here are new flowers of Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. They have little alternate leaves on the flower stalk, unlike the Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) which has opposite leaves. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year. It's a challenge to photograph these small flowers on long stems that sway in the slightest breeze! I wish those were rain drops on the petals, but they are just drops from the fog-drizzle last night. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2013)

File.Name: d414c_d414-013.jpg
Notes: 8649914407 These are new and old flowers of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. The mature flowers have pretty orange stamens. This is another flower where the stamens move into the center as they mature to insure self-pollination. They are not common in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2013)

File.Name: d414d_d412-024.jpg
Notes:
8650964074 I'm pretty sure this is the non-native Bulbous Bluegrass (Poa bulbosa) in the Poaceae plant family. It's a perennial grass that is becoming quite invasive in our mountains. It doesn't just follow the roads like many weeds, but gets into the woods. It's not listed as particularly invasive at Cal-IPC, but I think they should reconsider it - at least in the Santa Barbara region. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)

This is a plant to pull when you see it.

File.Name: d414e_d414-029.jpg
Notes:
8650896984 Is this a Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) or Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom? When I found it, I thought the former - but the striate margins and warts on the cap suggest the latter. Either way, it's quite poisonous. If this is really Amanita pantherina, it's an unusually slender specimen. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2013)

Who needs to go to Oregon to find new mushrooms!
ScientificName: Amanita ocreata; Amanita pantherina; Amanita pantherina
associatedTaxa: Amanita ocreata; Amanita pantherina; Amanita pantherina

File.Name: d414f_d414-036.jpg
Notes:
8649719047 My first thought was that this is a Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found in the woods today - but it doesn't look quite right. It has a thin ring on the stem and a deep volva, but it also has a yellowish cap with some veil fragments on the striate cap. Maybe the volva is really collar-like - adhering to the bulb except for a free rim? That makes it the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina), but I've never seen such a slender specimen. I'm puzzled. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2013)

Either way, it's quite poisonous.

File.Name: d414g_d409-036.jpg
Notes:
8649689491 This is "The Blusher" - the well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom. It develops pink colors on its cap and stem with age and handling. In some years, it's an early mushroom - but it fruits again later in the spring, like now. It doesn't seem to have any visible volva, though it has an unmistakable "amanita aura". (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2013)

I'm sorry this photo isn't quite sharp, but I wanted to post it to compare with my [Next] photo. My tripod is on its last legs, as it were. The friction- felts in the legs are worn out, and I use Pony clamps to hold them tight. Sometimes they wobble. But it's ridiculous how much a new tripod costs!

File.Name: d414h_d414-019.jpg
Notes:
8649641395 These are "Wood Chipper Mushrooms" (my name) growing on wood chips from a trimming project the neighbor did last year. I don't know what they are, but I suspect they come with the wood chipper. The checked caps are interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2013)

These mushrooms have brown spores and a partial veil that leaves an evanescent ring. I have no idea what they are or if they are edible. Maybe they are some Agrocybe other than A. praecox? If you recognize them, please let me know!

File.Name: d415a_d415-013.jpg
Notes:
8653241261 This is a different tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on a new flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see her long ovipositor. They lay their eggs inside the flowers - see my [Previous] photo. I suspect they are responsible for the galled flowers I sometimes find. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)

It was a tough day for new photos - gloomy and breezy all morning, then sunny and downright windy in the afternoon. Nothing else to do except kick up the ISO a notch.

My heart goes out to all who were effected by the Boston Marathon bombing today. I don't know what else to say...

File.Name: d415b_d415-033.jpg
Notes: 8653182309 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) inside a new flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. They oviposit - lay eggs - inside the flowers. I suspect they are responsible for the galled flowers I sometimes find. See this photo. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)

File.Name: d415c_d415-007.jpg
Notes:
8653118427 Here are tiny galls on a fallen catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure whose galls they are, though they are definitely wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. Maybe they are galls of the Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus) or the Kernel Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis serricornis)? (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)

A catkin seems like a silly place to build a home - it is so ephemeral! But I reckon it works for these little wasps.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp; Kernel Flower Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Callirhytis serricornis
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Callirhytis serricornis

File.Name: d415d_d415-024.jpg
Notes: 8653082655 This is looking close at a "leaf roller gall" (my name) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This is clearly a gall with altered leaf structure induced by the critter within, and not just a leaf that's been physically rolled over by an insect. I don't know what insect causes them. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)

File.Name: d415e_d415-027.jpg
Notes: 8653047557 I'm pretty sure the red growths are galls. I call them "leaf roller galls" (my name). They are occurring on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what insect causes them, and they are not listed in my book on "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, 2006). See my [Next] photo for a closer look. They look a little like the aphid galls in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: d415f_d411-034.jpg
Notes:
8654112720 These are strange and beautiful leaf galls on a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. They are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). If you peel one of the galls open, you can see the tiny aphids inside. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2013)

The stumper is why these galls are so darn beautiful!
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: d415g_d410-071.jpg
Notes:
8654090350 These are real "oak apple" galls - the galls of California Gall Wasps (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera) growing on a twig of flowering Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It's about the size of a golf ball. There are critters living in there! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

This is the gall that most people know, though there are many more. Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. I find them fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
CommonNames: California Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: d415h_d410-069.jpg
Notes:
8652938189 These are small galls on new leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure, but they may be the sexual galls of some Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) that will make more distinctive unisexual galls come summer. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013)

Many gall wasps have complicated life cycles - male and female get together and make eggs which produce a unisexual generation that lay eggs to produce a sexual generation of males and females and... the cycle goes on. That means that these wasps make two different kinds of galls. The summer galls are usually more interesting and distinctive.
CommonNames: galls; Scrub Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasps

File.Name: d416a_d416-023.jpg
Notes:
8656000241 These are beautiful Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies with their wings open. They are "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil. We cleaned our water tank last weekend, and had to drain the tank down the road to complete the job. These butterflies (and the previous few) are happy to find moisture in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2013)

*Sigh* It was another very windy day - but not so bad this morning when I got these photos. I've had enough of these wind events!

TCM is showing the wonderful French films of Pierre Etaix tonight. They are new to me. I've been listening, but the TV is around the corner. Almost there!

File.Name: d416b_d416-025.jpg
Notes: 8655980453 This is a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly taking water from the rock - actually a sand clump from the bottom of our water tank. We cleaned our water tank last weekend, and had to drain the tank down the road to complete the job. This butterfly is taking advantage of the moisture. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2013)

File.Name: d416c_d416-019.jpg
Notes: 8657060706 Here are a pair of Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterflies "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil after we drained our water tank last weekend. They are tattered and worn, but that's really their distinguishing feature - no strong makings below. Yet they are Blues - you can see a bit of color on the upper wings of the butterfly on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2013)

File.Name: d416d_d416-021.jpg
Notes: 8655908271 Here is a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly the first I've managed to capture this year. It's "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil. We cleaned our water tank last weekend, and had to drain the tank down the road to complete the job. This butterfly (and the next few) is happy to find moisture in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2013)

File.Name: d416e_d416-016.jpg
Notes: 8656966240 This is a dogpile of (I guess) little rusty-brown adult "Stink Bugs" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera. They are grouping on a native shrub of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The bug on the top-right obligingly stood up and looked right at me. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2013)

File.Name: d416f_d416-004.jpg
Notes: 8655843315 This is a beautiful little wasp (Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. It's quite small, maybe 3/8 inch long without the long antennae. Maybe it's an Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2013)

File.Name: d416g_d415-001.jpg
Notes: 8656924380 This is a large winged Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on the wall of our bathroom, with light from the skylight. I reckon it's a male ant. They collect every night on the toilet and especially in the shower. I guess it's a male, born to die. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)

File.Name: d416h_d415-034.jpg
Notes: 8656909280 I believe this is a large male Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. Sure, the flowers are over-exposed - but the bee looks just about right. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2013)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d417a_d417-017.jpg
Notes:
8658753571 This is a beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly on the finally open flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. There's another Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly right behind the butterfly's right antenna that I didn't notice until I got the photo up on my computer screen. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

These butterflies are never common in our mountains. I find them mostly in the fall, but I have records from June and even January!

I was cold last night - freezing on our upstairs deck, though the tomatoes in the garden pulled through. And it was *sigh* windy again today. I got today's photos in the morning when it wasn't so bad. The wind started to die down this afternoon instead of ramping up. Maybe this is the end of it?
CommonNames: Red Admiral; Sugar Bush; Brown Elfin
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Callophrys augustinus
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Callophrys augustinus

File.Name: d417b_d417-002.jpg
Notes: 8658736807 This white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) looks like it's reaching up to grab the photographer - me! I found it this morning, perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

File.Name: d417c_d417-058.jpg
Notes:
8658695139 This is the "False Puffball" (Reticularia lycoperdon) - NOT the "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" (Fuligo septica) as I once thought until Myxonz set me straight. It is in the same fascinating Myxomycetes or Myxogastria group of Slime Molds. It does look a lot like a white puffball, but its spores don't produce a fungus.

This one is growing on an old stump of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, and growing over an oak leaf. I agree, the white balance is funky, but I got this photo today in a place where the sun never shines. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

By the way, Myxonz has time lapse movies of a slime mold running a maze here and here. Astounding!
CommonNames: False Puffball; Dog Vomit Slime Mold; California Bay
ScientificName: Reticularia lycoperdon; Fuligo septica; Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Reticularia lycoperdon; Fuligo septica; Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: d417d_d417-025.jpg
Notes:
8659746810 This is the first flower on a plant of California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. This plant is about 3 feet tall, but other plants I saw today are only a few inches tall. It doesn't seem to be common. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

The flowers and buds are similar to the Desert Chicory (Rafinesquia neomexicana -see this photo. That's part of the reason I love this area so much - we have thimbleberries and huckleberries with northern affinities, but we also have desert plants like this.
Locality: Rafinesquia neomexicana -see this photo. That's part of the reason I love this area so much - we have thimbleberries and huckleberries with northern affinities

File.Name: d417e_d417-047.jpg
Notes: 8659710588 This is the whole flowering plant of a Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family. The flowers are more yellow than the true California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica), and they lack the collar below the flowers and seed pods that that poppy has. They seem to be rather uncommon in our mountains. I think of these as the real California Poppy, since so many of the others come from seed packets. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

File.Name: d417f_d417-041.jpg
Notes: 8659691172 Here are the whole plants of native Blue Field Gilia aka Globe Gilia (Gilia capitata ssp. abrotanifolia) in the (Polemoniaceae) plant family. See my photo yesterday. These flowers are more common in the backcountry, but here. I found these flowers today along West Camino Cielo a few miles from home. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

File.Name: d417g_d417-050.jpg
Notes: 8658543631 I believe this is a flower head of the Blue Field Gilia aka Globe Gilia (Gilia capitata ssp. abrotanifolia) in the (Polemoniaceae) plant family. I found a number of these today along West Camino Cielo a few miles from home. There's something unnerving about blue stamens on blue flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

File.Name: d417h_d417-013.jpg
Notes:
8658507103 You might have to squint to make out this photo - I have too!

I believe this is a Pacific Woodrush (Luzula comosa) in the Juncaceae plant family. Rushes usually grow in wet places, but this one prefers drier places. These have broad grass-like leaves and flower heads that are different. It's not common in our mountains, at least I rarely notice it. This one in growing on the hillside above our home. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)

File.Name: d418a_d418-009.jpg
Notes:
8661190071 Here are flowers of the rare native Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. It was breezy - nothing else to do except bump up the ISO. This is a beautiful lily-relative, maybe 3 feet tall, with whorled leaves like a skinny lily. Here it is catching a rare moment of sunshine in its north-slope habitat. They are actually having a good year when so many other native flowers are failing. I counted 6 plants flowering on the steep hillside, and many more basal leaves for next year's flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)

This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

It was still windy today, but down a notch from yesterday. I have hopes for tomorrow! Damn, it's been a windy spring.

File.Name: d418b_d418-013.jpg
Notes:
8662253592 This is almost the whole plant of a rare native Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's a beautiful lily-relative, maybe 3 feet tall, with whorled leaves like a skinny lily. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)

This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

File.Name: d418c_d418-015.jpg
Notes: 8661121669 This is the large single leaf of a rare Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains. See my [Next] two photos. I reckon this leaf will provide next year's flowers. It's curious that I didn't notice these large leaves at the base of the current flower stalks. Native orchids also make a large leaf or two that disappear before they flower, see this photo. This leaf is notched by a leaf of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) also in the Liliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)
ScientificName: Fritillaria ojaiensis; Liliaceae; Calochortus albus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Fritillaria ojaiensis; Liliaceae; Calochortus albus; Liliaceae

File.Name: d418d_d418-033.jpg
Notes: 8661103853 I believe this is the fantastic dandelion-like seedhead of native Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The flowers aren't much to look at, but the seed heads are wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)

File.Name: d418e_d418-041.jpg
Notes: 8662175588 I believe these are new flowers of annual Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. These plants are quite large for annuals. The flowers look washed out. Yes, the flower heads look a little like bristly woolly bear caterpillars. Hispid indeed! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)

File.Name: d418f_d418-016.jpg
Notes: 8662164500 The Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family are starting to flower. The yellow flowers in the background are native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's a real shrub with lupine flowers that grows taller than I am. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)
ScientificName: Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae; Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae; Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae

File.Name: d418g_d418-021.jpg
Notes: 8661050803 These are beautiful native flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, we have no finer native flower in our mountains. The flowers of this shrub have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. See this photo for more info. I know, it looks too pretty to be native - but it is. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)

File.Name: d418h_d417-019.jpg
Notes: 8661028473 Here is another photo of the beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly I found yesterday. I managed to chase it away from the flowers of Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family, and it flew over to these flower buds of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's just a temporary hangout. Looking at my notes, I realize I've never managed to photograph them before in the spring, though I have seen them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2013)
CommonNames: Red Admiral; Sugar Bush; Pitcher Sage
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d419a_d419-002.jpg
Notes:
8664885124 We had a mass emergence of Ants (family Formicidae, order Hymenoptera) this morning on our upstairs deck. There's a winged ant flying across in the upper right. This photo doesn't do it justice - 1,000s of ants were thick along 10 feet of our deck. Maybe these ants are the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale)? (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

The damn ants bite and sting! It was a personal sacrifice to get this photo today. I got "butt-nekkid" down on my knees and grabbed a few fast photos while the ants swarmed over me, and then stomped and swiped and took a shower! It happens every spring. At least they are not in the bedroom yet this year.

It was a day for emerging insects - ants in the morning and termites in the afternoon. Well today was the first really warm day without hellacious wind that we've had for a while, so it makes sense.

File.Name: d419b_d419-042.jpg
Notes: 8664850450 We had a Termite (order Isoptera) hatch this afternoon. They were emerging from a sawed-off log with pots on sitting it, a foot from the front porch. The whole emergence was over in a minute or two, so this was a quick shot. Look at the termites squeezing out of the hole in the lower right. I'm curious about that one termite without wings, and I'm glad this isn't the foundation of our house! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

File.Name: d419c_d419-013.jpg
Notes: 8663731011 I found this this harmless and beneficial Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer) catching some rays in a sunny spot in the chaparral. It's head is in the upper-left, hiding under a branch of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Maybe it's trying to hide "Ostrich-style" - if it can't see you then you can't see it? I tried to nudge it into a better pose, but it quickly sped away. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

File.Name: d419d_d419-015+d410-063.jpg
Notes:
8664781256 Signs of a drought year...

The photo on the left shows the wonderful native flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family just a week ago. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2013). The photo on the right shows the same flowers today - not quite the same position, but close enough. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013) And I think of these as summer flowering plants!

I'm sure these are the same plants, because they are actually two stems crossed. The wind has really taken a toll this week, drying out the already-dry woods. We've only had about 50% normal rainfall for the last two years, and it's starting to have effects. Spring wildflowers are disappearing fast. It's an interesting time to be a photographer of nature!

File.Name: d419e_d419-012.jpg
Notes: 8664755650 These are pretty new flowers of Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It was windy today, and I'm surprised I got any photo worth keeping! They are uncommon in our coastal mountains, but they are a common desert plant. I guess I shouldn't be surprised to find them, especially in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

File.Name: d419f_d419-018.jpg
Notes:
8663637715 These are new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the mountains. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. They have been flowering for a while in the mountains. The flowers turn kind of a dingy brown as they age. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

The ripe fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry!

File.Name: d419g_d419-011.jpg
Notes: 8664722612 Here is a new white flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a variety of "Cobweb Thistle" with white flowers, but there are also violet and bright red versions in our mountains. I love the native thistles. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you don't pull these natives by mistake! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

File.Name: d419h_d419-025.jpg
Notes: 8664678136 I believe these are new flowers of Knotted Hedge Parsley (Torilis nodosa) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a new weed for me, similar to it's cousin Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis), but with finer cut leaves. I found this growing along an overgrown dirt road that was used a few years ago to bring "mastication" equipment onto the ridge above Paradise Creek. (Torilis arvensis) is one of the worst invasive weeds in our mountains that gets into the woods. I hope this one doesn't follow it's lead. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)
ScientificName: Torilis nodosa; Apiaceae; Torilis arvensis; Torilis arvensis
associatedTaxa: Torilis nodosa; Apiaceae; Torilis arvensis; Torilis arvensis

File.Name: d420a_d420-021.jpg
Notes:
8667474098 Here is a new Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family down by the creek today. It's not the best photo, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. Catching the butterflies as they come back to the woods is like meeting old friends after many years! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2013)

It was quite warm today, almost 80∞ - but it was still breezy, from the ocean in the morning, and from the valley in the afternoon.

File.Name: d420b_d420-029.jpg
Notes: 8667434566 This is the whole plant of (I believe) native Stinging Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It doesn't really sting like a nettle, but it does have long stiff hairs like one - look close. This is one of our small lupines. They are fairly common in our canyon at home, but they are easy to overlook. I like the shadows of the leaves at the bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2013)

File.Name: d420c_d420-036.jpg
Notes: 8667416170 This is a pretty new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the Rosaceae plant family growing near the creek today. The stamens look like little lollipops and always make me think of the movie Lolita. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2013)

File.Name: d420d_d420-034.jpg
Notes:
8667374430 Here are new flowers of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It really is a beautiful plant, and important for wildlife. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch it if you are sensitive! Compare my [Previous] photo of native Basket Bush (Rhus trilobata). (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2013)

The leaves contain the oil urushiol that causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits! I'm one of the lucky ones who doesn't usually get the rash. It's a good thing - considering how much time I spend in the woods! See my old Treebeard's Stumper (1998).
ScientificName: Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Rhus trilobata; urushiol
associatedTaxa: Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae; Rhus trilobata; urushiol

File.Name: d420e_d412-039.jpg
Notes:
8666237849 Here are new flowers of a native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. This plant was very important to the native Chumash people for several kinds of their fabulous baskets.

This shrub looks a bit like Poison Oak with those 3-part leaves, but the leaves are dull not shiny, the flowers are different, and the middle leaf grows down to the base without a separate stalk. Compare this photo and my [Next] photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2013)
CommonNames: Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Squaw Bush; Poison Oak

File.Name: d420f_d418-039.jpg
Notes: 8667296980 These are new flower stalks of the native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. It does well after wildfires and brush clearings, but it's also doing well in the woods around home. Grasses are hard to photograph, but this one is just fine. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2013)

File.Name: d420g_d409-048.jpg
Notes:
8666128231 This is a tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods perched on a leaf in the woods. I guess it's a female Western Black-Legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), though the legs don't look especialy black. This tick is said to be the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. It has interesting markings. This is a 1:1 crop from my original photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2013)

It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me, though these are the ticks you find on lizards in their early stage. Ticks can spend weeks just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that. If you get ticked, just pull the damn thing out! Use tweezers if you have to. The first sign of a tick bite is a persistent irritation. The bites usually swell up and itch for a day after you remove the tick. No big deal.

I almost forgot about this photo hidden in the recent archives, but it's too good not to post.
Locality: and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me

File.Name: d420h_d420-001.jpg
Notes:
8666063411 This is day two of the mass emergence of Ants (family Formicidae, order Hymenoptera) in our house. Today they were in the bedroom, but we moved the bed away from the window a few years ago just because of them. You can see a couple of "alate" winged ants. This photo shows a few feet of the fascia board around the roof, but the ants are covering the whole wall outside the bedroom, so you can multiply this by a few hundred! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2013)

I believe these are the California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale). They are considered "Odorous Ants (subfamily Dolichoderinae), and they have a strong unpleasant "ant smell" when you crush them. They bite and sting!

Maybe the many ants are providing a safe take-off for the few winged adults? Whenever I get close, they converge and bite me. They have chased the cats off the porch. It works as defense. This happens every spring. In a week, it will be over.
CommonNames: Ants; California Velvety Tree Ant; Odorous Ants

File.Name: d422a-d421_024.jpg
Notes:
8673107733 This is a handsome little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working new flowers of annual Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year, though I've seen a few. It's a bit "over-processed" - my original was under-exposed, and I did what I could. (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

If you look close, there's a little green insect on the right that I didn't notice when I got the photo. I wonder if it's a Timema insect in the family Timematidae of the order Phasmatodea? Or maybe too small?

I apologize for the lapse. Our land-line telephone - and DSL connection - went dead Sunday morning, and it took until Monday afternoon for Verizon to get it working. Living without the Internet is fine for a day, but I wouldn't want to make a habit of it! And now I'm a day behind on photos - I haven't even looked yet at today's photo from Paradise.
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Timema; Timematidae; Phasmatodea
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Timema; Timematidae; Phasmatodea

File.Name: d422b-d421-021.jpg
Notes: 8673037263 Here is a colony of the interesting Wide-throated Yellow Monkeyflower (Mimulus brevipes) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Phrymaceae plant family? (Those darned botanists!) It's quite uncommon in our mountains. Thanks to NatureShutterbug for this location! (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d422c-d421-008.jpg
Notes: 8674095956 This is the "Winecup Clarkia" (Clarkia purpurea ssp. quadrivulnera) in the Onagraceaeplant family. It's also called Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching - too fast! I found this one flowering on a shale slope in the mountains, and the whole plant wasn't much larger than the flower. I also found them on the dry river flats at Paradise today. (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d422d-d421-013.jpg
Notes: 8674072286 This is a small - but rather pretty, if you get down on your hands and knees to see it - flower of native Spanish Clover (Lotus purshianus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I found this one growing on a shale slope, which seems like a particularly harsh environment in this dry year. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d422e-d421_028.jpg
Notes: 8674057422 This is a whole plant of native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family, with seed pods. They have skinny leaflets that are more or less "truncate" - square on the tips, but the well-separated flower stalks are a better clue. I find them now and then in the mountains and but the river in Paradise. I found this plant growing on a crumbly talus slope in the mountains. It's a dry year, and this is an especially dry habitat - but life is tough! (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d422f-d421_035.jpg
Notes: 8672930365 Here is a circle of pretty new flowers of locally uncommon Wide-throated Yellow Monkeyflower (Mimulus brevipes) in the Phrymaceae plant family. This one is growing on the scree of a steep shale bank, but the plant was down low where I could get to it. I only found this one plant. I only know it from this one place in our mountains, but I seem to find it most years. We're at the northern edge of this species, and it's more common further south. Thanks to NatureShutterbug for finding these a few years ago. Now they are an ephemeral memorial, RIP Lynn. plant family - lots of them for a change. I found this plant growing on a crumbly talus slope in the mountains. It's a dry year, and this is an especially dry habitat - but life is tough! (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d422g-d421-031.jpg
Notes: 8673963966 This is the whole plant with flowers of an annual Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. It's only 2 or 3 inches tall, growing on a crumbly talus slope in the mountains. Compare this recent photo of the same plant. It's a dry year, and this is an especially dry habitat - but life is tough! (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)
